9.18: "The needy (ʾebyôn) will not always be forgotten" — the Psalter's vocabulary of poverty is nuanced: ʾebyôn (economically needy), ʿānî (oppressed, afflicted), dallîm (weak, lowly), ʿănāwîm (humble, meek). These categories overlap but carry distinct social implications. Second Temple covenant ethics placed particular responsibility on the community to protect these groups (Deut 15:11; Isa 58:6-7). The promise that hope will not "perish forever" directly counters the wicked's permanent ruin (v. 6).
9.19: "Rise up, O LORD! Let not man have his way; let the nations be judged in your presence!" — the imperative qûmāh (rise up) is a standard battle-cry petition (Num 10:35; Pss 3:7; 7:6; 10:12; 17:13). "Man" (ʾĕnôš — frail, mortal humanity) in contrast to God deflates imperial pretension; the same contrast appears in Isa 31:3 ("The Egyptians are human, not God"). To be judged "in your presence" (ʿal-pānêkā) is to stand before the divine court — exactly what the wicked have denied would happen (Ps 10:4, 11, 13).
9.20: "Strike them with fear, O LORD" (šîtāh yhwh môrāh) — môrāh is the terror induced by divine presence and action; not psychological fear but the overwhelming awe that accompanies theophany. The goal is recognition: "let the nations know they are only human" (ʾĕnôš). Selah closes the psalm (the first half of the 9-10 acrostic) with a liturgical pause, inviting the congregation to absorb the contrast between divine sovereignty and human frailty that the entire poem has established.

10.1: Psalm 10 continues the acrostic of Psalm 9 in the Hebrew Psalter — this is the second panel of the A-to-Z meditation on divine justice. The opening lāmmāh (why) is the classic challenge of the lament genre — a direct rhetorical confrontation with God's apparent absence. "Hide yourself in times of trouble (ʿittôt bĕṣārāh)" uses the idiom of God hiding his face (hissatēr pānîm), which in covenant theology signals withdrawal of favor or presence (Isa 8:17; 54:8; Ps 13:1; 27:9). This psalm is one of the most vivid portraits of social oppression in the Psalter.
10.2: "In his arrogance (bĕgaʾăwat) the wicked person persecutes the poor" — the predatory imagery escalates through the psalm: the wicked is described as a prowling hunter (vv. 8-10) and a crouching lion (v. 9). This social portrait matches realities well-documented in ancient Israel and Second Temple Judaism: wealthy landowners exploiting tenant farmers, creditors seizing collateral in violation of Torah protections (cf. Neh 5:1-5; Amos 8:4-6; Isa 5:8). The retribution petition — "let him be caught in the very schemes he has devised" — is the boomerang theme.
10.3: "The wicked person boasts of the cravings of his heart (taʾăwat napšô)" — self-satisfaction in greed. "Curses and spurns the LORD" — the Hebrew uses the verb bēraḵ (bless) as a euphemism for "curse" (the same softening appears in Job 1:5, 11; 2:5, 9), a scribal device to avoid writing the blaspheming word directly. The contrast throughout the psalm is between the wicked's self-blessing and YHWH's blessing of the poor (Ps 9:18).
10.4: "In all his thinking, there is no place for God" — the classic statement of practical atheism. ʾên ʾĕlōhîm kol-maḥšĕbôtāyw (literally: all his thoughts — "no God"). This is not the philosophical atheism of the Enlightenment but the functional atheism of one who acts as if God does not govern human affairs. Second Temple wisdom literature (Ben Sira, Wisdom of Solomon) treats this as the root of all wickedness: the failure to account for divine judgment in one's practical decisions.
10.5: "His ways always succeed" — the theological scandal at the heart of this lament: the wicked prosper. This is the pressing question of Pss 37, 49, 73, and the book of Job. "Your judgments are too high for him to perceive" — not that divine judgments are intellectually incomprehensible, but that the wicked's diminished moral vision cannot see them. The wicked "sneers at all his enemies" — success breeds contempt for any opposition. The Psalter does not deny this experience; it holds the tension honestly.
10.6: "He says in his heart, 'I will never be shaken (bal-ʾemmoṭ); through all generations I will face no trouble.'" The heart-speech of the wicked (vv. 6, 11, 13) is a rhetorical device to expose the interior logic of oppression. The phrase bal-ʾemmoṭ ("will not be shaken") deliberately inverts the security language the Psalter applies to the righteous (Ps 15:5; 16:8; 21:7; 62:2). The wicked has appropriated covenant-stability language for his own arrogance — a theological usurpation the psalmist exposes.
10.7: "His mouth is full of cursing, deceit, and fraud; under his tongue are trouble and wickedness" — the anatomy of wicked speech. In ANE thought, curse formulas carried real causal power; to be under a curse was a genuine social-spiritual threat. The under-the-tongue image is forensic: the tongue conceals what the mouth will eventually release. "Trouble (ʿāmāl) and wickedness (ʾāwen)" — ʾāwen is a key word in the Psalter's moral vocabulary, combining meanings of wickedness, idolatry, and emptiness.
10.8: "He lurks in ambush near the villages" — the rural social setting: isolated farmsteads and small communities were vulnerable to strongmen who operated outside the protection of urban courts. The "innocent" (nāqî) he kills are those without legal standing. Vv. 8-10 use predator-prey imagery (lion, net, snare) that was standard in Egyptian and Mesopotamian wisdom texts for describing the oppressor. The psalmist maps ANE hunting vocabulary onto social exploitation — making the oppressor's predatory nature visible.
10.9: "He crouches like a lion in his lair; he lies in wait to catch the poor" — the lion as predator is one of the Psalter's persistent metaphors (Pss 7:2; 17:12; 22:13; 57:4). In Israelite pastoral culture, lions preying on livestock were a real threat (1 Sam 17:34-37; Amos 3:12); the metaphor carries immediate visceral force for an agricultural audience. The poor (ʿānî) are structurally vulnerable: without land, family network, or access to courts — those who cannot defend themselves.
10.10: "He crouches and springs, and the helpless fall by his strength" — dakkîm (the crushed/helpless) echoes the dak of Ps 9:9. The abruptness of the verse (only 3 words in Hebrew: "he crouches, he springs, the weak fall") mimics the sudden violence of the predatory act. In the ancient economy, "falling by strength" could mean debt-bondage, seizure of property, or physical coercion — all forms of power available to the strong against those without social protection.
10.11: "God has forgotten; he has hidden his face; he will never notice" — the wicked's three-part denial mirrors vv. 4 and 6: a consistent alternative theology. "Hiding the face" (hissatîr pānîm) was a covenant idiom for divine withdrawal that the wicked here declares permanent. The theological scandal of divine silence in the face of oppression was not only a personal spiritual crisis but a communal challenge to the entire covenant framework in which YHWH protects the vulnerable.
10.12: "Rise up, O LORD! O God, lift up your hand! Do not forget the afflicted!" — the imperative petitions (qûmāh, nĕśāʾ yādĕkā) directly counter the wicked's claim that God has forgotten. "Lift up your hand" is the gesture of power and striking — the raised hand prepares to act (cf. Exod 7:5; Deut 32:40). This petition echoes Ps 9:19 and reflects the liturgical context: the congregation physically enacting the prayer, perhaps with raised hands, prostration, or crying out.
10.13: "Why does the wicked person despise God and say in his heart, 'You won't call me to account'?" — nāʾaṣ (despise, treat with contempt) is a strong term for active repudiation. The wicked's inner speech "You won't call me to account" (lōʾ tidrōš) uses the verb dāraš (seek/require/demand): God will neither inquire into his actions nor demand accountability. This inverts the covenant: in which YHWH does seek and does require (Deut 18:19; Amos 5:4; Isa 55:6).
10.14: "But you do see! You observe trouble and grief and take it into your hands" — the rhetorical pivot. Against the wicked's assumption of divine indifference, the psalmist asserts divine attentiveness: God sees, observes, and intervenes. "The helpless person (yātôm — the fatherless/orphan) entrusts himself to you" — the orphan was among the most legally vulnerable in ancient Israelite society. To be fatherless was to lose one's gōʾēl (kinsman-redeemer), one's advocate before courts, and one's social standing.
10.15: "Break the arm of the wicked and the evildoer" — "arm" (zĕrôaʿ) is the standard symbol of power and strength (cf. Ezek 30:21-22, where God explicitly breaks Pharaoh's arm). To break the arm is to permanently incapacitate the means of oppression. "Call his wickedness to account until there is none left" — the demand is not merely personal punishment but the exhaustion of the capacity for wickedness: complete moral accountability leading to complete moral incapacitation.
10.16: "The LORD is King for ever and ever; the nations will vanish from his land" — the divine kingship proclamation (yhwh melek ʿôlām wāʿed) closes the lament with the enthronement affirmation. "The nations will vanish from his land (ʾarṣô)" — the land belongs to YHWH as its true owner; those who occupy it without acknowledging him are transient tenants. In Second Temple theology this pointed both to Eretz Israel and, eschatologically, to the whole earth over which YHWH would rule.
10.17: "LORD, you have heard the desire (taʾăwat) of the humble" — this word (taʾăwāh) was used in v. 3 for the wicked's cravings. Now it reappears for the humble's longing — a deliberate contrast. The wicked's desires lead to exploitation; the humble's desire is for vindication and survival. "You will strengthen their heart" — in Hebrew thought the heart (lēb) is the seat of will, intention, and courage; to strengthen the heart is to resolve and empower the will for perseverance.
10.18: "To vindicate the fatherless and the oppressed, so that mortal man will no longer strike terror" — the psalm closes where it opened: the question of human power over the vulnerable. The final petition is cosmic in scope: that the whole pattern of oppression over the defenseless would end. "Mortal man" (ʾĕnôš min-hāʾāreṣ — man from the earth) reprises Ps 9:20's deflation of human arrogance. The Pss 9-10 acrostic closes with this eschatological hope: the human terror-imposer brought to nothing before the eternal King.

11.1: Psalm 11 is a psalm of trust in the face of counsel to flee. David's counselors or friends advise escape to the mountains — presumably because the danger is too acute to face. The "mountain" (hārekem) is probably the Judean highlands (not Zion), a refuge for fugitives in the David narratives (1 Sam 23-26). David's refusal — "In the LORD I take refuge" — is the psalm's theological claim: the divine protector supersedes any geographic strategy. The tension between the counselors' pragmatic wisdom and the psalmist's covenantal trust is the pastoral core of the poem.
11.2: "The wicked string the bow; they have set their arrows on the string to shoot in the darkness (ʾōpel)" — the nocturnal archer was a real threat in the ancient world, but the image also captures hidden malice: the attack comes without warning, in darkness, targeting "the upright in heart (yiśrê-lēb)." Upright-hearted people are the ones who cannot be corrupted by the counselors' pragmatic flight advice — their very integrity makes them targets. ANE military contexts regularly described ambush arrows as the weapon of treachery.
11.3: "If the foundations are destroyed, what can the righteous do?" — the counselors' logic: if the basic structures of justice and social order have collapsed, individual righteousness has no purchase; flight is the only rational option. Šātôt (foundations) can mean the foundations of society, the covenant order, or the temple mount. Second Temple readers in the aftermath of the Babylonian destruction of 587 BCE would have heard this as directly relevant — what does covenant faithfulness mean when every institutional support of that covenant has been razed?
11.4: "The LORD is in his holy temple; the LORD's throne is in heaven" — the counter-claim to despair. Two levels: the earthly temple (where YHWH is present) and the heavenly throne (from which he governs). Both are secure and neither depends on the survival of human institutions. "His eyes observe; his eyelids examine (yiḥĕzû, yiblĕnû) humanity" — the examining gaze is forensic. Bāḥan (examine/test) is the metallurgist's assaying verb: God is scrutinizing humanity as a refiner tests metal. He is neither absent nor indifferent.
11.5: "The LORD tests (bōḥēn) the righteous" — the divine testing of the righteous is a covenant theme: God tests to refine (Deut 8:2-3; Job 1-2; Ps 66:10; Zech 13:9). "His soul hates the wicked and the one who loves violence (ḥāmās)" — ḥāmās (violence, cruelty) is one of the most weighty moral terms in the Hebrew Bible. It is listed alongside corruption as the reason for the flood (Gen 6:11-13) and is the defining characteristic of the wicked throughout the Psalter. God's hatred of ḥāmās is not emotional pique but the covenantal rejection of the anti-creation force.
11.6: "Let him rain down coals on the wicked; fire and sulfur and a scorching wind (rûaḥ zilʿāpôt)" — Sodom imagery (Gen 19:24) is deliberate: fire and sulfur raining from heaven is the paradigmatic divine judgment on the utterly wicked city. The scorching wind is likely the sirocco (east wind from the Arabian desert), which withers vegetation and exhausts humans. Fire from above, sulfur from below, scorching wind from the east — judgment from all directions simultaneously. "Their cup (mĕnāt kôsām)" — cup of wrath contrasts sharply with the cup of blessing in Ps 16:5.
11.7: "For the LORD is righteous (ṣaddîq); he loves righteous deeds (ṣĕdāqôt); the upright will see his face (yĕḥĕzû pānāyw)" — the closing affirmation. To "see God's face" was the goal of temple worship (Ps 17:15; 27:4; 63:2) — the culmination of access to the divine presence normally mediated through the sanctuary. The upright's access to the divine presence is grounded in the correspondence between their character and YHWH's own character: as he is righteous, the righteous see him. The psalm reverses the flight-counsel of v. 1: those who trust rather than flee are the ones who arrive at the divine presence.

12.1: Psalm 12's superscription mentions the "Sheminith" (šĕmînît) — possibly an eight-stringed instrument, a musical register (eighth = bass), or a liturgical category. The same direction appears in Ps 6. The psalm is a lament over total collapse of faithful speech in society. The hyperbole "the faithful have vanished from among humanity" is characteristic of the lament genre's extreme formulation (cf. Elijah's "I alone am left," 1 Kgs 19:10) — not a census claim but a rhetorical expression of moral isolation. The crisis of trustworthy speech was perennial in ancient courts and communities.
12.2: "Everyone lies to his neighbor; they speak with flattering lips and a divided heart (lēb wālēb)" — lēb wālēb (heart and heart = divided heart) is the idiom for duplicity: one public heart, one hidden heart. To have two hearts violated the fundamental social bond of covenant community. In ancient Israelite ethics, integrity meant the alignment of speech and heart (cf. Ps 15:2 — "speaks truth from the heart"). Šāwĕʾ (falsehood, emptiness) is paired with smooth speech — the combination of empty words and manipulative delivery.
12.3: "May the LORD cut off all flattering lips" — the petition against siptê ḥălāqôt (smooth/flattering lips) and lāšôn mĕdabberet gĕdōlôt (tongue that speaks great things, boastfully). The contrast throughout the psalm is between two kinds of speech: human speech (manipulative, boastful, self-serving) and divine speech (pure, reliable — v. 6). In ANE thought, words carried power — false words damaged the social fabric by corrupting the medium through which the community sustained trust and covenant loyalty.
12.4: "With our words we will prevail; our lips are our own — who can lord it over us?" — the claim of autonomous speech: no accountability to God or community. In ancient culture, one's word was one's bond, and to renege on it was to damage one's social honor (kābôd). The arrogance here is cosmological: to claim autonomy of speech is to deny that language is given by and accountable to the Creator. "Who can lord it over us?" denies any transcendent authority — a posture the psalm regards as both morally and theologically self-deceptive.
12.5: "'Because the poor are oppressed and the needy groan, I will now arise,' says the LORD" — a direct divine oracle (nĕʾum yhwh) embedded within the lament, a technique more common in prophetic literature than the Psalms (cf. Isa 33:10 — "now I will arise"). The direct divine response to the poor's cry mirrors the Exodus pattern: God hears the groaning (nĕʾāqāh) of the oppressed and acts (Exod 2:24; 6:5). "I will set them in the safety for which they long (yāpîaḥ lô)" — the safety is precisely the covenantal protection the corrupt community has denied them.
12.6: "The words of the LORD are pure words, like silver refined in a furnace in the ground, purified seven times" — the great contrast. Human speech is corrupt (vv. 2-4); divine speech is maximally pure. The metallurgical image is precise: silver refined in a clay furnace (ʿălîl lāʾāreṣ), heated repeatedly to remove all dross. Seven is the number of completeness — sevenfold refining = absolute purity. This is the standard by which all other speech is measured. Second Temple Judaism applied this to Torah: God's word is perfectly reliable (cf. Ps 19:7-10).
12.7: "You, O LORD, will keep them; you will protect us from this generation (haddôr hazzeh) forever" — the pronouns shift ambiguously (them/us) but the meaning is clear: God preserves both his words and those who trust in them against the corrupt generation. "This generation" (haddôr hazzeh) is a recurring moral category in Scripture — not a biological cohort but a spiritually defined community characterized by faithlessness (cf. Deut 1:35; Ps 78:8; Matt 12:39). The righteous are embedded in history alongside the corrupt and require divine protection.
12.8: "The wicked parade on every side when what is vile is honored among humanity" — the closing observation rather than resolution. When the honor-system is corrupted — when the dishonorable gain honor — the wicked multiply unchecked. Honor and shame were the primary social regulators in ancient Mediterranean culture; when the system inverts (vileness honored rather than shamed), the mechanisms of social accountability collapse entirely. The psalm ends with this ongoing reality, having placed its hope entirely in the divine speech of vv. 5-7 rather than in social reform.

13.1: Psalm 13 is the classic short lament — six verses moving from abandonment (vv. 1-2) through petition (vv. 3-4) to trust and praise (vv. 5-6). The fourfold "ʿad-ʾānāh" (how long) is one of the most repeated formulas of the lament genre (cf. Pss 74:10; 79:5; 89:46; Hab 1:2; Jer 12:4). It does not expect an answer; it is rhetorical pressure applied to God. "Will you forget me forever (lĕneṣaḥ)?" — neṣaḥ means both "forever" and "completely/permanently," compounding the feeling of absolute divine absence.
13.2: "How long must I wrestle with my thoughts (ʾāśît ʿēṣôt bĕnapšî — setting plans/counsels in my soul)?" — the psalmist is not passively suffering but is exhausting himself in deliberation, turning over options, none of them working. "My enemy (ʾōybî) triumphs over me" is the social dimension: the internal suffering has external visibility. In the honor-shame culture of the ancient world, the enemy watches and interprets the psalmist's distress as evidence that God has abandoned him — the individual's suffering is a public theological statement about YHWH's reliability.
13.3: "Give light to my eyes" (hāʾîrāh ʿênay) — the standard petition for renewed vitality. In Hebrew medical thought, dimming eyes signal mortal weakness (1 Sam 14:27, 29 — Jonathan's eyes "lit up" when he ate honey; 1 Sam 3:2 — Eli's eyes had grown dim). "Lest I fall into the sleep of death" (pen-ʾîšan hammāwet) — to die was to fall asleep into Sheol (Dan 12:2; Jer 51:39). The sleep of death is not peaceful rest but permanent cessation; the petition is for the light of renewed life before the threshold is crossed.
13.4: "Lest my enemy say, 'I have overcome him,'" — the social stakes of the individual's survival. In an honor-shame culture, the psalmist's death would be read publicly as YHWH's defeat; the enemy would gloat (yāgîl). This is not vanity but covenantal argument: God has a stake in not being publicly shamed by the victory of those who oppose his covenant servant. The psalmist's appeal to God's honor as part of his petition is bold but conventional in the lament genre — "act for your name's sake" (cf. Ps 79:9; 109:21).
13.5: "But I have trusted in your steadfast love (ḥeseḏ)" — the pivot from lament to trust. Ḥeseḏ is covenantal loyalty: God's faithful adherence to his covenant commitments, not transient emotion. The psalmist's trust is not in a feeling but in a covenant attribute. "My heart will rejoice in your salvation (yĕšûʿātĕkā)" — the prospective perfect: the psalmist speaks of not-yet-experienced salvation as already certain because God's ḥeseḏ guarantees it. This grammar of faith (speaking future salvation as present reality) is the Psalter's characteristic mode of trust.
13.6: "I will sing to the LORD, for he has been generous (gāmal ʿālay) to me" — the sudden resolution. No explanation is given for the transition from distress (v. 4) to praise (v. 6): the psalmist simply arrives there. This unnarrated pivot is characteristic of the individual lament form. Scholars have proposed various explanations: an intervening priestly oracle now absent from the text, the act of prayer itself as transformative, or the conscious choice of trust as an act of will. In any case, the worshipping community would recognize this pattern from repeated liturgical experience of moving through lament into praise.

14.1: Psalm 14 is nearly identical to Psalm 53, with systematic substitution of YHWH for Elohim — the same poem appears twice in the Psalter in two different theological collections (Pss 3-41 use YHWH; Pss 42-83 prefer Elohim). The nābāl (fool) who says "there is no God" is not a philosophical atheist but a moral-practical one — nābāl derives from a root meaning "to wither/fade" (the same root as Nabal in 1 Sam 25, whose name his wife acknowledges means "fool"). The denial is not of God's existence but of his moral governance: the practical posture that divine accountability does not extend to one's actions.
14.2: "The LORD looks down from heaven on all people to see if there are any who understand (maśkîl), who seek God (dōrēš ʾĕlōhîm)" — the divine survey from heaven parallels Gen 11:5 (Tower of Babel — "the LORD came down to see") and Gen 18:21 (Sodom). God's "looking down" is a forensic examination. Maśkîl (one who understands/acts with wisdom) and dōrēš ʾĕlōhîm (seeking God) are parallel: to seek God is the definition of wisdom. This reverses the common modern assumption that intellectual sophistication dismisses religious inquiry.
14.3: "All have turned aside; all have become corrupt together; there is no one who does good, not even one" — the universal indictment. Paul quotes this verse along with Pss 5:9; 10:7; 36:1; 140:3; Isa 59:7-8; and Prov 1:16 in Romans 3:10-18 to establish universal human sinfulness as the theological basis for universal need of the gospel. In the psalm's original context, "all" likely refers to the generation of the godless described in vv. 1-4 — but Paul draws out the text's full anthropological scope, reading the Hebrew totality language as a genuine universal claim.
14.4: "Those who devour my people as they eat bread" — the devouring of "my people" (ʿammî) is striking language: the wicked treat Israel's vulnerable community as food, consumed casually and without hesitation. "And who do not call on the LORD" — in ancient Israel, meals were covenant occasions as well as sustenance; to eat without calling on YHWH was to deny the covenant context of all provision. The "workers of evil" (pōʿălê ʾāwen) are defined by their total mode of action — ʾāwen combining wickedness, idolatry, and futility.
14.5: "There they were filled with fear, for God is with the righteous generation (dôr ṣaddîq)" — "there" (šām) is the place where the wicked enact their oppression. The sudden terror overtakes them when they encounter God's presence with the righteous community — the divine solidarity the wicked's theology had denied. "The righteous generation" is the covenant community defined not by birth but by moral-covenantal alignment: those who seek God (v. 2). Their presence (or God's with them) constitutes the shock that breaks the wicked's assumed impunity.
14.6: "You would shame the plans of the poor (bôšîšû ʿăṣat-ʿānî)" — the psalmist addresses the wicked directly. Their strategy of shaming the poor — stripping them of social honor and undermining their strategies — fails because YHWH himself is the poor's maḥseh (shelter, refuge). The honor-shame mechanism the wicked deploys against the poor is overridden by divine advocacy: the ultimate patron of the patron-less is YHWH, whose honor-claim on behalf of the poor cannot be overridden by any human court or social power.
14.7: "Oh, that salvation for Israel would come from Zion!" — the eschatological longing. Zion as the source of salvation draws on the Zion theology in which YHWH's throne on Zion is the origin of divine action for his people. "When the LORD restores the fortunes of his people (bĕšûb yhwh šĕbût ʿammô)" — the idiom šûb šĕbût (restore fortunes, literally "turn the captivity") was associated in Second Temple Judaism with the return from Exile and, eschatologically, with the restoration of all things. The psalm ends facing the future, hoping for what is not yet fully present.

15.1: Psalm 15 is an entrance liturgy — a question-and-answer ritual performed at the gates of the sanctuary before entry. Psalm 24 has the same structure ("Who may ascend the hill of the LORD?"), and Isaiah 33:14-16 and Micah 6:6-8 show the same genre operating in prophetic contexts. Pilgrims approaching the sanctuary would ask "Who may dwell in your sanctuary?" and a priest or the congregation would answer with the ethical requirements. The two verbs — gûr (sojourn, temporary dwelling) and šākan (dwell, permanent residence) — together span both temporary access and full covenant belonging to the holy mountain.
15.2: "The one who walks blamelessly (tāmîm), does what is right (ṣedeq), and speaks truth from the heart" — three positive qualifications. Tāmîm (blameless, integrity of wholeness) is the term used of Noah (Gen 6:9) and Job (Job 1:1) — not sinless perfection but undivided moral integrity; the opposite of the divided heart in Ps 12:2. "Does what is right" is covenant-justice in practice. "Speaks truth from the heart" — heart and mouth aligned — is the antithesis of the duplicity condemned in Ps 12 and the flattery condemned throughout the Psalter.
15.3: "Who does not slander with the tongue (rāḡal ʿal-lĕšônô)" — literally "foot it on his tongue," the idiom for one who goes about from house to house bearing tales (cf. Lev 19:16 — "do not go about spreading slander"). Social harm (rāʿāh) to a neighbor and reproach against a friend cover the range of speech that damages reputation. In ancient Israel, one's standing in the community was inseparable from one's social life — to damage reputation was to harm the person at the most fundamental level of their social existence.
15.4: "Who despises the contemptible but honors those who fear the LORD; who keeps an oath even when it costs (nišbaʿ lĕhāraʿ wĕlōʾ yāmîr)" — to honor YHWH-fearers while despising the dishonorable inverts normal social dynamics where power and wealth determine honor. "Swears to his own hurt and does not change" — in ancient commercial and social life, oaths were legally and spiritually binding; to renege on a sworn commitment was both social treachery and covenant violation. The one who keeps costly oaths demonstrates that their word is genuinely reliable, not self-serving.
15.5: "Who does not lend money at interest (nešek) or take a bribe (šōḥad) against the innocent" — the Torah prohibited lending at interest to fellow Israelites (Exod 22:25; Lev 25:35-37; Deut 23:19-20) though permitted it with foreigners. Interest-bearing loans were a primary mechanism of predatory wealth accumulation in the ancient economy: the small farmer who borrowed after a crop failure could lose his land within a season (cf. Neh 5:1-5). The bribe against the innocent was the standard form of judicial corruption that perverted the court system for the wealthy (Exod 23:8; Deut 16:19; Amos 5:12). "Whoever does these things will never be moved (lōʾ yimmôṭ lĕʿôlām)" — covenant stability.

16.1: Psalm 16 carries the superscription miktam — an uncertain genre term appearing in Pss 16 and 56-60. Proposed meanings include a "golden poem" (kātam = gold) or a penitential form, but no consensus exists. The psalm is centrally important for early Christian theology: Peter cites vv. 8-11 at Pentecost (Acts 2:25-28) and Paul cites v. 10 at Pisidian Antioch (Acts 13:35), both applying "your Holy One will not see decay" to Jesus's resurrection. The opening "keep me safe (šāmrēnî) for in you I take shelter (ḥāsîtî)" establishes the covenantal posture of complete dependence — ḥāsāh (take refuge) is the root of maḥseh (refuge), the Psalter's standard term for divine protection.
16.2: "I said to the LORD, 'You are my Lord (ʾādonāy); I have no good apart from you.'" — the radical covenant confession: God is the source of all good, not only religious or spiritual good. ʾĀdonāy (my Lord) is the respectful vocative form used for YHWH. "My good is nothing without you (ṭôbātî bal-ʿāleykā)" is textually and grammatically debated (the LXX reads it differently), but in context expresses absolute dependence: all benefit flows from the relationship with YHWH; outside it there is no ṭôb (good, flourishing).
16.3: "As for the holy ones who are in the land, they are the noble — in them is all my delight" — this verse is textually difficult. The most natural reading: the qĕdôšîm (holy ones) are the covenant faithful in Israel, the pious community. Their nobility (ʾaddîrê) is not social rank but spiritual standing. The psalmist's delight (ḥēpeṣ) in the community of the faithful mirrors YHWH's own delight in the righteous (Ps 147:11) — the psalmist's values align with God's, forming a coherent pattern of covenant loyalty at both the vertical and horizontal dimensions.
16.4: "The sorrows of those who chase other gods will multiply" — the covenant warning of Deut 28-30: apostasy produces suffering. ʿAṣbôtām (sorrows, idols — the same root as ʿōṣeḇ, idol) may be a deliberate wordplay: both their idols and their sorrows will multiply. "I will not pour out their libations of blood" — blood libations were Canaanite or foreign ritual practices; Israelite law strictly prohibited the drinking of blood (Lev 17:10-14) and blood-ritual worship. The psalmist refuses cultic assimilation to surrounding practices, maintaining covenant distinctiveness.
16.5: "The LORD is my allotted portion (mĕnāt-ḥelqî) and my cup (kôsî)" — two layered images. (1) Ḥēleq (portion, allotment): the Levites received no tribal land inheritance because YHWH was their portion (Num 18:20; Deut 10:9; 18:2) — to be sustained by the altar, not the earth. The psalmist claims the same priestly relationship: not land but YHWH himself is the inheritance. (2) The cup: in covenant ritual, the cup of blessing was passed at sacred meals (cf. 1 Cor 10:16); the cup could also contain wrath (Ps 11:6; 75:8; Isa 51:17). Here the cup is blessing.
16.6: "The boundary lines have fallen for me in pleasant places; truly I have a beautiful inheritance" — land allotment language. When Joshua divided Canaan by lot, each tribe received its ḥēleq (inheritance), the boundary lines (ḥăbālîm) marking the permanent grant (Josh 18-19). The psalmist applies this to his relationship with God: the "inheritance" of divine fellowship is more desirable than any parcel of land. The contentment expressed — "pleasant places (nĕʿîmôt)," "beautiful inheritance (šāpĕrāh ʿālay)" — echoes the šālôm of Ps 4:7-8: satisfaction from the divine presence itself rather than material provision.
16.7: "I will praise the LORD who counsels me (yĕʿāṣênî); even at night my inmost self (kĕlāyôtay — kidneys) instructs me" — divine counsel received in the night connects Ps 16 with Ps 17 (also a night vigil). The kidneys (kĕlāyôt) were the seat of deep feeling and moral discernment in ancient Israelite anthropology (cf. Jer 11:20; 17:10; 20:12 — where God examines the kidneys and heart), analogous to the modern conscience or deepest intuition. Divine instruction reaches the deepest level of the person, not merely the conscious surface.
16.8: "I have set the LORD always before me; because he is at my right hand (liymînî), I will not be shaken" — the right hand is the position of the chief advocate, counselor, or protector (cf. Ps 110:1 — God says to the Davidic lord: "sit at my right hand"). The king's military commander stood at his right hand. Peter at Pentecost (Acts 2:25) quotes this verse and the following as David speaking prophetically of Christ — the one who is now literally exalted to God's right hand (Acts 2:33). The covenant-stability phrase "will not be shaken" recurs across Pss 10-21.
16.9: "My heart is glad and my spirit (kābôd, lit. glory/honor-self) rejoices; my body (bāśār, flesh) also rests secure" — the triad of heart (lēb), glory-self (kābôd), and body (bāśār) expresses total well-being. Using kābôd for the inner self is unusual — possibly the psalmist's social-spiritual honor-identity. The LXX translates kābôd as glōssa (tongue), a significant deviation that shapes Peter's quotation in Acts 2:26 ("therefore my heart was glad, and my tongue rejoiced"). The Hebrew triad and the LXX triad each emphasize totality of wellbeing.
16.10: "For you will not leave my soul (nafšî) in Sheol, or let your Holy One (ḥăsîdĕkā) see decay (šaḥat)" — the center of the NT's reading of Ps 16. Peter (Acts 2:27-31) and Paul (Acts 13:35-37) argue that David cannot be speaking only of himself, since David did die and his tomb was known in Jerusalem; therefore, David spoke prophetically of Christ's resurrection as the fulfillment of the non-decaying Holy One. Šaḥat (pit/decay) — whether it means the pit of Sheol or physical decomposition carries significant interpretive weight. The psalm originally expresses personal confidence in divine preservation from death; the NT reads the promise as pointing beyond any Davidic fulfillment.
16.11: "You will make known to me the path of life (ʾōraḥ ḥayyîm); in your presence is complete joy (śimbat pānîm); at your right hand are pleasures forevermore (nĕʿimôt niṣṣaḥ)" — Ōraḥ ḥayyîm (path of life) is wisdom language: the way that leads away from death (Prov 2:19; 5:6; 10:17). The complete joy in God's presence contrasts with the multiplied sorrows of those who chase other gods (v. 4). "Pleasures forevermore" is an eschatological horizon — joy in God's presence has no terminus. The psalm closes not at Sheol but at the right hand of God, where the fullness of joy and eternal pleasures reside.

17.1: Psalm 17 is a royal prayer — the king petitions for divine vindication against unjust enemies, and the language is consistently forensic throughout. "Hear a just cause (ṣedeq), O LORD; listen to my cry; give ear to my prayer from lips without deceit (mirmāh)" — the king approaches God as a plaintiff before the divine court, claiming the justice of his cause and the sincerity of his petition. "Lips without deceit" is the claim of complete rhetorical integrity — no manipulation, no flattery, no hidden agenda (contrasting with Ps 12:2-4's corrupt speech). The divine court is the final court of appeal when human courts fail.
17.2: "Let my vindication (mišpāṭî) come from you; let your eyes behold what is just (mêšārîm)" — mišpāṭ is both legal process and verdict: the psalmist requests that the divine judge render a fair decision. "Behold what is just" — the impartial divine gaze (cf. Ps 9:8; 11:4) is exactly what the psalmist invites. The petition is not for special favor but for honest judgment, confident that honest judgment will vindicate. This is the radical confidence of Ps 1's theology: the righteous need only ask for divine scrutiny, not divine partiality.
17.3: "You have tested my heart; you have examined me through the night; you have tried me (ṣĕraftanî) and found nothing" — the refining metaphor again (cf. Ps 12:6). Ṣāraf is the metallurgist's verb: to smelt, assay, refine metal in fire. God has applied the refiner's examination and found no impurity. "Through the night" — either the night of a temple vigil during which this prayer was offered (a common practice; cf. Ps 16:7 and 17:15), or the ongoing night of trial and suffering through which God has been silently watching and testing the psalmist's integrity.
17.4: "By the word of your lips I have avoided the ways of the violent (pārîṣ)" — the claim of Torah-shaped formation. "By the word of your lips (biḏbar śĕpātêkā)" — the psalmist has shaped his conduct by divine speech, not by cultural convention. Pārîṣ (violent, destructive) describes the social predator of Pss 10, 12, 14. The word of God functions as counter-formation, creating a person whose character resists the ambient culture of violence — an ethic shaped from above, not from below.
17.5: "My steps have kept to your paths (ʾăšûrōtay); my feet have not stumbled (lōʾ-māṭû)" — ʾāšar (to step, go straight) and nāṭāh (to turn aside, deviate) form the contrast. The paths of God are the covenant way of Torah; stumbling is the standard metaphor for moral failure in wisdom literature (Prov 3:23; 4:12; Isa 59:10). The path metaphor in the Psalms (Pss 1, 23, 25, 27) reflects the ancient understanding of covenant life as a journey on a defined road — not a series of isolated ethical decisions but a sustained directional movement.
17.6: "I call on you, for you will answer me, O God; turn your ear (haṭṭēh-ʾoznĕkā) to me; hear my words" — the confidence of petition grounded in covenant relationship. "Turn your ear" is a physical idiom: the judge or patron physically turning to give ear signals willingness to receive a petition. In ancient Mediterranean patronage, the ability to get access to a powerful person's ear was the key social currency. The psalmist claims access to the greatest patron — not by social standing but by covenant — with the confidence that YHWH will incline toward those who call on him.
17.7: "Display your wonderful steadfast love (hapleʾ ḥăsādêkā), O Savior (môšîaʿ) of those who seek refuge from their enemies by your right hand" — Hapleʾ (make wonderful, distinguish, make visible) — make the ḥeseḏ conspicuously evident. Môšîaʿ is the Hiphil participle of yāšaʿ (save): the one who saves, the deliverer. This is the root of yēšûaʿ (Joshua/Jesus) — "the LORD saves." The petition invokes God's covenant name and saving character simultaneously, asking for a visible demonstration of ḥeseḏ against the background of military threat.
17.8: "Guard me as the apple of your eye (ʾîšôn bat-ʿāyin); hide me in the shadow of your wings" — two protection images. (1) ʾIšôn (apple of the eye) literally means "little man" — the tiny image one sees reflected in another's pupil; by extension, what is most precious and most carefully guarded. Cf. Deut 32:10 — God keeps Israel "as the apple of his eye." (2) Shadow of wings (bĕṣēl kĕnāpêkā) — God protecting as a bird covers its young (Deut 32:11; Ruth 2:12; Pss 36:7; 57:1; 61:4; 91:4). Both images express the intimate, attentive protection of what is most valued.
17.9: "From the wicked who assail me, from my deadly enemies (ʾōyĕbay bĕnepeš — enemies against the soul/life) who encircle me" — the military encirclement image. In ancient siege warfare, to encircle a city cut off escape and supply — the total vulnerability of the besieged. The phrase "enemies against the soul/life" specifies those who seek not mere defeat but complete annihilation — nepeš (life, soul, the person) itself is what they target. The psalmist is not dealing with political opposition but with enemies who want him utterly destroyed.
17.10: "Their hearts are closed to compassion (ḥelbāmô sāgerû — their fat encloses them); their mouths speak with arrogance" — "their fat" (ḥēleb) refers to the rich fat around vital organs. To be enclosed in fat is to be insulated against any perception of others' suffering — self-satisfied, impervious. This physiological metaphor for moral imperviousness recurs in the Prophets: Isaiah 6:10 (heart fat, ears heavy, eyes closed); Jeremiah 5:28 (the wicked grow fat and sleek). The sequence is anatomically precise: a fat heart produces an arrogant mouth.
17.11: "Now they surround our every step; they watch for a chance to bring us to the ground (lintôt bāʾāreṣ — to stretch us out on the earth)" — the shift from "me" (my steps, my feet — vv. 5, 11) to "us" (bring us to the ground) suggests the prayer has a communal dimension: the king petitions for himself and his community together. The watching for a moment of vulnerability (ʿênêhem yāšîtû — their eyes they set) is the predatory gaze scanning for the opportune moment, the same gaze as the lion crouching in Ps 10:9-10.
17.12: "He is like a lion (ʾaryēh) eager for prey, like a young lion (kĕpîr) crouching in ambush" — the dual lion image, recalling Ps 10:9. ʾAryēh (mature lion) and kĕpîr (young male lion in his prime — the most dangerous) emphasize both power and cunning at their peak. ANE royal iconography regularly depicted enemies as lions to be defeated — the king's prowess was demonstrated by subduing both wild animals and foreign enemies. David's lion-killing (1 Sam 17:34-37) established his credentials as the one capable of overcoming such threats; here the enemy takes the lion's role against the psalmist.
17.13: "Arise, LORD! Confront him! Bring him down! Rescue me from the wicked by your sword" — five imperatives in rapid succession. The battle-cry qûmāh yhwh (Arise, LORD) echoes Num 10:35 (the ark's departure formula), Pss 3:7; 7:6; 9:19; 10:12. The urgency and compression of five consecutive imperatives mirrors the panic of immediate threat. "Your sword (ḥarbĕkā)" — God as divine warrior brandishing his weapon is widespread in ANE iconography and biblical tradition: Deut 32:41-43 ("I will sharpen my flashing sword"); Isa 66:16; Ezek 21.
17.14: "By your hand, LORD, from those whose portion (ḥeleq) is in this present life" — the enemies are "men whose portion is in this life" — contrasting sharply with Ps 16:5 (YHWH as the psalmist's portion). The wicked have no heavenly inheritance; this world's goods are their entire allotment. "Their belly is filled with what you store up" — God is giving them temporal prosperity (their "portion"), yet this is precisely what makes them dangerous. Their children will inherit what remains, but the inheritance is finite. The contrast with the psalmist's eternal inheritance (Ps 16:11) frames the psalm's two competing life-orientations.
17.15: "As for me, I will behold your face in righteousness (ṣedeq); when I awake I will be satisfied with your likeness (tĕmûnātĕkā)" — the closing verse sets the ultimate contrast with v. 14. The wicked have their entire portion in this life; the psalmist's portion is the vision of God. "Beholding the face" is the language of temple access — to see God's face in the sanctuary (Pss 11:7; 42:2; 63:2). Tĕmûnāh (likeness, form) suggests a vision of divine glory — the manifestation of God's presence, not direct access to the divine essence (cf. Num 12:8, where Moses sees the tĕmûnāh of YHWH). "When I awake" carries both the immediate sense of dawn after a night vigil and an eschatological overtone resonating with Ps 16:10's resurrection hope.

34.1: The superscription connects Psalm 34 to David's flight to Gath and his feigned madness before the Philistine king (1 Sam 21:10–15). However, that narrative names the king "Achish," while this superscription says "Abimelech" — a discrepancy long noted by commentators. The most plausible explanation is that Abimelech ("my father is king") functioned as a dynastic title for Philistine rulers, much as Pharaoh designated Egyptian kings generically, and Agag was a title for Amalekite kings. This is supported by Gen 20:2 and 26:1, where Abraham and Isaac both encounter someone named Abimelech in similar contexts centuries apart. The psalm itself is a carefully structured alphabetic acrostic: each of the 22 verses begins with a successive letter of the Hebrew alphabet (aleph through taw), a wisdom teaching device signaling comprehensive coverage of the subject.
34.2: "My soul boasts in the LORD" — the verb hāllal (to boast, praise, shine) is the root of hallĕlûyāh. The distinction here is between self-boasting (hāllal benapšô), which is condemned in the Psalter (Ps 52:1; 94:4), and boasting in the LORD (hāllal bYHWH, cf. Jer 9:24; 1 Cor 1:31), which is the right orientation of praise. "The humble (ʿănāwîm) will hear and be glad" — the ʿănāwîm are the pious poor, those who have internalized their dependence on God; this is a social and spiritual category that became central to Second Temple piety (the ʿănāwîm of Isaiah 61, the "poor in spirit" of the Beatitudes).
34.3: "Magnify the LORD with me and let us exalt his name together" — the invitation to communal praise is characteristic of thanksgiving psalms performed at the temple following personal deliverance. The thanksgiving psalm had a social function: the worshipper who had been delivered would bring an offering and testify publicly in the temple courts, inviting the assembled community to join in praise (cf. Ps 22:22–25; 116:12–14). "His name" — the divine name carried the accumulated weight of all YHWH's prior acts of deliverance; to exalt the name is to declare the character it represents.
34.4: "I sought the LORD (dāraštî) and he answered me" — dāraš (seek, inquire) is the standard term for consulting the sanctuary oracle or petitioning the temple deity. In ancient Israel, "seeking the LORD" could mean physically going to the sanctuary, consulting a priest, or earnest prayer. The verb here functions as a testimony formula: the speaker reports what happened when they sought YHWH in prayer. "He delivered me from all my fears (mĕgûrôtāy)" — māgôr (fear, dread, terror) from gûr (to sojourn, be a stranger); the fears of one who is without protection in a hostile environment — precisely the David-in-Gath situation of the superscription.
34.5: "Those who look to him are radiant (nāhărû); their faces will not be ashamed" — nāhar means to shine or to stream (as a river); both meanings carry nuance here. The radiance of those who look to YHWH contrasts with the "shining" of the proud who glorify themselves. Face-shame (bōšet pānîm) in ANE honor-shame culture was the most devastating social consequence — public humiliation before one's community. The promise is not merely internal dignity but vindication in the eyes of the community, the restoration of social honor.
34.6: "This poor man (ʿānî) cried and the LORD heard" — the verse shifts abruptly to third person, creating a slight puzzle. Many interpreters read it as the psalmist speaking of himself in the third person (distancing himself from his own abasement), which was common in the Psalter and in ANE royal inscriptions. Others read it as a general statement about the pious poor inserted into the personal testimony. The shift from "I" (vv. 3–5) to "this poor man" highlights that the psalmist's experience is paradigmatic — it illustrates what the LORD does for all who call on him in their poverty.
34.7: "The angel of the LORD (malʾak YHWH) encamps around those who fear him and rescues them" — the malʾak YHWH (messenger/angel of YHWH) is a distinctive presence in the Hebrew Bible who speaks as YHWH himself (Gen 16:7–13; 22:11–18; Exod 3:2; Josh 5:13–15; Judg 2:1–4). This figure simultaneously represents and is identified with YHWH, leading to later theological reflection on divine presence and mediation. The imagery here is military: the angel "encamps" (ḥōneh) like a protective garrison around those who fear God — one warrior deity providing a protective perimeter around the pious.
34.8: "Taste and see that the LORD is good!" — one of the Psalter's most vivid sensory invitations. "Taste" (ṭaʿamû) may carry a specific liturgical reference: in the peace offering (šĕlāmîm) worship, the worshipper ate portions of the sacrifice in the temple courts as a feast of communion with God. To eat of the sacrifice was to participate in the divine table — the taste of the offering was meant to convey the goodness of the one worshipped. The invitation to "taste and see" thus may be a literal invitation to join the communal sacrificial feast, in which divine goodness was experienced through the physical act of eating. Ṭôb (good) — divine goodness is both moral excellence and practical beneficence toward those who seek him.
34.9: "Fear the LORD, you his holy people (qĕdōšāyw)" — qādôš (holy, set apart) applied to the human worshippers of YHWH designates those who belong to the divine sphere through covenant relationship. The qĕdōšîm are the covenant community understood as YHWH's holy ones, distinct from the nations. "Those who fear him lack nothing" (ʾên maḥsôr lîrēʾāyw) — the comprehensive sufficiency of divine provision for the covenant community; the same claim as Ps 23 ("I shall not want"). The statement is empirical-covenantal: those within the covenant structure experience its material and spiritual provisions.
34.10: "Young lions (kĕpîrîm) go hungry and lack food" — the young lion is the apex predator, the creature most capable of obtaining its own food by strength and violence. The shocking reversal: even the mightiest of natural hunters may go without, while "those who seek the LORD lack no good thing." This antithesis situates covenant piety above the food chain itself. In ANE wisdom literature, the power of the strong against the weak was taken as a natural fact; Psalm 34 subverts this by making YHWH's provision superior to the strength of the predator.
34.11: "Come, children, listen to me; I will teach you the fear of the LORD" — this verse signals the explicit wisdom-teaching register of the psalm. The address "children" (bānîm, sons/students) matches the teacher-student address of Proverbs (Prov 1:8; 2:1; 3:1; 4:1; 5:1; 7:1). The psalm shifts from testimony (vv. 1–10) to instruction (vv. 11–22), making explicit the pedagogical function of the thanksgiving form: the public testimony becomes a teaching event. "The fear of the LORD" is the wisdom tradition's master concept — not paralytic terror but reverential orientation toward God as the beginning of wisdom (Prov 1:7; 9:10; Ps 111:10).
34.12: "Who is the person who desires life (ḥayyîm) and loves long days to enjoy what is good?" — the wisdom question in rhetorical form, expecting the answer "all of us." In the wisdom tradition, long life and enjoyment of good are the fruits of righteous living (Prov 3:1–2, 16; Deut 5:16; 6:2; 11:9). "Life" here is not merely biological existence but flourishing existence — what the OT calls šālôm: wholeness, integration, prosperity within a good life. The question sets up the instruction of vv. 13–14 as the path to this desired outcome.
34.13: "Keep your tongue from evil and your lips from speaking lies" — speech ethics is a major domain of Second Temple wisdom. Ben Sira (Ecclesiasticus) devotes multiple chapters to the dangers of the tongue (Sir 14:1; 19:4–17; 22:27–23:15; 28:12–26). In the social world of the ancient village, false testimony, slander, and deceptive speech could destroy a person's reputation, legal standing, and economic livelihood. The "lying lips" (śĕpātayim mibbĕr mirmāh) echo the legal context of false witness in Exod 20:16 and the covenant curses of Deut 27–28.
34.14: "Turn from evil and do good; seek peace (šālôm) and pursue it" — the two movement verbs are emphatic: sûr (turn away, remove oneself) and rādap (pursue, chase, hunt). The pursuit of šālôm requires active chase, not passive waiting. Šālôm in its full semantic range is not merely absence of conflict but wholeness, completeness, right relationship between persons and with God. This verse was prominent in Second Temple Judaism as a summary ethical instruction (cf. 1 Pet 3:11, which quotes it, and its echo in the Beatitude "blessed are the peacemakers").
34.15: "The LORD's eyes are on the righteous and his ears are open to their cry" — the divine senses directed toward the righteous: eyes (watchful care) and ears (responsive attention). This anthropomorphic language maps onto the temple theology in which the divine presence "watches" and "hears" from the sanctuary. Solomon's prayer at the temple dedication (1 Kgs 8:29) uses identical language: "May your eyes be open toward this temple night and day, this place of which you said, 'My Name shall be there,' so that you will hear the prayer your servant prays toward this place."
34.16: "The LORD turns his face (pānāyw) against those who do evil, to cut off the memory of them from the earth" — the divine "face" directed against someone is the most severe form of divine opposition (Lev 17:10; 20:3, 5, 6; 26:17; Ezek 14:8; 15:7). This inverts the Aaronic blessing in which the divine face is lifted upon the worshipper (Num 6:25–26). "Cutting off the memory" — the same eradication formula used throughout the Psalter and Deuteronomy for enemies; in ANE culture, to have one's name and memory cut off was the ultimate defeat, equivalent to non-existence.
34.17: "The righteous cry out (ṣāʿăqû) and the LORD hears; he delivers them from all their troubles" — ṣāʿaq is the urgent cry of distress, the same verb used for the cry of the Israelites in Egypt (Exod 2:23; 3:7, 9) and for the blood of Abel crying from the ground (Gen 4:10). It is more urgent than ordinary prayer — a cry from a situation of genuine danger or oppression. The pattern established here — the righteous cry, YHWH hears, YHWH delivers — is the structural backbone of the entire Psalter's theology of prayer.
34.18: "The LORD is close to the brokenhearted (nišbĕrê-lēb) and rescues those whose spirit (rûaḥ) is crushed (dakkāʾ)" — šābar lēb (broken heart) is the condition of one whose inner strength has been destroyed by grief, loss, or oppression. Dakkāʾ (crushed) from dākaʾ (to crush, grind down, pulverize) — the most severe form of spiritual depletion. This verse became a foundational text for understanding divine compassion toward the suffering in Second Temple Judaism and in the NT (Isa 57:15; 61:1 — which Jesus applies to himself in Luke 4:18). The irony: precisely when a person is most broken, God is closest — divine proximity is inversely proportional to human strength.
34.19: "The righteous face many afflictions (rabbôt rāʿôt)" — the Psalter does not promise immunity from suffering; it promises divine companionship through suffering. This is the antidote to any prosperity-gospel reading of the Psalter. The wisdom tradition is honest about the suffering of the righteous (Job, Ps 73, Lamentations) — the question is not whether suffering occurs but whether YHWH is present within it. "The LORD delivers them from all of them" — the comprehensive deliverance is eschatological as much as historical: the full salvation may await final vindication.
34.20: "He protects all his bones; not one of them will be broken" — the language of bone-breaking has a specific legal and cultic background. In the Passover law, the bones of the Passover lamb were not to be broken (Exod 12:46; Num 9:12) — the lamb was to be consumed whole, unbroken. The preservation of the righteous person's bones echoes this sacrificial requirement, implicitly comparing the righteous sufferer to the Passover lamb. John 19:36 explicitly cites this verse (or the Passover law it echoes) as fulfilled when the soldiers did not break Jesus's legs. The verse thus stands at the intersection of wisdom ethics and sacrificial typology.
34.21: "Evil will slay the wicked, and those who hate the righteous will be condemned (yĕʾšāmû)" — the retributive theology of the Psalter: evil is self-destructive. Tišlāḥ rāʿāh rāšāʿ (evil slays the wicked) — the instrument of destruction is the wicked's own evil, which turns on its owner (the boomerang motif of Pss 7:15–16; 9:15–16; 35:7–8). ʾāšam (to be condemned, bear guilt, be guilty) — in the legal sphere, this is the verdict of criminal guilt; in the cultic sphere, it is the state of incurred guilt requiring atonement. For those who hate the righteous, the guilt they incur is their own sentence.
34.22: "The LORD redeems (pōdeh) the lives of his servants; none who take refuge (ḥōsîm) in him will be condemned" — pādāh (redeem, ransom) in its original context meant buying back something pledged as collateral or a person sold into slavery; it is an economic-legal metaphor for divine rescue. The ḥôsîm (those who take refuge) is a recurring designation for those who trust in YHWH rather than military strength or wealth (Pss 2:12; 5:11; 17:7; 25:20; 31:19; 37:40). "Will not be condemned (lōʾ yeʾšāmû)" — inverts the condemnation of the wicked in v. 21; the final verse of the acrostic closes the A-to-Z argument: those in YHWH's keeping are not under the condemnation that destroys the wicked.

35.1: Psalm 35 is an individual lament in three clearly marked sections (vv. 1–10, 11–18, 19–28), each ending with a vow to praise God in the assembly. The opening imperative "Contend (rîbāh), O LORD, against those who contend with me" is a lawsuit term: rîb is the legal dispute, the pleading of a case in court. The psalmist summons YHWH as his legal advocate — the one who will argue his case before the divine court. This forensic framing (also Ps 43:1; Isa 49:25; Jer 2:9; 51:36; Mic 7:9) reflects the ancient Israelite institution of the divine judge as the ultimate court of appeal for those who cannot obtain justice in human courts.
35.2: "Take up your shield (māgēn) and armor (ṣinnāh) and come to my aid" — the divine warrior motif, in which YHWH is imagined as a fully armed soldier fighting for his people. Two types of shield are mentioned: the māgēn (small round shield carried on the arm) and the ṣinnāh (the large full-body shield). The armament request is hyperbolic — the psalmist calls on the entire arsenal of the divine warrior. This divine warrior imagery is central to the Exodus tradition (Exod 14:14; 15:3), the prophets (Isa 42:13; 59:16–17; Zeph 3:17), and appears in the armor of God metaphor of Eph 6:10–17.
35.3: "Brandish the spear (ḥănît) and block the path" — the specific weapons named here are those of the professional warrior class: the ḥănît (long battle-spear) was the characteristic weapon of the Philistine giant (1 Sam 17:7; 2 Sam 21:19). The divine warrior who carries such weapons surpasses even the feared Philistine heavy infantry. "Say to my soul, 'I am your deliverance (yĕšûʿātĕkā)!'" — YHWH speaking into the nepeš (soul, inner self) is the personal dimension of divine assurance: not just external military action but an inner word of peace. The term yĕšûʿāh (salvation, deliverance) is the root of the name Yeshua/Jesus.
35.4: "Let those who seek my life be put to shame (yēbōšû) and humiliated" — the shame petition is a recurring feature of lament psalms (Pss 25:3; 31:17; 40:14; 69:6; 70:2; 71:24). In ANE honor-shame culture, shame before the community was the severest social consequence — a reversal of social standing. The petition is not merely personal vindication but the public restoration of honor that has been taken away by the enemy's slanders and attacks. "Those who plot evil against me be turned back in confusion (wĕyissōgû ʾāḥôr wĕyikālamû)" — the double movement of retreat and disgrace.
35.5: "Let them be like chaff (môṣ) before the wind" — the threshing floor analogy. In ancient Israelite agriculture, grain was threshed by trampling (oxen or teams walking over the stalks) and then winnowed by tossing into the air so the wind could carry away the chaff while the grain fell. The chaff that blew away was worthless, insubstantial, easily dispersed. This imagery appears in Ps 1:4 for the wicked in contrast to the righteous person who is like a firmly planted tree. "The angel of the LORD driving them off" — the same divine warrior-messenger who protected the righteous in Ps 34:7 here pursues the wicked.
35.6: "Make their path dark and treacherous (ḥălaqlaqqôt), with the angel of the LORD pursuing them" — ḥălaqlaqqôt (slippery, treacherous places) from ḥālaq (smooth, slippery). In hilly terrain, smooth rock faces and loose scree were genuinely dangerous; the metaphor of a slippery path for those being pursued invokes the genuine terror of flight through hostile terrain (cf. Jer 23:12; Pss 73:18). The pursuing angel here mirrors the pursuing angel of Num 22:22–27, who blocked Balaam's path. The divine pursuer makes escape impossible.
35.7: "Without reason (ḥinnām) they concealed their net to trap me; without reason they dug a pit for my life" — ḥinnām (without cause, gratuitously, for nothing) is the same word used in Job 1:9 when the satan asks if Job fears God "for nothing" (ḥinnām), and in Job 2:3 where God says the satan moved against Job "without cause." The word marks the moral bankruptcy of the attack: there is no legitimate grievance, no provocation, no justification. Hunting traps (nets, pits) were the standard OT metaphor for hidden schemes and ambushes (Pss 9:15; 31:4; 57:6; 140:5; 141:9–10; Jer 18:22).
35.8: "May ruin overtake him when he least expects it" — the boomerang principle: the trap designed for the righteous catches the trapper instead. This retributive pattern runs through the Psalter and wisdom literature (Prov 26:27; Eccl 10:8; Sir 27:26–27). "The net he hid catch him; may he fall into it to his destruction (bĕšôʾāh)" — šôʾāh is the same root as the Hebrew word for the Holocaust (destruction, catastrophic ruin). The poetic justice is complete: the pit dug for another becomes one's own grave.
35.9: "Then my soul will rejoice in the LORD, exulting in his deliverance" — the vow-of-praise formula that ends the first section. The future joy anticipated in prayer is a distinctive feature of the lament-to-praise movement in the Psalter: the psalmist commits in advance to praise the God who has not yet acted, expressing confidence that deliverance is coming. This "anticipatory praise" (sometimes called the "psalm of trust" element within laments) is the most distinctive theological contribution of the lament genre: praise uttered in the darkness before the dawn.
35.10: "Every bone in my body will say, 'LORD, who is like you?'" — the totality of the person (every bone = the whole physical self) engaged in praise. "Who rescues the poor (ʿānî) from those who overpower them" — the divine champion of the weak is one of the central themes of the Psalter and the prophets. The rhetorical question mî kāmôkā (who is like you?) is the incomparability formula, the most emphatic assertion of monotheistic uniqueness. It appears in the Song of the Sea (Exod 15:11), in the prophets (Isa 40:18; 46:5; Mic 7:18), and throughout the Psalter as the climactic proclamation of divine uniqueness.
35.11: "Malicious witnesses (ʿēdê ḥāmās) rise against me; they charge me with things I know nothing of" — the legal setting is explicit. ʿēd ḥāmās (witness of violence/malice) is the false witness condemned in the Decalogue (Exod 20:16: "do not give false testimony") and in the Deuteronomic law of witnesses (Deut 19:15–21). In the legal system of ancient Israel, testimony by two or three witnesses was required for conviction; false accusers who bore false witness received the penalty that would have been inflicted on the accused (Deut 19:19). The psalmist faces the worst social-legal threat of the ancient world: coordinated perjury before an Israelite court.
35.12: "They repay me evil for good, leaving my soul (napšî) desolate (šĕkôl)" — the inversion of reciprocity. In ANE honor culture, reciprocal gift exchange and loyalty were the social glue of community life. To repay evil for good was the most profound violation of social covenant (cf. Prov 17:13; Gen 44:4; 1 Sam 25:21; John 10:32). Šĕkôl (desolate, bereaved, like a parent whose child has died) — the root of šĕkôl (bereavement after the death of children); to be left šĕkôl is to experience the most devastating social loss known in antiquity, the cutting off of one's line.
35.13: "But when they were sick, I wore sackcloth (śāq) and humbled myself with fasting" — sackcloth was a coarse garment of dark goat-hair worn as the standard dress of mourning, fasting, and penitence throughout the ancient Near East (1 Kgs 21:27; 2 Kgs 6:30; Neh 9:1; Isa 58:5; Jon 3:5–8). Donning sackcloth for someone else's illness was the ultimate expression of solidarity — treating their pain as your own grief. Fasting accompanied sackcloth as the bodily practice of prayer, intensifying the petition (Ps 69:10; 109:24; Ezra 8:23; Esth 4:3).
35.14: "I acted like one grieving for a dear friend (ʾōhēb) or brother; I bowed down in mourning as one who weeps for his mother" — the depth of social solidarity expressed here is remarkable: the psalmist mourned for his enemies as if they were his closest kin — friend, brother, mother. ANE mourning customs included wearing sackcloth, tearing garments, putting dust or ashes on the head, refusing to eat, and verbal lamentation (Gen 37:34; 2 Sam 1:11–12; 3:31–32; 13:19). To perform all these customs for an enemy who has now turned on you is the deepest form of social betrayal: the person you wept for now brings false charges against you.
35.15: "But when I stumbled, they rejoiced and gathered together; attackers assembled against me" — Schadenfreude (joy at another's misfortune) was a recognized moral vice in both Israel and the ancient Near East. Ezek 25:3, 6 and Lam 2:15–16 record the enemies of Israel rejoicing over Jerusalem's fall with the same šāmaḥ (rejoice) verb. The gathering of accusers and attackers when the psalmist stumbled suggests a social situation in which a change of fortune (illness, legal setback, military defeat) was treated as evidence of guilt — the friends who were mourned now become accusers.
35.16: "Like godless mockers at a banquet (ḥanpê-laʿăgê māʿôg), they gnashed their teeth at me" — a notoriously difficult Hebrew phrase. Ḥanpê (godless, profane) + laʿăgê (mockers) + māʿôg (cake, pastry; or a place name). The most defensible reading: "like the profane mockers at a feast [who mock the absent]" — the banquet setting where mockery and slander of the absent (table gossip, symposium wit at others' expense) was a recognized social practice. "Gnashing teeth" (ḥāraq šinnayim) is the physical expression of aggressive rage or contempt (Pss 37:12; 112:10; Lam 2:16; Acts 7:54).
35.17: "How long, O Lord (ʾădōnāy), will you keep watching (tirʾeh)?" — the "how long?" question is the characteristic temporal challenge of the lament psalm (Pss 6:3; 13:1–2; 74:10; 80:4; 90:13; 94:3; Hab 1:2; 2:6). Divine watching without acting is experienced as cruel indifference — the judge who sees the crime and does nothing. "Rescue my precious self (napšî) from these lions" — the lion metaphor returns (cf. v. 10 above; Ps 22:12–13, 21). The yĕḥîdāh (my precious one, my only one) is a particularly intimate self-designation — one's unique, irreplaceable life.
35.18: "I will give you thanks in the great assembly (qāhāl rāb); among the vast multitude (ʿam ʿāṣûm) I will praise you" — the second vow of praise closes the second section. The public assembly is a key setting for thanksgiving in the Psalter. The peace offering feast (cf. Ps 34:8 above) was performed in the temple courts and included a public proclamation of the deliverance received (cf. Ps 22:22–25; 40:9–10; 66:16–19; 116:14–19). To vow public praise is to commit to the social performance of gratitude — the testimony that invites the community into the psalmist's experience of divine faithfulness.
35.19: "Do not let my enemies gloat over me without cause (ḥinnām); do not let those who hate me without reason (šĕqer) exchange knowing glances" — ḥinnām appears again (cf. v. 7): the gratuitous, unprovoked nature of the hostility is emphasized. "Exchange knowing glances (yiqrĕṣû-ʿāyin)" — the wink or meaningful glance exchanged between conspirators was the non-verbal communication of shared deception in ancient social settings (Prov 6:13; 10:10). It was the signal of those who coordinate slander without using words that could be overheard.
35.20: "They do not pursue peace (šālôm); instead they invent slanders (mirmôt dĕbārê) against those who live quietly in the land" — the "quiet in the land" (rĕgîʿê ʾāreṣ) is an unusual phrase sometimes taken as a technical term for the peaceful peasant class, those living without political prominence and without causing harm. The attack on those who "live quietly" — who seek no conflict — makes the hostility even more gratuitous. This is not a conflict between powers; it is the persecution of the unassuming by the politically connected. Second Temple Jewish interpretation often read the "quiet in the land" as the pious remnant (ʿănāwîm) persecuted by the powerful.
35.21: "They open their mouths wide against me and say, 'Aha! Aha! (heʾāḥ heʾāḥ) We have seen it with our own eyes!'" — the exclamation heʾāḥ is a gloating cry of triumph at another's downfall, used for mock-triumph over an enemy (Pss 40:15; 70:3; Ezek 25:3; 26:2; 36:2). The claim "we have seen it with our own eyes" is a legal formula — eyewitness testimony was the strongest form of evidence in ancient courts. The adversaries are manufacturing false eyewitness accounts, the severest form of legal perjury.
35.22: "You have seen this (rāʾîtāh), LORD; do not stay silent (al-teḥĕraš)" — the psalmist turns the courtroom language against the accusers: YHWH has seen what they claim to have witnessed; divine testimony trumps human perjury. "Do not stay silent" — divine silence in the face of injustice is experienced as complicity (cf. Hab 1:2, 13; Ps 83:1). The petition for YHWH to speak is simultaneously a petition for action: when God speaks, things happen.
35.23: "Arise (hāʿîrāh) and wake yourself (hāqîṣāh) to my defense" — the "rise up" imperative battle-cry (cf. Num 10:35; Pss 3:7; 7:6; 9:19; 10:12) is here doubled with "wake yourself," creating an image of the sleeping deity who must be roused. This anthropomorphism reaches its most dramatic expression in Ps 44:23 ("Awake, O Lord! Why do you sleep?") and its gentle negation in Ps 121:4 ("He who guards Israel will neither slumber nor sleep"). The tension between the sleeping/silent God and the responsive God is honestly maintained in the Psalter without easy resolution.
35.24: "Vindicate me, LORD my God, according to your righteousness (ṣidqĕkā)" — the appeal to divine righteousness as the basis of vindication invokes the covenant character of YHWH. Ṣādaqāh (righteousness) in the legal sphere means the right order of things — what is due to each party in a dispute. God's righteousness is not a demanding standard the psalmist must meet but the character that drives God to restore right order when it has been violated. The same appeal appears in Ps 31:1; 71:2; 143:1, 11.
35.25: "Do not let them say in their hearts, 'Aha, just what we wanted!'" — the language of desire satisfied (heʾāḥ napšênû, literally "our soul says aha!") echoes the gloating of v. 21. The petition reaches into the adversaries' inner life — not merely their outward triumph but the inner satisfaction of appetite achieved. "We have devoured him (bĕlaʿănûhû)" — the swallowing image (cf. Lam 2:16; Ps 124:3) presents the enemy as predator consuming prey, the apex-predator imagery of the psalms of persecution.
35.26: "Let all who rejoice at my misfortune be put to shame and humiliated together" — the shame-reversal petition: those who enjoyed the psalmist's shame will experience shame themselves. "Clothed with shame and disgrace (ʿōṭû-bōšet ûkĕlimmāh)" — shame is a garment that visibly marks the one who wears it in ancient honor-shame culture. The image of being "clothed" in shame or glory occurs throughout the Psalter and the prophets (Ps 93:1 — YHWH clothed in majesty; Ps 109:18 — the wicked clothed in curses; Isa 61:3 — the garment of praise for the spirit of heaviness).
35.27: "Let those who delight in my vindication (ṣidqî) shout for joy and say, 'Great is the LORD, who takes pleasure in the welfare (šālôm) of his servant!'" — the communal dimension of individual vindication: the psalmist's restoration is not only a personal event but a liturgical occasion for all who have invested hope in YHWH's justice. "Those who delight in my vindication" are the solidarity community — the pious who have prayed for the falsely accused. YHWH's "pleasure in the welfare of his servant" (ḥāpēṣ šĕlôm ʿabdô) grounds the petition in divine character: the God who takes pleasure in human flourishing is the basis for expecting deliverance.
35.28: "Then my tongue will proclaim your righteousness and your praise all day long" — the third and final vow of praise closes the psalm. The temporal marker "all day long" (kol-hayyôm) indicates the continuous, unending character of the praise: not a single act of thanksgiving but a lifetime orientation. The psalm began with a forensic challenge (contend for me, v. 1) and ends with a public declaration (my tongue will proclaim) — the movement from crisis to testimony, from silence to speech, from fear to praise, is the structural arc of the entire Psalter's lament genre.

36.1: Psalm 36 is unusual in its structure: a wisdom meditation on wickedness (vv. 1–4) followed immediately by a hymn to divine character (vv. 5–9) and a brief petition with confidence statement (vv. 10–12). The superscription calls it a psalm of "David the servant of the LORD" — the ʿebed YHWH (servant of the LORD) designation links the author to the great servant figures of the prophets. The opening is remarkable: "the voice of transgression (nĕʾum-pešaʿ) speaks deep within the heart of the wicked." Nĕʾum is the prophetic oracle formula ("oracle of the LORD," nĕʾum YHWH); here wickedness itself speaks with prophetic authority from within the wicked person's lēb (heart). Sin is personified as an inner oracle, a demonic voice dictating the wicked's inner life.
36.2: "For he deceives himself into thinking his guilt cannot be discovered and condemned (limsōʾ ʿăwōnô lĕśānōʾ)" — the self-deception of the wicked is the theme. Hĕlîq (hāllaq, to be smooth, flattering) — the wicked person speaks smoothly to himself, self-flattering inner speech that makes his own guilt invisible. The result: there is no "fear of God before his eyes" — quoted by Paul in Rom 3:18 as the defining statement of universal human sinfulness and the culmination of his catena of OT texts demonstrating universal guilt (Rom 3:10–18). In Paul's reading, this is not only the condition of the wicked but the condition of all humans apart from divine grace.
36.3: "His mouth pours out wickedness (ʾāwen) and deception (mirmāh)" — ʾāwen (wickedness, vanity, emptiness, idolatry) is one of the Psalter's key moral terms, combining the meanings of wickedness, emptiness, and often idolatrous practice. The coupling of ʾāwen with mirmāh (treachery, deception) describes speech that is both morally corrupt and strategically deceptive. "He has stopped acting wisely (ḥādal lĕhaśkîl)" — śākal (to act wisely, have insight, prosper) is the wisdom vocabulary; to cease from wisdom is to abandon the fundamental orientation of covenant life. This cessation is presented as a deliberate act, not merely ignorance.
36.4: "On his bed he hatches schemes (yĕḥaššōb ʾāwen)" — the privacy of the bed where no social constraint operates becomes the laboratory of wickedness. The bed as the place of wicked scheming also appears in Mic 2:1: "Woe to those who plan iniquity and devise evil on their beds." "He places himself on a path that is not good (ʿal-derek lōʾ-ṭôb yityaṣṣēb)" — the deliberate positioning on an evil path; yāṣab (to stand firm, position oneself) suggests intentional commitment, not accidental wandering. "He refuses to reject evil (rāʿ lōʾ yimʾās)" — the active refusal to turn from evil. The portrait of wickedness in vv. 1–4 is complete: inner oracle, self-deception, wicked speech, cessation of wisdom, nocturnal scheming, deliberate path-setting, refusal of repentance.
36.5: "Your steadfast love (ḥasdĕkā), LORD, reaches to the heavens (bašāmayim); your faithfulness (ʾĕmûnātĕkā) extends to the clouds" — after the claustrophobic portrait of wickedness (vv. 1–4), the psalm explodes into cosmic scope. The divine attributes are mapped onto the vertical dimensions of the cosmos: ḥesed (steadfast love, covenant loyalty) reaches to the heavens; ʾĕmûnāh (faithfulness, reliability, steadiness) extends to the clouds. The heavens and clouds together represent the upper atmospheric sphere — but also beyond it: the attributes of God transcend even the highest created reality.
36.6: "Your righteousness (ṣidqātĕkā) towers like the mighty mountains (harĕrê-ʾēl); your judgments (mišpāṭêkā) plunge as deep as the ocean (tĕhôm rabbāh)" — the four paired terms (ḥesed, ʾĕmûnāh, ṣĕdāqāh, mišpāṭ) constitute the core vocabulary of divine character in the wisdom and prophetic traditions. Harĕrê-ʾēl ("mountains of God/El") — the superlative use of the divine name ʾEl means "towering/mighty mountains," not mountains literally belonging to God. Tĕhôm rabbāh (the great deep) — the primordial ocean-abyss of Gen 1:2, Job 38:16, the symbol of immeasurable depth. "You preserve both human and animal (ʾādām ûbĕhēmāh)" — the scope of divine care encompasses all living creatures, both human and non-human (echoing Ps 104 and anticipating the Noahic covenant).
36.7: "How priceless (mah-yāqār) is your steadfast love, O God! The children of humanity (bĕnê ʾādām) take refuge under the shadow of your wings (bĕṣēl kĕnāpêkā)" — the wing-shadow image occurs throughout the Psalter (Pss 17:8; 57:1; 61:4; 63:7; 91:4) and in the Torah (Deut 32:11 — God as eagle bearing Israel on his wings). The image draws on multiple registers: (a) the cherubim wings spread over the ark in the Holy of Holies, representing divine protection; (b) the maternal bird covering her chicks; (c) the Boaz-Ruth idiom of covenant protection (Ruth 2:12: "under whose wings you have come to take refuge"). The temple context may be primary: the ark-cherubim wings were the visible symbol of divine shelter in the sanctuary.
36.8: "They feast on the richness of your house (yirwĕyûn middeśen bêtêkā)" — the language of the temple feast is explicit: dešen (fatness, richness) is the abundance of the peace offering meal. In the temple liturgy, the peace offering (šĕlāmîm) was the only sacrifice where the worshipper ate — portions of the cooked meat were eaten by the priest, the Levites, and the worshipper's household in the temple courts. To eat from the "fatness of your house" is to participate in this communal sacred meal.
