The sun and moon as measures of temporal duration are the cosmic markers of permanence (cf. Ps 89:36-37: the Davidic throne established 'as long as the sun endures ... like the moon established forever'). The prayer expands from historical kingship to the eternal kingdom that all Davidic kings pointed toward.
72.6: 'May he be like rain falling on newly mown grass, like showers that water the earth' — the agricultural metaphor of the king's beneficent presence. Rain on newly mown grass was the timing of the spring rains in Israel's agricultural calendar: the early rains after the harvest prepared the ground for the next cycle. The king's just rule is like the seasonal rain that makes agricultural life possible — without it, everything withers; with it, all grows. The metaphor identifies the king's justice with the natural order of fertility.
72.7: 'In his days may the righteous flourish, and peace abound, until the moon is no more' — 'the righteous will flourish' — the correspondence between righteous governance and the flourishing of the righteous: the just king creates conditions under which covenant-faithful people can thrive. 'Until the moon is no more' — beyond the limits of the cosmic order itself, reaching eschatological finality. The prayer's temporal horizon has expanded to encompass the end of the present cosmos.
72.8: 'May he have dominion from sea to sea, from the River to the ends of the earth' — the universal dominion formula. 'From sea to sea' and 'from the River (Euphrates) to the ends of the earth' is the maximum geographical extent possible in the ancient Near Eastern conceptual world. This is the Abrahamic promise of universal blessing (Gen 12:3; 22:17-18) applied to the Davidic king, and it is the same language used in Zech 9:10 for the coming king who would 'speak peace to the nations' — the messianic king on a donkey.
72.9: 'May desert peoples bow before him, and his enemies lick the dust' — the 'lick the dust' was the extreme submission gesture in ancient Near Eastern protocol: the prostrated vassal's mouth at ground level before the king. ANE royal inscriptions and art regularly depicted conquered peoples in this posture. The 'desert peoples' and enemies bowing evoke universal submission — not just the known nations but even those on the margins of the known world.
72.10: 'May the kings of Tarshish and the coastlands bring tribute; may the kings of Sheba and Seba offer gifts' — specific named locations representing the known world's extremities. Tarshish was in the far west (likely Spain or Sardinia — the most distant point of the ancient Phoenician trading world; cf. Jonah's attempt to flee there). Sheba was in the far south (Arabia or East Africa — the source of spice and gold; 1 Kgs 10: the Queen of Sheba's visit to Solomon). The four directions of the known world bring tribute to the ideal king.
72.11: 'Yes, may all kings bow down before him; may all nations serve him' — the universal scope: every political sovereign and every ethnic group. This is the eschatological vision of Ps 2:8 and Isa 49:23 combined. In the NT, this vision is applied to Christ — the King before whom every knee will bow (Phil 2:10-11: 'at the name of Jesus every knee shall bow ... every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is Lord').
72.12: 'For he delivers the needy when they cry for help, the poor and those who have no one to help them' — the 'for' grounds the universal submission of v. 11 in the king's care for the poor: the nations submit because this king is genuinely just. Unlike other ancient conquerors whose power was built on exploiting the weak, this king's authority rests on protecting the powerless. The king's greatness is measured not by tribute extracted but by the needy delivered.
72.13: 'He has compassion on the weak and the needy, and saves the lives of the poor' — 'compassion' is the Hebrew term for the tender concern that protects rather than exploits. 'Saves the lives of the poor' — the most direct intervention: preserving the very existence of those who would otherwise perish. In ancient law, the death of a poor person who could not pay debts was not necessarily the state's concern; the ideal king makes it his personal business.
72.14: 'He redeems their life from oppression and violence, and their blood is precious in his eyes' — 'their blood is precious in his eyes' is the king's counter-logic to oppression: in a world where the powerful treated the poor as disposable, this king values the life of the powerless as costly. The ransom/redemption vocabulary is the same used for YHWH redeeming Israel from Egypt. The king who redeems the poor images YHWH's own redemptive character.
72.15: 'Long may he live! May gold from Sheba be given to him! May prayer be made for him continually and blessings pronounced on him all day long' — the coronation liturgy of prayer and blessing for the king. 'Gold from Sheba' was the most prestigious gold in the ancient world (cf. Job 28:16; Isa 60:6: Sheba's gold coming to Jerusalem in the eschatological pilgrimage). The subjects' ongoing prayer and blessing for the king is the people's liturgical support of the royal office.
72.16: 'May grain be abundant throughout the land; may it wave on the mountaintops; may its yield be like Lebanon, and may the city bloom like the grass of the field' — the agricultural vision of the righteous king's reign: grain growing even on the mountaintops (normally too exposed for cultivation), yield as lush as the cedar forests of Lebanon (the most densely vegetated landscape in the ancient Near East). This is the reversal of the Deuteronomic curse (Deut 28:23: 'The sky over your head will be bronze, the ground beneath your feet iron') — the land expressing the covenant blessing of right rule.
72.17: 'May his name endure forever; may it continue as long as the sun endures! May all peoples find blessing through him; may they call him blessed' — 'his name endure forever' echoes the Davidic covenant promise (2 Sam 7:13: 'I will establish the throne of his kingdom forever'). 'May all peoples find blessing through him' (veyitbarachu vo kol-goyim, all nations shall bless themselves in him) — the Abrahamic blessing formula of Gen 12:3 ('in you all families of the earth shall be blessed') now applied to the Davidic king. The king becomes the conduit of the Abrahamic promise to the nations.
72.18: 'Blessed be the LORD, the God of Israel, who alone does wondrous things' — the doxology that begins the close of Book II. 'Who alone does wondrous things' — divine exclusivity in miracle-working: only YHWH, not the king, not any human patron, performs the genuinely marvelous acts. The doxology redirects the psalm's accumulated praise from the ideal king to the God who gives the king his authority and character.
72.19: 'Blessed be his glorious name forever; may the whole earth be filled with his glory! Amen and Amen' — the double 'Amen and Amen' closes Book II (as Ps 41:13's 'Amen and Amen' closed Book I). The prayer that 'the whole earth be filled with his glory' echoes the seraphim's acclamation of Isa 6:3 ('the whole earth is full of his glory') and the prayer of Moses in Num 14:21 ('all the earth shall be filled with the glory of the LORD'). The Psalter's editorial design reaches toward this eschatological hope: the God of Israel glorified throughout all creation.
72.20: 'The prayers of David, the son of Jesse, are ended' — the editorial colophon closing the Davidic collection within Book II. The statement is editorially significant: many psalms after Ps 72 bear Davidic superscriptions, so 'ended' refers to this collection specifically, not to all Davidic psalms in the Psalter. The colophon makes visible the editorial process behind the Psalter: collections of psalms were assembled and organized by editors who left markers of their work. 'David the son of Jesse' — the full genealogical identification emphasizes the historical particularity of the one from whom the royal tradition flows.

73.1: Psalm 73 opens Book III of the Psalter (Ps 73-89) and is attributed to Asaph, the Levitical musician appointed by David (1 Chr 6:39; 15:17) who became the head of one of the three guilds of temple singers. Psalm 73 is one of the great wisdom-theodicy psalms of the OT, comparable to Job in its engagement with the problem of the wicked prospering. 'Truly God is good to Israel, to those who are pure in heart' — the psalm opens with the conclusion reached only after the crisis of vv. 2-16 and the resolution of v. 17. The opening statement is the post-sanctuary wisdom, not the naive assumption the psalmist held throughout. The psalm is structured as a retrospective: this is what I know now, having been through the crisis.
73.2: 'But as for me, my feet nearly slipped; my steps almost gave way' — the psalmist's near-apostasy: the 'slipping' and 'almost giving way' are the metaphors of losing footing on the covenant path. The 'slippery ground' (v. 18) on which the wicked stand becomes the ground on which the psalmist himself nearly slipped — the crisis was interior, not external. The psalm admits that the observation of the wicked's prosperity nearly destroyed the psalmist's own covenant faith.
73.3: 'For I was envious of the arrogant when I saw how the wicked prosper' — the theodicy problem stated plainly: seeing the wicked's visible, undeniable prosperity produces envy and the corresponding question about covenant justice. 'Arrogant' (holalim, boasters/fools) are those who live without reference to YHWH — the 'fool' (naval) of Ps 14:1 who says there is no God. The problem is not abstract but visceral: the psalmist saw the wicked prospering and found in this a specific challenge to the covenant premise that righteousness is rewarded.
73.4: 'For they have no anguish in their death; their bodies are sleek and strong' — the specific evidence: the wicked die without suffering. 'Sleek and strong' (bari ulam, fat and healthy) — the well-fed, physically robust condition of the prosperous wicked is the visible sign of their ease. In the ancient world, physical robustness was a sign of divine favor; the wicked's gleaming health contradicts the expected covenant correlation between wickedness and physical decline.
73.5: 'They are not in trouble like other people; they are not plagued like the rest of mankind' — the wicked's exemption from common human suffering: they do not share in the general troubles or in the plagues of humanity. This exemption from suffering is what makes their prosperity so theologically troubling: they lack not only punishment but even the normal burdens of human existence. They seem to operate under different rules.
73.6: 'Therefore pride adorns them like a necklace; violence wraps them like a garment' — the visual metaphor of pride and violence as clothing: in the ancient world, jewelry and fine garments were signs of wealth and status. The wicked wear pride as their ornament and violence as their robe — their social presentation is made of the very sins that should mark them for judgment. Instead of covenant virtues adorning them, their wickedness has become their badge of honor.
73.7: 'Their eyes bulge with prosperity; the desires of their heart exceed all bounds' — 'their eyes bulge from fat' is a satirical hyperbole of excessive wealth. In the ancient Near East, visible prosperity (full body, bright eyes, healthy skin) was a recognized sign of well-being. 'The desires of their heart exceed all bounds' — the imagination of the heart overflows; their desires cannot be contained by any moral or social limit.
73.8: 'They scoff and speak with malice; they talk of oppression with arrogance' — the social dimension of the wicked's prosperity: their speech is scoffing, malicious, and openly oppressive. The combination of prosperity and social dominance: they can say openly what others must whisper, and suffer no consequences. Their impunity in speech reflects their impunity in action.
73.9: 'They direct their mouths against the heavens, and their tongue ranges across the earth' — the spatial claim of the wicked's speech: from heaven to earth, their tongue sweeps across the entire created order. 'Mouths against the heavens' — speech directed upward as a challenge to divine authority (cf. Rev 13:6: the beast 'opened its mouth to blaspheme God'). The wicked's speech claims universal authority by challenging both the divine sphere (above) and the human sphere (below).
73.10: 'Therefore people keep returning to them, and a full cup of water is drained by them' — the social magnetism of power: people are attracted to the wicked precisely because of their prosperity and social dominance. The surrounding people empty themselves in service to the powerful. This is the patron-client dynamic at its most exploitative: the powerful attract clients who drain themselves in service, while the powerful accumulate further wealth and status.
73.11: 'They say, How can God know? Does the Most High have knowledge?' — the practical atheism of the prospering wicked: not denial of divine existence but denial of divine moral attention. If YHWH does not see or does not care about their conduct, the covenant framework of accountability is null. This is the same challenge as Ps 10:11-13 and Job 22:12-14 (Eliphaz's accusation that Job assumed God could not see through the clouds). The question is theological: does the covenant God actually monitor human conduct and respond to it?
73.12: 'See — these are the wicked, always at ease, growing in wealth' — the summary of the evidence: the wicked are 'at ease' (shalvei olam, at peace always) and 'growing in wealth.' The psalmist has made an empirical observation that challenges the Deuteronomic theology of covenant consequences. The observation is accurate; the theological problem it raises is genuine. The psalmist does not deny the evidence — he is wrestling with it honestly.
73.13: 'Surely I have kept my heart clean for nothing, and washed my hands in innocence in vain' — the crisis of covenant meaning: if the wicked prosper without consequence, covenant faithfulness (keeping the heart clean) and ritual purity (washing the hands — the declaration of innocence, as in Ps 26:6; Deut 21:6-7) are pointless. 'For nothing' (riq, in vain/empty) is the wisdom vocabulary of futility (Eccl 1:2: havel havalim, vanity of vanities). The psalmist is applying the vanity verdict specifically to the covenant life of righteousness.
73.14: 'For all day long I have been afflicted, and punishment comes every morning' — the personal experience that contrasts with the wicked's ease (v. 12: 'always at ease'): the psalmist is 'afflicted' (nagua, stricken — the same word used for leprosy in Lev 13 and for the Suffering Servant in Isa 53:4) 'all day long.' 'Punishment every morning' — while the wicked sleep soundly, each morning brings the psalmist fresh chastisement. The asymmetry is the pastoral and theological crisis.
73.15: 'If I had said, I will speak this way, I would have betrayed the generation of your children' — the check on apostasy: the psalmist did not speak the crisis aloud because doing so would have 'betrayed' the covenant community. The social responsibility of covenant speech: speaking the crisis theology (righteousness is pointless) publicly would have undermined the faith of those whose covenant trust depended on the community's collective testimony. The psalmist's internal struggle remained private precisely out of covenant loyalty to the community.
73.16: 'When I tried to make sense of all this, it was a torment before my eyes' — 'I tried to make sense of it' — the rational-theological effort to resolve the theodicy problem through reflection alone. It failed: the attempt produced 'torment' (amal, hard labor/misery) rather than resolution. The theodicy problem is not solvable by intellectual effort alone — the psalmist's wisdom categories cannot absorb the evidence he has observed. This is the limit of human wisdom confronted in Job and here.
73.17: 'Until I entered the sanctuary of God; then I grasped their end' — the resolution comes in the sanctuary (miqdeshei-el, the holy places of God). Not through additional reasoning but through the experience of the divine presence in worship. The sanctuary context shifts the psalmist's perspective: from the immanent time-frame (present prosperity of the wicked) to the transcendent time-frame (their final destiny). 'I grasped their end' — eschatological perspective on the wicked is only possible from within the space where divine reality is encountered: the temple, the sanctuary, the place of covenant worship.
73.18: 'Surely you have placed them on slippery ground; you cast them down to desolation' — the divine perspective on the wicked's position: what looks like solid prosperity is actually 'slippery ground' (chalaqot, smooth/slick places). The same word as 'nearly slipped' in v. 2: the psalmist who nearly slipped is actually on firmer ground than the wicked who seem secure. The wicked's ease is not a stable platform but a frictionless surface from which they will suddenly fall.
73.19: 'How suddenly they are destroyed, completely swept away by terrors!' — 'suddenly' — the speed of divine judgment on the wicked is the shocking reversal of their apparent permanence. 'Completely swept away by terrors' — the terrors that end the wicked are the same 'terrors' of death and judgment that characterized Sheol in ancient Israelite thought (Job 18:11, 14; 24:17: the 'terrors of death').
73.20: 'Like a dream that vanishes when one awakes, so when you arise, O Lord, you despise their fleeting form' — the dream image of the wicked's existence: vivid while it lasts, completely dissolved when morning comes. YHWH's 'arising' is the language of divine judicial intervention after apparent inaction (cf. Ps 44:23: 'Wake up! Why are you sleeping, O Lord?'). When YHWH acts in judgment, the wicked's substantial-seeming prosperity turns out to have been a dream-image with no lasting substance.
73.21: 'When my heart was embittered and I was pierced in my inner being' — the confession of the emotional and physical experience of the theodicy crisis. 'Embittered' (the verb used for sourdough leavening in Exod 12:19) and 'pierced in my inner being' (my kidneys were sharpened/pierced) — the kidneys/inner being was understood as the seat of deep emotion and conscience (cf. Job 16:13; Ps 16:7; 139:13). The crisis had penetrated to the deepest interior of the psalmist's emotional and moral life.
73.22: 'I was brutish and ignorant; I was no better than an animal before you' — the psalmist's self-assessment of his condition during the crisis: 'brutish (ba'ar, stupid/animal-like) and ignorant.' The animal comparison (behemot hayiti imach, I was like an animal before you) is the sharpest self-condemnation: the person who reduced the covenant relationship to earthly accounting had descended to the level of creatures who have no covenant knowledge. The sanctuary experience (v. 17) revealed not just the wicked's end but also the psalmist's own blindness.
73.23: 'Yet I am always with you; you hold me by my right hand' — the pivot from self-condemnation (v. 22) to covenant affirmation. 'Yet I am always with you' — despite the crisis, the confusion, the near-apostasy, the covenant relationship remained intact from YHWH's side. 'You hold me by my right hand' — the guidance image of the stronger leading the weaker: the divine patron grasping the client's hand and preventing the fall that the psalmist described in v. 2. YHWH held him even when the psalmist did not know he was held.
73.24: 'You guide me with your counsel, and afterward you will receive me into glory' — 'you guide me with your counsel' — divine wisdom as the navigation system through the theodicy crisis; the same counsel the psalmist tried to find through his own reflection (v. 16) is available through covenant relationship. 'Afterward you will receive me into glory' (ve'achar kavod tiqacheni) — one of the most discussed verses in the Psalter: the 'receiving into glory' has been read as a this-worldly vindication, as the moment of death taken by YHWH (cf. Ps 49:15), or as anticipating resurrection. The language closely parallels Gen 5:24 (Enoch: 'God took him') and 2 Kgs 2:10 (Elijah's translation).
73.25: 'Whom in heaven do I have but you? And on earth there is nothing I desire besides you' — the comprehensive declaration of covenant exclusivity: in heaven and on earth, YHWH alone is desired. The double negation covers the entire cosmos: no heavenly being (no member of the divine council, no angel, no idol) and no earthly thing can substitute for YHWH as the object of desire and trust. This is the Shema's implication worked out psychologically: if YHWH is one, then all other objects of desire are relativized. The psalmist has arrived at the affective dimension of monotheism.
73.26: 'My flesh and my heart may fail, but God is the strength of my heart and my portion forever' — 'my flesh and my heart may fail' — the physical-emotional exhaustion of the crisis: the complete depletion of natural resources. But YHWH remains 'the strength of my heart (tsur-levavi, the rock of my heart) and my portion (chelqi) forever.' 'My portion' (cheleq) is the inheritance language of the covenant land allotment: YHWH himself is the psalmist's share, as YHWH was the Levites' portion (Num 18:20: 'I am your portion and your inheritance among the Israelites').
73.27: 'For behold, those who are far from you will perish; you destroy all who are unfaithful to you' — the complement of v. 25's positive declaration: those who are not in the covenant relationship (far from YHWH) perish. Being 'far' is the opposite of 'being near' in v. 28. 'Unfaithful' (zonim, those who commit prostitution/whoredom) — the covenant-betrayal vocabulary of Hosea and Ezekiel: idolatry as sexual unfaithfulness to the covenant spouse. Those who abandon YHWH for other loyalties are destroyed.
73.28: 'But as for me, it is good to be near God; I have made the Lord GOD my refuge, that I may declare all your works' — the psalm closes with the psalmist's personal covenant declaration, echoing the wisdom principle of Prov 18:10: 'The name of the LORD is a strong tower; the righteous person runs to it and is safe.' 'It is good to be near God' — the summary wisdom of the entire journey: after the theodicy crisis, the sanctuary encounter, and the restored perspective, the simplest and most profound conclusion is that proximity to YHWH is the supreme good. The declaration of YHWH's works continues the mission of testimony that characterized Ps 71's aged psalmist.

74.1: Psalm 74 is a national lament over the destruction of the temple — most likely composed in response to the Babylonian destruction of 586 BCE (cf. vv. 3-8: sanctuary defiled and burned, carved paneling smashed, meeting places burned throughout the land). It is attributed to Asaph (or the Asaphite guild) and forms part of the Asaph collection (Ps 73-83). 'O God, why have you rejected us forever? Why does your anger smolder against the sheep of your pasture?' — the central question: divine rejection that appears permanent (lanetsach, forever). The 'sheep of your pasture' is the covenant image of YHWH as shepherd and Israel as his flock (Ps 79:13; 95:7; 100:3) — the flock he now seems to have abandoned to destruction.
74.2: 'Remember your congregation, which you acquired long ago, the tribe of your inheritance, which you redeemed — Mount Zion, where you dwelt' — 'remember' (zekor) — the covenant appeal to divine memory as the activator of divine action. YHWH's memory of the covenant is the theological engine of Israel's restoration in Exodus (Exod 2:24: 'God remembered his covenant with Abraham'), exile, and return (Lev 26:42, 45). 'Acquired long ago' (qanita qedem) — the Exodus generation as YHWH's purchased people (Exod 15:16). 'Mount Zion, where you dwelt' — YHWH's chosen residence in Zion as the ultimate ground for the petition: restore the place you chose.
74.3: 'Make your way to the perpetual ruins; the enemy has wrought complete destruction in the sanctuary' — 'perpetual ruins' (mashu'ot netsach, ruins of eternity) — the temple site left as permanent wreckage. YHWH is implored to visit these ruins, to see their condition, to be moved to action. The enemy's 'complete destruction' is laid before YHWH as the evidence demanding his attention. The eyewitness detail of vv. 4-8 provides the specifics of this destruction.
74.4: 'Your enemies have roared in the midst of your assembly; they have set up their own banners as signs' — the enemy 'roaring' in the place of YHWH's assembly (bemo'adecha, in your meeting-place) is the profanation: the lion's roar in the sanctuary that should have been the place of YHWH's voice. 'Their own banners as signs' — the Babylonian military standards erected in the destroyed sanctuary, replacing the signs of YHWH's presence with the symbols of Babylonian conquest. The sacred space claimed by foreign military insignia is the ultimate desecration.
74.5: 'They were like men wielding axes to fell a dense forest' — the woodcutting imagery is both literal (they cut the cedar paneling and wood structures of the temple) and metaphorical (the temple as a great forest/garden being clear-cut). The comparison uses the image of the total, systematic destruction of a woodland — nothing is left standing. The temple's cedar panels (1 Kgs 6:9-18: cedar from Lebanon lining the inner sanctuary) made the comparison especially apt.
74.6: 'All its carved paneling they smashed apart with axes and hammers' — the specific violence against the temple's decoration. The 'carved paneling' (pittuchim) of the temple — the carved cherubim, palm trees, and open flowers that 1 Kgs 6:29-35 describes adorning the inner sanctuary — were systematically destroyed with axes and hammers. The detail of ax-and-hammer destruction gives the eyewitness specificity that marks this as composed by someone who witnessed or had detailed reports of the 586 BCE destruction.
74.7: 'They set your sanctuary on fire; they defiled the dwelling place of your name by pulling it down to the ground' — the burning of the sanctuary (mishekan shemecha, the dwelling of your name) is the act that extinguished YHWH's official presence in Israel: the divine name's dwelling destroyed. The temple was not merely a building but the place where YHWH's shem (name = his personal presence and authority) was resident (Deut 12:11; 1 Kgs 8:29: 'I have set my name there'). Its destruction left YHWH technically without an earthly residence — a theological crisis without precedent since the original establishment of the tabernacle.
74.8: 'They said in their hearts, We will crush them all together; they burned down every meeting place of God throughout the land' — the systematic destruction beyond the central sanctuary: the burning of 'all the meeting places of God in the land.' If this refers to pre-exilic synagogue-like gathering places (mo'adei-el), it is the earliest possible reference to local worship assemblies in Israel, suggesting that corporate worship had developed at local sites before the exile, not only in Jerusalem. The total destruction of all worship sites was the comprehensive religious suppression of the Babylonian campaign.
74.9: 'We see none of our signs; there is no longer a prophet; and none of us knows how long this will last' — the three absences that define the exile's theological crisis: (1) no signs (ototeinu) — no visible tokens of YHWH's presence and action; (2) no prophet — no one speaking YHWH's authoritative word (cf. 1 Sam 3:1: 'The word of the LORD was rare in those days; there was no frequent vision'); (3) no knowledge of duration ('none of us knows how long'). The exile silenced the prophetic voice and removed all signs of divine presence simultaneously — a total communication blackout from YHWH.
74.10: 'How long, O God, will the enemy mock you? Is the adversary to revile your name forever?' — the ad-matai ('how long?') question that characterizes the lament genre (Ps 13:1-2; 79:5; 80:4; 89:46; Hab 1:2). The lament moves from 'why?' (vv. 1, 11) to 'how long?' — from the question of causation to the question of duration. The enemies' mocking of YHWH and reviling of his name is both an offense against Israel and an offense against YHWH himself — the basis for the petition that YHWH act for his own name's sake.
74.11: 'Why do you hold back your hand, your right hand? Draw it out from under your garment and act!' — the provocative petition: YHWH's right hand — the divine warrior's instrument of victory — is hidden 'in your bosom' (from within your garment-fold). The image of the concealed weapon: the divine warrior has a powerful hand but has not drawn it. The boldness of the petition (essentially telling YHWH to stop hiding his hand) is characteristic of the lament genre's honesty before the covenant God.
74.12: 'Yet God my King is from of old, bringing salvation in the midst of the earth' — the pivot from petition to hymnic declaration. 'From of old' (mikedem) — YHWH's ancient track record of salvation: the Exodus, the Conquest, the period of the judges and kings. 'Bringing salvation in the midst of the earth' — YHWH as the active agent of historical rescue. The hymn section (vv. 12-17) recites YHWH's creative and historical acts as the grounds for the petition of vv. 18-23: if he has done these things before, he can act now.
74.13: 'You split the sea apart by your power; you shattered the heads of the sea monsters on the waters' — the creation/Exodus imagery of YHWH's victory over the primordial waters. 'The sea monsters' (tanninim, sea dragons) represent both the literal sea creatures of chaos and the mythological chaos figures of ANE cosmogony (Babylonian Tiamat, Ugaritic Yam). YHWH's splitting of the sea evokes both the Red Sea crossing (Exod 14:21) and the primordial creation division of the waters (Gen 1:6-7). The Psalm uses creation and Exodus as one theological event: YHWH's mastery over chaos waters.
74.14: 'You crushed the many heads of Leviathan; you gave his carcass as food for the creatures of the wilderness' — Leviathan was the many-headed sea dragon of Canaanite mythology (the Ugaritic Baal cycle describes Baal killing the seven-headed serpent Lotan/Leviathan). Ps 74:14 appropriates this mythological figure for YHWH's sovereign power: YHWH defeated even the chaos monster that Baal struggled against. 'Giving his carcass as food for wilderness creatures' — the complete desacralization of the chaos monster: far from being the great enemy, Leviathan becomes food for desert animals. The chaos powers are reduced to garbage.
74.15: 'You split open springs and streams; you dried up the ever-flowing rivers' — the two sides of YHWH's power over water: creating water sources (splitting open springs = the provision in the wilderness, Exod 17:6; Num 20:11) and destroying water sources (drying up rivers = the Jordan crossing, Josh 3:14-17, and the Red Sea crossing). YHWH commands water in both directions: he can produce it from rock and stop it in its course. The same God who controlled the primordial sea (v. 13) controls the terrestrial water systems.
74.16: 'The day belongs to you; the night also is yours; you established the light and the sun' — the creation hymn continues: YHWH's ownership of time (day and night) as well as space (earth and waters). In the ANE, the sun (Shamash in Babylonia) was a major deity; here the sun is YHWH's creation and instrument. The psalm asserts YHWH's sovereignty over the natural order that other nations attributed to subordinate deities.
74.17: 'You have fixed all the boundaries of the earth; you have made both summer and winter' — the seasonal order as YHWH's governance: summer and winter are both YHWH's creation, following the covenant promise of Gen 8:22 ('as long as the earth endures, seedtime and harvest, cold and heat, summer and winter, day and night will never cease'). The Psalm has moved from the chaos dragon (vv. 13-14) through the water systems (v. 15) through the luminaries (v. 16) to the seasonal order (v. 17): a comprehensive survey of creation's order under YHWH's sovereignty.
74.18: 'Remember this, O LORD: the enemy has mocked, and a foolish people has reviled your name' — the petition section resumes with 'remember' (zekor-zot) — the second covenant-memory appeal (cf. v. 2: 'remember your congregation'). The 'foolish people' (am naval) who reviles YHWH's name echoes Deut 32:21 where naval describes those without understanding. YHWH's own covenant song uses naval of Israel in rebellion; here it is applied to the enemy nations who mock YHWH.
74.19: 'Do not hand over your dove to the pack of enemies; do not forget the community of your poor forever' — 'your dove' (toratech, your turtledove) is an unusual metaphor for Israel: the vulnerable, gentle creature in the grip of predators. The dove as the pure animal of covenant sacrifice (Gen 8:8-12; Lev 1:14; 5:7) evokes Israel's sacrificial role and helplessness. 'The community of your poor' (adat aniyecha) — the covenant community reduced to poverty by the exile and enemy occupation, whom YHWH must not forget.
74.20: 'Look to your covenant, for the dark corners of the land are filled with dens of violence' — the explicit covenant appeal: look at what was promised, look at what is happening, and act on the contradiction. 'Dark corners (machashakkei-arets, dark places of the land) filled with dens of violence' — the land of Israel's habitations are now places of violent occupation. The covenant land promised as a place of rest and abundance (Deut 12:9-10) is now filled with violence. The appeal to the covenant is the appeal to YHWH's own commitments made under oath.
74.21: 'Do not let the oppressed be turned away in shame; let the poor and needy praise your name' — the reversal prayer: instead of shame falling on the powerless, let them praise YHWH. The 'oppressed' (dal) and 'poor and needy' (evyon ve'aniy) praise YHWH when they are vindicated — their praise is contingent on YHWH's action. The silence of the poor (shamed) and the mocking of the enemies (vv. 10, 18) would leave YHWH without any worshipers in the land. The petition for the poor's vindication is simultaneously a petition for YHWH's own worship to be restored.
74.22: 'Rise up, O God, and defend your cause; remember how fools mock you every single day' — 'rise up' (qumah Elohim) — the military call to YHWH to stand up and fight, as in the ancient battle cry of Num 10:35: 'Rise up, O LORD, and may your enemies be scattered.' 'Defend your cause' (rivah rivecha, plead your own case) — YHWH as the plaintiff in his own cause: the enemies' mockery of YHWH is a legal offense against YHWH himself, and YHWH is the one with standing to press charges. The psalm calls YHWH to advocate for his own honor.
74.23: 'Do not forget the clamor of your enemies; the uproar of those who defy you rises without ceasing' — the closing petition: YHWH must not forget the enemies' persistent noise. 'The uproar of those who defy you rises without ceasing' (sha'on qamecha oleh tamid) — the enemies' defiance is continuous and escalating. Unlike Israel's prayer, which must keep pressing YHWH to remember, the enemies' defiance never stops. The final verse holds both elements: the persistent clamor of the enemy and the implicit plea that YHWH not allow this clamor to go unanswered indefinitely.

80.1: Ps 80: Asaph's Lament for the Northern Kingdom — 'Lilies of the Covenant.' The superscription 'Shoshannim Edut' (Lilies of the Covenant/Testimony) designates a tune also used in Ps 60. The Asaph psalm addresses YHWH specifically as the 'Shepherd of Israel' leading Joseph 'like a flock' — the northern tribal vocabulary (Joseph = Ephraim/Manasseh) suggests this psalm originates in or addresses the northern kingdom, possibly in the context of the Assyrian conquest of 722 BC. 'Give ear, O Shepherd of Israel' — ro'eh Yisrael (Shepherd of Israel) — the royal shepherd metaphor applied to YHWH: the divine king as the one who leads the covenant flock.
80.2: 'Before Ephraim and Benjamin and Manasseh, stir up your might and come to save us!' — the three tribes named are the Rachel tribes: Benjamin (Jacob and Rachel's youngest), Ephraim and Manasseh (Jacob's grandsons through Joseph and Rachel's descendant). These were the northern and central tribes most directly affected by Assyrian pressure. The ark-procession context may be implied: these tribes marched behind the ark in the wilderness (Num 2:18-24). The prayer 'stir up your might' (orerah et gevuratecha) is a summons for YHWH to awaken his dormant military power.
80.3: 'Restore us, O God; let your face shine, that we may be saved!' — the first occurrence of the three-part refrain that structures the psalm (vv3, 7, 19), with the divine title escalating: Elohim (God, v3) → Elohim Tzvaot (God of hosts, v7) → YHWH Elohim Tzvaot (LORD God of hosts, v19). 'Let your face shine' (haer panecha) — the Aaronic blessing language (Num 6:25) repurposed as national rescue plea. The shining face is the antithesis of the hiding face that has allowed the national disaster.
80.4: 'O LORD God of hosts, how long will you be angry with your people's prayers?' — the lament question 'how long?' (ad-matai) is the standard lament form (Pss 6:3; 13:1-2; 35:17; 74:10; 79:5; 90:13). 'Angry with your people's prayers' — an extraordinary statement: YHWH is depicted as angry even at the prayers offered to him. The smoke metaphor (aneshan) in some readings: as smoke rises from an altar, so the prayers rise but YHWH responds with anger rather than grace. The prayer itself has become an occasion of divine displeasure.
80.5: 'You have fed them with the bread of tears and given them tears to drink in full measure' — 'bread of tears' (lechem dimah) and 'tears to drink in full measure' (bashalishet) — the covenant curse vocabulary: in the covenant blessings Israel would eat bread in plenty; in the curse, they eat grief. The measurement term (shalish, a third part or a large measure) indicates the quantity of grief is regulated — it is not random suffering but measured covenant discipline. Lam 3:15 similarly uses the 'bread of bitterness' image for the exile.
80.6: 'You make us an object of contention for our neighbors, and our enemies laugh among themselves' — the shame-dimension of the national disaster. 'Object of contention' (madon, strife) — Israel has become the source of mockery and dispute among neighboring nations. The enemies' laughter (yilagu) is the vindication of the enemy and the humiliation of YHWH's people: if YHWH cannot protect Israel, the nations conclude that YHWH himself has failed. The theological problem of national disaster is not only Israel's suffering but YHWH's reputation.
80.7: 'Restore us, O God of hosts; let your face shine, that we may be saved!' — second refrain, intensified to 'God of hosts' (Elohim Tzvaot). The divine title Tzvaot (hosts/armies) invokes YHWH's military capacity: the God of the heavenly armies is being petitioned by the earthly people who have lost their earthly battle. The refrain's repetition creates liturgical insistence: not satisfied by silence.
80.8: 'You brought a vine out of Egypt; you drove out the nations and planted it' — the vine allegory (vv8-16) is the theological heart of the psalm. Israel as YHWH's vine is a sustained metaphor in OT prophecy (Isa 5:1-7: the Song of the Vineyard; Jer 2:21; Ezek 15; 17; John 15). 'Brought out of Egypt' — the Exodus as the vine's transplanting: YHWH uprooted the vine from Egypt (slavery, Pharaoh's garden) and replanted it in Canaan. 'Drove out the nations' — the conquest as land-clearing for the vine.
80.9: 'You cleared the ground for it; it took deep root and filled the land' — the vineyard preparation: clearing (pinnita, clearing of rocks and underbrush), deep rooting (sheresh), and filling the land. The growth of Israel in Canaan under Joshua-to-Solomon is the vine's flourishing period. ANE vineyards required intensive preparation — the metaphor captures both divine labor and Israel's dependence on that labor.
80.10: 'The mountains were covered with its shade, the mighty cedars with its branches' — the vine's expansion at its height: covering the mountains and the cedars of Lebanon. This is the Davidic-Solomonic empire at its greatest extent: from the Sinai wilderness to the Euphrates (1 Kgs 4:21). The cedars of Lebanon (Baal's territory in ANE mythology — cf. Ps 29:5) are now shaded by Israel's vine — YHWH's people have overtaken even the domain of the rival gods.
80.11: 'It sent out its branches to the sea, and its shoots to the River' — 'the Sea' (yam) = the Mediterranean; 'the River' (nahar) = the Euphrates. The maximal extent of Davidic-Solomonic sovereignty. This is the fulfillment of the Abrahamic land promise (Gen 15:18: 'from the river of Egypt to the great river, the Euphrates') and the Mosaic promise (Deut 11:24). The vine has filled the promised territory.
80.12: 'Why then have you broken down its walls, so that all who pass along the way pluck its fruit?' — the reversal: the vine that YHWH planted and protected (vv8-11) has had its walls broken down. The vineyard wall (geder) was the essential protection of an ANE vineyard — without it, animals and passersby could freely plunder. YHWH as the one who broke down the wall is the most disturbing image: the divine vinedresser has become the destroyer of his own vineyard. The same image in Isa 5:5 (YHWH breaking down the vineyard wall as judgment).
80.13: 'The boar from the forest ravages it, and all that move in the field feed on it' — the wild boar (chazir miyaar) from the forest is the ANE symbol of destructive, unclean, violent plunder. The pig was ritually unclean for Israel (Lev 11:7); its ravaging of the vineyard is doubly defiling — the unclean animal destroying the sacred vine. The Assyrian army (or any subsequent oppressor) functions as the boar: brutal, unclean, unstoppable. 'All that move in the field feed on it' — general pillaging following the major devastation.
80.14: 'Turn again, O God of hosts! Look down from heaven, and see; have regard for this vine' — the pivot to petition: shuv-na Elohim Tzvaot (return, please, O God of hosts). The 'return' (shuv) is the repentance/return vocabulary applied to YHWH: just as Israel must return (shuv) to YHWH, YHWH is asked to return his attention to the vine. 'Look down from heaven' — the divine descent from the heavenly viewpoint to see the damage. The vine now identified explicitly: 'have regard for this vine' (vephaqed gefen zot) — the paqad (visitation/oversight) of YHWH invoked for restoration rather than judgment.
80.15: '...the stock that your right hand planted, and for the son whom you made strong for yourself' — 'the son' (ben) — either the nation (Israel as YHWH's son: Exod 4:22; Hos 11:1) or the Davidic king (YHWH's son: Ps 2:7; 2 Sam 7:14). The ambiguity is productive: the vine-nation and its royal representative both claim the designation 'son.' YHWH's 'right hand' (yamin) — the planting hand (cf. v17: 'the man of your right hand').
80.16: 'They have burned it with fire; they have cut it down; may they perish at the rebuke of your face!' — the devastation described at its worst: fire and cutting down. ANE conquerors routinely destroyed vineyards and orchards as part of scorched-earth policy (Deut 20:19-20 restricts this for Israel's army). 'The rebuke of your face' (gaar panecha) — the same divine rebuke that drove the sea back (Ps 104:7; cf. Ps 76:6) invoked for the enemy's destruction.
80.17: 'But let your hand be on the man of your right hand, the son of man whom you have made strong for yourself!' — 'the man of your right hand' (ish yeminecha) and 'the son of man' (ben adam) — the Davidic king as YHWH's appointed human representative. The phrase is unique: 'man of your right hand' positions the king as standing at YHWH's right hand (cf. Ps 110:1). The 'son of man' designation, while here simply meaning 'human being,' feeds into the developing 'Son of Man' trajectory of Dan 7:13 and the NT.
80.18: 'Then we shall not turn back from you; give us life, and we will call upon your name!' — the covenant vow: if YHWH restores, Israel will not again turn away (lo-nasog mimecha). The vow of not turning back is the anti-apostasy pledge — a recognition that the disaster arose from Israel's turning away. 'Give us life' (vechayyenu) — the divine life-giving that makes covenant loyalty possible: life is the prerequisite for calling on the divine name.
80.19: 'Restore us, O LORD God of hosts! Let your face shine, that we may be saved!' — the third and fullest refrain: YHWH Elohim Tzvaot — the complete divine title. The escalation from Elohim (v3) to Elohim Tzvaot (v7) to YHWH Elohim Tzvaot (v19) culminates in the covenant name. The final plea is grounded in the covenant name: YHWH — the God who revealed himself as 'I AM WHO I AM' at the burning bush (Exod 3:14), who is the same God of the Exodus vine-transplanting — is asked to restore what his own name has promised.

81.1: Ps 81: Asaph's Festival Psalm — 'Gittith.' 'Gittith' (al hagittit) is a musical term of uncertain origin: possibly connected to Gath (a Philistine lute), to the wine-press (gat = wine vat, suggesting an autumn harvest festival), or to a place or melody name. The psalm divides into two parts: a joyful call to festival worship (vv1-5b) and a divine oracle reviewing the covenant history and lamenting Israel's unfaithfulness (vv5c-16). The transition from human voice to divine voice (v5c: 'I heard a voice I had not known') is one of the most dramatic shifts in the Psalter. The psalm may have been used at the Festival of Tabernacles (Sukkot) or at the New Year (Rosh Hashanah), when the trumpet blast of v3 would have been liturgically fitting.
81.2: 'Raise a song; sound the tambourine, the sweet lyre with the harp' — the festival instrumentation: toph (tambourine/hand drum, associated with Miriam's celebration at the sea, Exod 15:20), kinnor naim (sweet/beautiful lyre), and nevel (harp). The plurality of instruments signals a full temple orchestra. 'Raise a song' (seu zimrah) — the imperative of festive praise: the community is summoned to collective musical performance.
81.3: 'Blow the trumpet at the new moon, at the full moon, on our feast day' — tiqeu vachodesh shofar (blow the ram's horn at the new moon). The shofar (ram's horn) was blown at new moons (Num 10:10) and at the Feast of Trumpets (Rosh Hashanah, the seventh month's new moon: Lev 23:24; Num 29:1). The 'full moon' (bakeseh, at the covering/full moon) may refer to Passover (first month, 15th day — full moon) or Tabernacles (seventh month, 15th day — also full moon). The dual reference could encompass both spring and fall festivals, or specifically Tabernacles (Sukkot) when the moon is full and the harvest complete.
81.4: 'For it is a statute for Israel, a rule of the God of Jacob' — ki choq leYisrael hu mishpat le-Elohei Yaakov — the festival is not voluntary but covenantally mandated: a choq (statute, the engraved/fixed law) and a mishpat (ordinance, the legal ruling). The festival calendar was a covenant obligation, not a cultural tradition. YHWH's claim over time — over the calendar — is an expression of his sovereignty over the whole of life.
81.5: 'He made it a decree in Joseph when he went out over the land of Egypt. I heard a language I had not known' — the transition verse. 'Over the land of Egypt' — the Passover statute was established when YHWH 'went out' over Egypt (Exod 12:12-13: 'I will pass through the land of Egypt that night'). Then: 'I heard a language I had not known' (sephath lo-yadati eshma) — the psalm's voice suddenly shifts: someone hears an unfamiliar language. Most commentators take this as the beginning of YHWH's divine oracle, where YHWH speaks in first person for the remainder of the psalm. The 'unfamiliar language' may be the divine speech itself — the experience of receiving the word of YHWH.
81.6: 'I relieved your shoulder of the burden; your hands were freed from the basket' — YHWH speaks in first person, recounting the Exodus. 'The burden on the shoulder' (misevel shichmo) and 'the basket' (dudim) — the specific labor of Egyptian slavery: carrying heavy loads on the shoulders and working clay in brick-making baskets. Exod 1:11-14 describes exactly this labor. The physical liberation from literal burden is the Exodus in miniature.
81.7: 'In distress you called, and I delivered you; I answered you in the secret place of thunder; I tested you at the waters of Meribah' — the divine first-person recital of the Exodus events: (1) the cry from Egypt (ba-tzarah qarata, you called in distress) and the divine response (Exod 3:7-9: YHWH heard the cry); (2) the divine response 'in the secret place of thunder' (beseter raam) — the Sinai theophany of Exod 19-20; (3) the testing at Meribah (Exod 17:1-7; Num 20:1-13) — the water-from-rock incident where Israel tested YHWH.
81.8: 'Hear, O my people, while I admonish you! O Israel, if you would but listen to me!' — the divine appeal (shema ammi vaaodicha, hear my people and I will warn/testify you) — the covenant lawsuit tone: YHWH as both the rescuing covenant partner and the plaintiff who demands a hearing. 'If you would listen' (im-tishma li) — the conditional formulation: the covenant blessings are contingent on listening. This is the Deuteronomic refrain: Deut 6:3-4; 11:13-14; the Shema itself is the listening command.
81.9: 'There shall be no strange god among you; you shall not bow down to a foreign god' — the First Commandment restated (Exod 20:3): 'no other gods before me.' 'Strange god' (el zar) and 'foreign god' (el nekar) — both terms for the rival deities of surrounding nations. The prohibition is covenantal: YHWH has an exclusive claim on Israel's allegiance. The covenant violation being diagnosed in the divine speech is precisely this idolatry.
81.10: 'I am the LORD your God, who brought you up out of the land of Egypt. Open your mouth wide, and I will fill it' — the Decalogue self-identification (Exod 20:2: 'I am the LORD your God who brought you out of the land of Egypt') followed by a remarkable promise: 'Open your mouth wide, and I will fill it' (harkhev picha vaemale'ehu). The open mouth as the posture of requesting: the covenant God offers unlimited provision to the one who asks with full expectancy. The metaphor may be from the feeding of young birds (Ps 147:9) or from the royal court where the king fills the open mouths of petitioners.
81.11: 'But my people did not listen to my voice; Israel would not submit to me' — the divine lament: lo-shama ammi leqoli (my people did not listen to my voice). The contrast with v8's appeal ('if you would listen') and v10's promise is stark: the invitation was refused. 'Would not submit' (lo-ava, Qal of avah, to be willing, to consent) — Israel refused to consent to YHWH's sovereignty. The ANE treaty violation: the vassal refuses the suzerain's terms.
81.12: 'So I gave them over to their stubborn hearts, to follow their own counsels' — ashalcheihu bishrerut libbam (I sent them away in the stubbornness of their heart). 'Gave them over' — the divine abandonment to consequence: YHWH does not destroy but withdraws, leaving Israel to the path it chose. Paul uses identical logic in Rom 1:24-28 (God 'gave them over'). The 'stubborn heart' (sherirut lev) — the Jeremiah vocabulary for Israel's unreformed heart (Jer 3:17; 7:24; 9:14; 11:8; 13:10; 18:12; 23:17).
81.13: 'Oh, that my people would listen to me, that Israel would walk in my ways!' — lu ammi shomea li (if only my people would listen to me) — the divine yearning in the 'if only' conditional (lu). This is the closest YHWH comes to lament: a mourning over unrealized possibility. The conditional shows that YHWH does not coerce: the doors of covenant blessing are open, contingent on Israel's listening. The divine grief over refused grace anticipates Isa 65:2 ('I held out my hands all day to a rebellious people') and Matt 23:37 ('How often I would have gathered your children...').
81.14: 'I would soon subdue their enemies and turn my hand against their foes' — the conditional promise: had Israel listened, YHWH would have defeated the enemies. The 'quickly' (kimat) of divine victory contrasts with the prolonged suffering of disobedience. The ease of divine military victory makes the current distress all the more tragic: the battles that Israel has fought and lost, YHWH would have won 'quickly.'
81.15: 'Those who hate the LORD would cringe before him, and their fate would last forever' — 'those who hate the LORD' (mesane YHWH) would become false flatterers (yekhachashelu lo, would dissemble/cringe before him) — the enemy's posture of enforced submission. 'Their fate would last forever' (veyihu itam leolam) — the submission would be permanent: no escaping the covenant victory of YHWH's people.
81.16: 'But he would feed you with the finest of the wheat, and with honey from the rock I would satisfy you' — the covenant abundance promised: 'finest wheat' (chelev chittah, the fat/best of wheat) and 'honey from the rock' (dvash misela). These are the Canaan-plenty images: the land flowing with milk and honey (Exod 3:8; Deut 8:8). 'Honey from the rock' appears in Deut 32:13 (Moses' song: 'he made him suck honey out of the rock') — the paradox of sweetness from hardness, abundance from the most barren source. The divine provision transcends even physical impossibility.

82.1: Ps 82: The Divine Council Psalm — the Most Theologically Significant Asaph Psalm. Ps 82 is one of the most theologically charged and debated texts in the OT. It depicts YHWH presiding over the adat El (assembly/council of El/God) — the divine council of the ANE, which in Ugaritic texts is El's court of divine beings. The three main interpretations: (1) YHWH judging the pagan gods of the nations (the ANE polytheistic framework being absorbed and transcended); (2) YHWH judging corrupt human judges who have been called 'gods' by virtue of their divine-representative role (cf. Exod 21:6; 22:8-9, 28; John 10:34-35); (3) YHWH judging angelic beings entrusted with governance of the nations (Deut 32:8: 'according to the number of the sons of God'; cf. Dan 10). Jesus quotes v6 in John 10:34-35 in the context of the charge that claiming divine sonship is blasphemy.
82.2: 'How long will you judge unjustly and show partiality to the wicked?' — the divine indictment of the council members. Whether divine beings or human judges, the charge is the same: unjust judgment and partiality toward the wicked. In the ANE, the divine council was supposed to ensure cosmic justice; corrupt council-members meant a cosmos in moral disorder. The failure of justice in the created order is traced to corruption at the governing level.
82.3: 'Give justice to the weak and the fatherless; maintain the right of the afflicted and the destitute' — the divine council's mandate: to protect the vulnerable. In ancient Israel's legal system, the 'elders at the gate' were responsible for legal protection of the widow, orphan, and sojourner (Exod 22:22-24; Deut 10:18; 27:19). The divine council's members share this mandate at the cosmic level. Their failure to enforce it represents a catastrophic failure of the created moral order.
82.4: 'Rescue the weak and the needy; deliver them from the hand of the wicked' — the rescue mandate: not merely legal adjudication but active deliverance. The weak (dal), needy (evyon), and those in the hand of the wicked (rasha) are the triple-protected class of covenant law. YHWH's own self-description includes protection of these groups (Deut 10:17-18: 'the LORD your God... executes justice for the fatherless and the widow, and loves the sojourner'); the council-members are supposed to extend this protection throughout creation.
82.5: 'They have neither knowledge nor understanding, they walk about in darkness; all the foundations of the earth are shaken' — the diagnosis of the council's failure: lo yadu velo yavinnu (they do not know and they do not understand) — moral and intellectual blindness. 'Walk about in darkness' (bachashechah yithallachu) — the darkness of moral disorder. 'All the foundations of the earth are shaken' (yimotu kol-mosdei aretz) — the cosmic consequences of judicial corruption: when those who govern the creation fail, the physical creation itself becomes unstable. Moral disorder has cosmological effects.
82.6: 'I said, You are gods, sons of the Most High, all of you' — ani amarti elohim atem uvene elyon kulchem — the divine declaration of the council-members' status: they are elohim (gods/divine beings) and bene elyon (sons of the Most High). This was their appointed dignity. Jesus quotes this verse in John 10:34-35 to argue: if the OT calls those to whom the word of God came 'gods,' then claiming to be the Son of God is not blasphemy. The verse establishes a precedent for divine-representative status through divine appointment.
82.7: 'Nevertheless, like men you shall die, and fall like any prince' — the reversal of the dignity of v6: despite being called elohim, they will die ke'adam (like men/Adam). The mortality of the divine council members is the judgment for their moral failure. In the ANE, death was what distinguished mortals from the gods; here the gods are condemned to mortal death. 'Fall like any prince' (kachad hasarim tipolu) — their end is the same as the most powerful human ruler who has been deposed: they fall.
82.8: 'Arise, O God, judge the earth; for you shall inherit all the nations!' — the psalm closes with a prayer for YHWH to take direct sovereignty over all the nations, bypassing the corrupt council. 'Arise, O God' (qumah Elohim) — the divine arising to action. 'Judge the earth' (shophta haaretz) — YHWH himself exercising the justice that the council members failed to render. 'You shall inherit all the nations' (ki attah tinchal bekhol hagoyim) — the eschatological universalism: YHWH's inheritance is all peoples, not just Israel. This prayer forms the basis of the NT's universal mission and the eschatological judgment of all nations.

83.1:
Ps 83: Asaph's Pan-National Enemy Lament — Ten Nations Conspiring Against Israel. The final Asaph psalm (Pss 50, 73-83 form the Asaph collection). Ps 83 contains the longest enemy-list in the Psalter: ten nations or peoples conspiring to destroy Israel. The historical occasion is uncertain — some have connected it to Jehoshaphat's crisis (2 Chr 20: Moab, Ammon, and Meunites attacking), or to the post-exilic period, or to an eschatological scenario. The sheer breadth of the coalition (including Assyria, v8) suggests either a telescoping of multiple threats or a comprehensive future vision.

'O God, do not keep silence; do not hold your peace or be still, O God!' — the urgent summons for divine speech: three synonyms of divine silence (al-tachresh al-tishqot al-tishhot) as the thing to be avoided. Divine silence = divine inaction. The enemies are active and vocal (vv2-8); the problem is YHWH's apparent silence in contrast.
83.2: 'For behold, your enemies make an uproar; those who hate you have raised their heads' — the enemies are both YHWH's enemies (oyevecha) and Israel's: the equation is a covenant claim. 'Raised their heads' (naseu rosh) — the posture of self-assertion and aggression: the lifted head is the opposite of the bowed head of submission (cf. Ps 3:3: YHWH who lifts the psalmist's head against those who have raised their own).
83.3: 'They lay crafty plans against your people; they consult together against your treasured ones' — 'crafty plans' (ya'arimu sod, they scheme with cunning) — political conspiracy, the war council. 'Your treasured ones' (tzephunechah) — those whom YHWH has hidden/treasured (tzaphan, to hide safely). The enemies target precisely what YHWH has chosen to protect.
83.4: 'They say, Come, let us wipe them out as a nation; let the name of Israel be remembered no more!' — the explicit goal of the coalition: national annihilation (venachridem migoy, we will cut them off from being a nation) and name-erasure (velo yizakher shem Yisrael od, let the name of Israel be remembered no more). This is genocide: not merely defeat but the elimination of corporate memory. The nations want what Pharaoh attempted (Exod 1:8-22) — the elimination of the covenant people as a people.
83.5: 'For they conspire with one accord; against you they make a covenant' — the nations form a counter-covenant (berith) against YHWH and Israel. The anti-covenant coalition: using the covenant-language to organize opposition to the covenant people. In ANE diplomacy, defensive and offensive alliances were ratified by treaty-covenant; the nations have formed such a treaty against YHWH's covenant people.
83.6: 'The tents of Edom and the Ishmaelites, Moab and the Hagrites' — the enemy list begins. Edom (southern neighbor, Esau's descendants), Ishmaelites (descendants of Ishmael, son of Abraham and Hagar; northern Arabia), Moab (eastern neighbor, Lot's descendants), Hagrites (an Arab tribe related to Hagar).
83.7: 'Gebal and Ammon and Amalek, Philistia with the inhabitants of Tyre' — continued: Gebal (Phoenician city/Byblos, or the Edomite Gebal), Ammon (eastern neighbor, Lot's descendants), Amalek (the perpetual enemy from Exod 17; Num 24:20), Philistia (the coastal enemy of the judges and early monarchy period), Tyre (the major Phoenician trading city, sometimes ally but often hostile).
83.8: 'Assyria also has joined them; they are the strong arm of the children of Lot' — Assyria (Ashur) as the military superpower backing the coalition: the 'strong arm' (zeroa = arm/strength) of the smaller nations. 'Children of Lot' — both Moab and Ammon were Lot's descendants (Gen 19:37-38). Assyrian backing of smaller states against Israel/Judah is historically attested in the 8th century (Tig-lath-pileser III's campaigns). The inclusion of Assyria gives the coalition both local and imperial dimensions.
83.9: 'Do to them as you did to Midian, as to Sisera and Jabin at the river Kishon' — the historical precedents invoked: (1) Midian — Gideon's victory over the Midianite coalition in Judg 7-8; (2) Sisera — Deborah and Barak's victory over Sisera (Judg 4-5); (3) Jabin — the Canaanite king who commanded Sisera. These are the Judges-period military victories where YHWH routed vastly superior forces through unexpected means. The prayer asks for the pattern to repeat.
83.10: '...who were destroyed at En-dor, who became dung for the ground' — En-dor (near the Valley of Jezreel) was likely the site of one of the battles of Judg 4-5; Sisera's army was destroyed in this region. 'Became dung for the ground' (hayu domen la-adamah) — the most extreme form of ANE disgrace: unburied bodies decomposing into fertilizer. No burial = no honor, no rest, no ancestral memory. The enemies of YHWH become manure.
83.11: 'Make their nobles like Oreb and Zeeb, all their princes like Zebah and Zalmunna' — the Midianite leaders killed by Gideon: Oreb ('raven') and Zeeb ('wolf') were killed in Judg 7:25; Zebah ('sacrifice') and Zalmunna ('withdrawn shade/protection') were captured and killed by Gideon personally in Judg 8:5-21. These names were preserved as victory-trophies in Israel's memory. The prayer invokes specific named enemies as precedents for the current enemy leaders.
83.12: '...who said, Let us take possession for ourselves of the pastures of God' — the enemies' goal stated in their own words: to seize ne'ot Elohim (the pastures/habitations of God) — the land of Canaan that YHWH claims as his own estate. The land is YHWH's pasture (Lev 25:23: 'the land is mine; you are strangers and sojourners with me'); the enemies want to seize it. The economic motivation of empire — taking the best agricultural land — is expressed in theological terms.
83.13: 'O my God, make them like whirling dust, like chaff before the wind' — two dispersion images: galgal (tumbleweed/whirling wheel) — the dry thistle or wheel of chaff spinning across the open field before the wind. 'Chaff before the wind' (qash lifnei ruach) — the harvesting metaphor: threshed grain has the chaff blown away. Both images express complete scattering: the coalition's unity dissolved and each member blown in a different direction.
83.14: 'As fire consumes the forest, as the flame sets the mountains ablaze' — the fire-imagery of total destruction: forest fire as YHWH's weapon. ANE rulers used fire as an instrument of conquest (burning cities, forests, crops); Ps 83 invokes divine fire against the enemy coalition. The mountain fire corresponds to the theophanic fire of Sinai and the eschatological fire of judgment.
83.15: 'So may you pursue them with your tempest and terrify them with your hurricane!' — the storm-theophany as weapon: suphah (tempest/whirlwind) and searah (stormy wind). These are the same storm-forces that YHWH directed at the Egyptians at the sea (Exod 14:21) and that Ps 29 attributes to the divine storm-lord. The prayer: let YHWH deploy his cosmic storm against the national coalition.
83.16: 'Fill their faces with shame, that they may seek your name, O LORD' — the purpose of judgment: shame (boshet) leading to seeking (yevaqshu shimecha, they may seek your name). The divine judgment is not merely punitive but pedagogical: the enemies' shame is intended to drive them toward seeking YHWH. This anticipates the universal conversion vision of Isa 19:22 (Egypt struck and then healed) and the NT mission to the Gentiles.
83.17: 'Let them be put to shame and dismayed forever; let them perish in disgrace' — the harder prayer: if shame does not produce seeking (v16), then permanent disgrace. The two-track petition (v16 seeks conversion; v17 seeks judgment) reflects the OT covenant pattern: blessing for the obedient, curse for the persistent rebel. The 'forever' (leolam) of v17 is the eschatological permanence of irreversible judgment.
83.18: 'That they may know that you alone, whose name is the LORD, are the Most High over all the earth' — the final theological statement of the psalm: the goal of all the judgment is knowledge. Yeda'u ki attah levadechah shemechah YHWH elyon al-kol haaretz (that they may know that you alone, your name is YHWH, are Most High over all the earth). The nations should come to know (the Exodus-knowledge formula: 'that they may know I am the LORD') through the judgment what they refused to know through revelation. This is the most universalist verse of the Asaph collection.

84.1: Ps 84: Sons of Korah Pilgrim Psalm — One of the Psalter's Most Beloved Poems. Ps 84 is a pilgrimage psalm expressing longing for the Jerusalem temple and the joy of presence in YHWH's courts. The Sons of Korah guild (associated with the temple gatekeeping and music ministry: 1 Chr 9:19; 26:1-19) composed an ardent love-poem for the sanctuary they served. 'How lovely is your dwelling place, O LORD of hosts!' — ma yedidot mishkenotechah YHWH Tzvaot — the exclamatory ma (how!/how much!) expresses overwhelming aesthetic and spiritual delight. Mishkenotechah (your dwelling places, plural) — possibly the tabernacle precincts in their multiple areas, or the emphatic plural of the sanctuary's rich dimensions.
84.2: 'My soul longs, yes, faints for the courts of the LORD; my heart and flesh sing for joy to the living God' — nichsephah vegam kalethah naphshi (my soul longs, even faints with longing) — the vocabulary of intense desire: nichsephah (Niphal of kasaph, to long/yearn with physical ache) + kalethah (kalah, to be consumed, exhausted by longing, cf. Ps 119:81). Both soul (naphesh) and flesh (basar) sing — the whole person, interior and exterior, is engaged. 'The living God' (El chai) — not a static deity of idol-cult but the dynamically active God.
84.3: 'Even the sparrow finds a home, and the swallow a nest for herself, where she may lay her young, at your altars, O LORD of hosts, my King and my God' — one of the most charming images in the Psalter: birds nesting at the temple altars have what the pilgrim desperately longs for — permanent, unimpeded access to YHWH's presence. The sparrow (tzippor) and swallow (deror) build nests near the bronze altars of the temple courts. The birds are not conscious worshipers, but their habitual presence near the divine dwelling is the pilgrim's envy. 'My King and my God' (malki v-Elohai) — the personal covenant appropriation at the psalm's center.
84.4: 'Blessed are those who dwell in your house, ever singing your praise!' — ashre yoshve veitechah — the first of three beatitudes in the psalm (vv4, 5, 12). 'Those who dwell in your house' — the Levites and priests who had permanent access to the temple precincts. The pilgrim who visits occasionally envies those who can 'ever' (od, continually) be in the presence of YHWH. The temple servants have what the pilgrim travels far to experience briefly.
84.5: 'Blessed are those whose strength is in you, in whose heart are the highways to Zion' — the second beatitude: the blessed person has both their strength (oz) in YHWH and the pilgrim roads (mesillot) in their heart. 'Highways to Zion' (mesillot bilvavam) — the actual pilgrimage roads to Jerusalem are internalized in the pilgrim's heart: the longing to make the journey, the mental rehearsal of the route. The roads that lead to Zion run through the heart before they run through the landscape.
84.6: 'As they go through the Valley of Baca, they make it a spring; the early rain also covers it with pools' — the 'Valley of Baca' (emek habaka) — the word baka means 'weeping' or may be the name of a specific dry valley on the pilgrimage route to Jerusalem. In either reading, the transformation is miraculous: a valley of weeping or dryness becomes a spring (maayan) through the pilgrims' passage. Their tears become water; their longing transforms the landscape. 'The early rain covers it with pools' — the autumn rains that begin the new year make even the dry valley overflow. The pilgrimage journey transforms both the traveler and the terrain.
84.7: 'They go from strength to strength; each one appears before God in Zion' — yelkhu mechayil el-chayil (they go from strength to strength) — a unique phrase: the pilgrimage journey does not exhaust but strengthens. Each stage of the journey produces more capacity for the next. 'Each one appears before God in Zion' (yera'e el-Elohim betziyon) — the covenant obligation of the three pilgrimage festivals was 'to appear before the LORD' (Deut 16:16; Exod 23:17). The appearing (yera'eh) is the formal covenant audience with the divine king at his palace.
84.8: 'O LORD God of hosts, hear my prayer; give ear, O God of Jacob!' — the interlude petition: the pilgrim at the temple prays for YHWH's attention. The two divine titles: YHWH Elohim Tzvaot (LORD God of hosts — the most complete title) and Elohei Yaakov (God of Jacob — the covenant-patriarch title). The combination: cosmic power (Tzvaot) and covenant faithfulness (Yaakov) are both invoked.
84.9: 'Behold our shield, O God; look on the face of your anointed!' — maginenu (our shield) — YHWH as the covenant shield (Gen 15:1: 'I am your shield'). 'Your anointed' (meshichechah) — the Davidic king. The pilgrim's prayer for the king: YHWH's attention to the king's face (panim) is a request for divine favor. The pilgrimage psalm includes prayer for the royal patron through whom YHWH's blessings flow to the people.
84.10: 'For a day in your courts is better than a thousand elsewhere. I would rather be a doorkeeper in the house of my God than dwell in the tents of wickedness' — the famous comparison: one day (yom echad) in YHWH's courts > 1000 days (alaphim) anywhere else. 'Doorkeeper in the house of my God' (histopheph beveyt Elohai) — histopheph (to stand at the threshold/doorway, to hover at the entrance) — the most peripheral possible position inside the temple boundary is preferable to the most comfortable position in 'the tents of wickedness' (ahale resha). The contrast: covenant poverty at the temple edge vs. covenant-free prosperity.
84.11: 'For the LORD God is a sun and shield; the LORD bestows favor and honor. No good thing does he withhold from those who walk uprightly' — 'the LORD is a sun' (ki shemesh umagen YHWH Elohim) — uniquely in the OT, YHWH is called a shemesh (sun). Solar imagery was intensely politically charged in the ANE: sun-gods (Ra, Shamash, the Aten) were the supreme deities of Egypt and Mesopotamia. Israel's anti-solar-cult polemic (Deut 17:3; Ezek 8:16) made comparing YHWH to the sun rare. Here the comparison works: as the sun gives light and warmth to all creation, YHWH gives favor (chen) and honor (kavod) to those who walk uprightly.
84.12: 'O LORD of hosts, blessed is the one who trusts in you!' — the third beatitude closes the psalm: ashre adam boteach bach (blessed is the person who trusts in you). The pilgrimage psalm that opened with 'how lovely' and moved through longing, transformation, prayer, and comparison closes with the simplest possible covenant affirmation: the person who trusts YHWH is blessed. Batach (trust) is the covenant posture that organizes all the other elements of the psalm.

85.1:
Ps 85: Sons of Korah's Restoration Psalm — Post-Exilic Yearning. The opening verses (vv1-3) describe a past restoration in the perfect tense ('you showed favor,' 'you restored,' 'you forgave'), which most interpreters understand as the return from Babylonian exile (538 BC, under Cyrus's decree). Verses 4-7 then petition for a further restoration, suggesting the post-exilic community has returned but not fully experienced the promised renewal. The tension between 'you have restored' and 'restore us again' reflects the partially-realized eschatology of the post-exilic period: the return happened, but the full restoration described by Isaiah has not yet come.

'LORD, you were favorable to your land; you restored the fortunes of Jacob' — shavta shevit Yaakov (you turned the captivity/fortunes of Jacob) — the exile-return formula used in Jer 29:14; 30:3, 18; Ezek 39:25; Amos 9:14. The specificity 'your land' (artzecha) and 'Jacob' (Yaakov) — the covenant-patriarchal name — ground the restoration in the particular covenant relationship.
85.2: 'You forgave the iniquity of your people; you covered all their sin' — the forgiveness accompanying restoration: nasa avon amecha (you bore/lifted the iniquity of your people) and kissita kol-chattatam (you covered all their sin). The two forgiveness terms echo Ps 32:1-2: lifting the iniquity and covering the sin. The exile was understood as punishment for covenant sin (2 Chr 36:15-21); the restoration from exile was therefore an act of forgiveness, not merely geopolitical maneuvering.
85.3: 'You withdrew all your wrath; you turned from your hot anger' — asaphta kol-evratchah (you gathered in all your wrath) — the divine anger withdrawn and stored away. 'Turned from your hot anger' (shavta mecharon appechah) — the divine repentance/turning parallel to the human repentance required for restoration. In the covenantal pattern, Israel's repentance produces divine 'turning' from judgment (Jonah 3:9-10; Joel 2:13-14).
85.4: 'Restore us again, O God of our salvation, and put away your indignation toward us!' — the pivot to petition: despite the past restoration (vv1-3), the present situation still requires shuvenu (restore us, turn us, Qal imperative of shuv). The double meaning of shuv is crucial: restore us (bring us back from a difficult situation) and turn us (turn our hearts toward you). The same word applies to divine turning (v3: 'you turned from your anger') and human turning. Restoration and repentance are both expressed by shuv.
85.5: 'Will you be angry with us forever? Will you prolong your anger to all generations?' — the lament question (cf. Pss 79:5; 80:4) about the duration of divine anger. The post-exilic community's experience: the temple is rebuilt (under Zerubbabel/Haggai/Zechariah), but the full Isaianic restoration has not materialized. The Davidic throne is not restored; the nations have not flocked to Zion; the material poverty of the return is far from the abundance promised. 'Forever' and 'all generations' probe whether this partial restoration is all YHWH intends.
85.6: 'Will you not revive us again, that your people may rejoice in you?' — halo attah tashuv techayyenu (will you not again revive us) — the reviving (chayah in the Piel/Hiphil = give life, revive) is what the partial restoration requires: more than physical return but spiritual renewal. 'That your people may rejoice in you' (veyismach amecha bak) — joy is the test of true restoration: the returned community praying for the joy that proves the restoration is complete.
85.7: 'Show us your steadfast love, O LORD, and grant us your salvation' — the covenant petition: chesed (steadfast/covenant love) and yeshuah (salvation) — the twin pillars of the covenant promise. The 'showing' (areenu, show us, make visible) of chesed is the request that the invisible covenant love become experientially visible in the community's life. The salvation (yeshuah) is the yeshu'at YHWH (salvation of YHWH) that Israel's whole history has anticipated.
85.8: 'Let me hear what God the LORD will speak, for he will speak peace to his people, to his saints; but let them not turn back to folly' — the liturgical listening posture: the psalmist waits for the divine oracle. 'He will speak peace' (yedabber shalom) — the anticipated content of the divine word is shalom (wholeness/peace). This suggests a liturgical setting where a priest or prophet delivered an assurance-oracle (Heilsorakel) after the lament was offered. 'Let them not turn back to folly' (al-yashuvou lekesilah) — the warning embedded in the hope: divine peace cannot be claimed while returning to the covenant-foolishness that caused the exile.
85.9: 'Surely his salvation is near to those who fear him, that glory may dwell in our land' — the divine oracle received: salvation is qarov (near) for the fearers. 'That glory may dwell in our land' (lishkon kavod be-artzenu) — the anticipated Shekinah: the kavod (glory, divine radiant presence) that departed from the temple in Ezekiel's vision (Ezek 10:18; 11:23) will return and dwell (shakhan) in the land again. Ezek 43:1-5 describes the return of the kavod; Ps 85 prays for it.
85.10: 'Steadfast love and faithfulness meet; righteousness and peace kiss each other' — the most celebrated verse of the psalm, one of the most beautiful in the Psalter. Four covenant attributes personified: chesed (steadfast love) and emet (faithfulness/truth) meet (niphgashu); tzedek (righteousness) and shalom (peace) kiss (nashaquu). In the present age these attributes are in tension: the demands of righteousness and the gift of peace; the expectations of faithfulness and the need for love. The psalmist envisions a future moment when they converge. Medieval Christian commentators saw this as a prophecy of the incarnation: God's justice and mercy reconciled in Christ. The verse has served in Christian preaching as a summary of the atonement.
85.11: 'Faithfulness springs up from the earth, and righteousness looks down from the sky' — the cosmic dimensions of the restoration: emet (faithfulness) emerges from the earth (mitaretz titzmach, springs up like a plant) while tzedek (righteousness) looks down from the sky (min-shamayim nishqaph). Heaven and earth participate in the restoration: the created order aligns with the covenant attributes. This mirrors the creation-restoration language of Isa 45:8 ('let the skies rain down righteousness; let the earth open and salvation and righteousness spring up together').
