113.7: 'He raises the poor from the dust and lifts the needy from the ash heap' — the pivot to particular action. The transcendent God of vv4-6 acts specifically for the lowest human beings. 'Raises the poor from the dust' (meqimi meaphar dal) and 'lifts the needy from the ash heap' (meashpot yarim evyon) — this verse is borrowed almost verbatim from Hannah's Song (1 Sam 2:8: 'He raises up the poor from the dust; he lifts the needy from the ash heap'). Hannah's prayer at the birth of Samuel, and the Psalm's echo of it, create a trajectory: the barren woman given a son becomes the type for all divine reversals.
113.8: '...to make them sit with princes, with the princes of his people' — 'to make them sit with princes' (lehoshivi im nedivim) — the social reversal: the ash-heap poor seated with the nobles. 'With the princes of his people' (im nedivei amo) — not foreign nobility but Israel's own leadership: the covenant community's highest seats are given to those the world would place lowest. Mary's Magnificat (Luke 1:52: 'he has brought down the mighty from their thrones and exalted those of humble estate') draws on this text through Hannah's song.
113.9: 'He gives the barren woman a home, making her the joyful mother of children. Praise the LORD!' — the third reversal: barrenness to motherhood. The barren woman (aqeret habbayit, the childless woman of the house) is given a home and made a 'joyful mother of children' (em habanim semecha). The barren-to-fruitful theme runs through the matriarchal narratives: Sarah (Gen 21), Rebekah (Gen 25:21), Rachel (Gen 30:22), and Hannah (1 Sam 1-2). The Hallel psalm closes with the same reversal that Hannah's prayer celebrated — the God who grants children to the barren is the God who raised up the poor from the dust. The 'Hallelujah!' closes the psalm as it opened it.

114.1: Ps 114: The Exodus as Cosmic Event — Creation Responding to YHWH's Presence. Ps 114 is one of the most artistically accomplished poems in the Psalter. It recounts the Exodus and the wilderness journey through vivid personification of creation: the sea flees, the Jordan reverses, mountains skip. The key theological move is in v7: 'Tremble, O earth, at the presence of the LORD' — creation's response to divine presence is not just metaphorical but theologically essential: the created order is designed to respond to its creator. 'When Israel went out from Egypt, the house of Jacob from a people of strange language' — the Exodus as the fundamental national event: Israel 'going out' from Egypt (the Passover theme) and from a 'people of strange language' (am lo'ez, a foreign-speaking people). The alien language of Egypt marks the foreignness of the bondage: Israel was not at home there.
114.2: 'Judah became his sanctuary, Israel his dominion' — 'Judah became his sanctuary' (hayetah Yehudah leqodsho) — the establishment of the Davidic-Solomonic temple in Jerusalem as the divine sanctuary. 'Israel his dominion' (Yisrael mamshelotav) — the covenant nation as YHWH's realm of governance. The Exodus produces not merely freedom but a new political-religious reality: Israel as the territory where YHWH's holiness and rule are distinctively present.
114.3: 'The sea looked and fled; Jordan turned back' — the sea's flight: the Red Sea at the Exodus (Exod 14:21-22). The Jordan's reversal: the crossing into Canaan under Joshua (Josh 3:14-17). The two water-crossings that bracket the wilderness period are telescoped into a single statement. 'Looked and fled' (raah vayanos) — the sea is personified as a sentient being who sees and flees in terror at YHWH's approach. The Jordan 'turned back' (yissov leachor) — reversal of natural flow.
114.4: 'The mountains skipped like rams, the hills like lambs' — the mountains of Sinai (the theophany of Exod 19): the shaking of the mountain at YHWH's descent is now described as playful skipping (raqad, to leap, skip, dance). 'Like rams' (ke'eilim) and 'like lambs' (kivnei-tzon) — the animal comparisons give the geological event a pastoral, almost whimsical quality. The terror of Exod 19 is reframed through the Hallel's celebratory register: even Sinai's earthquake was an expression of creation's delight at divine presence.
114.5: 'What ails you, O sea, that you flee? O Jordan, that you turn back?' — the psalmist addresses the elements directly, mocking them with the question 'what ails you?' (mah lecha hayam ki tanus). The rhetorical question assumes the answer is known: the elements flee because of YHWH's presence. The playful tone of the Hallel psalm makes the cosmic reversal approachable: creation's obedience to its maker is presented as almost amusing in its completeness.
114.6: 'O mountains, that you skip like rams? O hills, like lambs?' — the same rhetorical question extended to the mountains and hills. The repetition creates a poetic symmetry with v4 while reinforcing the point: all the elements of creation — sea, river, mountains, hills — are addressed and found to have the same answer. The answer is in v7.
114.7: 'Tremble, O earth, at the presence of the LORD, at the presence of the God of Jacob' — the answer to the rhetorical questions: all creation trembles at 'the presence of the LORD' (miliphne adon) and 'the presence of the God of Jacob' (miliphne Eloah Yaakov). The 'presence' (panim) of YHWH is the cause of all the natural reversals described. The God of Jacob — the covenant-patriarch who himself experienced divine reversals (from exile to restoration) — is the one whose presence reorganizes the created order.
114.8: '...who turns the rock into a pool of water, the flint into a spring of water' — the water-from-rock miracle at Meribah (Exod 17:1-7; Num 20:1-13): the hardest material transformed into the most life-giving. The 'flint' (challamish, the extremely hard flint-rock of the Sinai) becomes a 'spring of water' (lema'ano mayim). This final image recapitulates the whole Hallel psalm's logic: the God who transforms the sea, the Jordan, the mountains, and the hills also transforms the hardest rock into flowing water. Nothing in creation can resist his transforming presence.

115.1: Ps 115: Anti-Idol Polemic and the God Who Acts — 'Not to Us, O LORD.' Ps 115 opens with the most radical self-effacing statement in the Psalter: 'Not to us, O LORD, not to us, but to your name give glory.' The glory of YHWH is the psalm's organizing concern, and the contrast between YHWH (who acts, vv3,12-16) and idols (who cannot act, vv4-8) is the rhetorical engine. 'Not to us, O LORD, not to us, but to your name give glory, for the sake of your steadfast love and your faithfulness!' (lo lanu YHWH lo lanu ki leshimcha ten kavod al chasdecha al amitecha) — the double 'not to us' is emphatic: the people disclaim any credit for YHWH's acts. The ground for divine action is YHWH's own chesed and emet — not Israel's merit.
115.2: 'Why should the nations say, Where is their God?' — the nations' taunt: ayeh na Eloheihem (where now is their God?). This is the theodicy question posed by Israel's enemies (also Pss 42:3; 79:10): if YHWH is Israel's God, why is Israel in distress? The question assumes that a god's worth is measured by his people's visible prosperity and power. The psalm responds by inverting the terms: the question reveals not YHWH's absence but the questioners' false theological assumptions.
115.3: 'Our God is in the heavens; he does all that he pleases' — 'in the heavens' (bashamayim) — the answer to 'where is their God?': in the heavens, above all earthly measurement. 'Does all that he pleases' (kol asher chaphetz asah) — unrestricted divine sovereignty. The contrast with the idols (vv4-8) is immediate: YHWH does whatever he wants; the idols can do nothing at all. The nations' taunt is answered by the assertion of radical divine freedom.
115.4: 'Their idols are silver and gold, the work of human hands' — 'the work of human hands' (ma'aseh yedei adam) — the idol-polemic's core argument: idols are human artifacts, not divine beings. The same argument appears in Isa 40:18-20; 44:9-20; Jer 10:1-16. The silver and gold of the idols are the most valuable human materials — yet they are inert matter shaped by human craftsmen. The very costliness of the materials exposes the absurdity: the most precious human resources employed to create a powerless object.
115.5: 'They have mouths, but do not speak; eyes, but do not see' — the sensory catalogue of idol-incapacity: mouths without speech, eyes without sight. The idol has the form of the human senses — the senses that covenant relationship with YHWH requires (hearing his word, seeing his works) — but none of the function. The idol's face is a parody of the 'face of YHWH' (panim) that worshipers seek.
115.6: 'They have ears, but do not hear; noses, but do not smell' — ears without hearing, noses without smelling: the catalogue continues. 'Hearing' (shama) in covenant theology is the fundamental act of covenant responsiveness — the Shema ('hear, O Israel') frames everything. An idol that cannot hear cannot receive a prayer. A nose that cannot smell means the sacrificial offerings are not received: the covenant communication is one-directional and therefore no communication at all.
115.7: 'They have hands, but do not feel; feet, but do not walk; and they do not make a sound in their throat' — hands without touch, feet without movement, throats without sound: the final sensory failures. The idol cannot act (hands), cannot come (feet), cannot respond (throat). The climactic list reveals the idol as the anti-YHWH: where YHWH hears, speaks, walks with his people (covenant walk-language: Lev 26:12), the idol does none of these.
115.8: 'Those who make them become like them; so do all who trust in them' — the most devastating point of the idol polemic: worshipers become like their gods. 'Those who make them become like them' (kemothem yihyu oseihem) — the ontological contagion of idolatry. The idol-maker shapes the idol in his own image, but in worshiping it, he shapes himself in the idol's image: deaf, blind, mute, unable to walk. This is the anthropological corollary of the theological point: worship forms the worshiper. To trust in the incapacitated idols is to share their incapacity.
115.9: 'O Israel, trust in the LORD! He is their help and their shield' — the first of three covenant-community addresses (Israel, v9; house of Aaron, v10; those who fear YHWH, v11). 'Trust in the LORD' (batach bYHWH) — the alternative to idol-trust. 'He is their help and their shield' (ezram umaginam hu) — the two covenant images of divine protection: 'help' (ezer, the same word as Gen 2:18, the 'helper' Adam needed; also the divine title in Ps 121:2) and 'shield' (magen, the divine protection of Gen 15:1).
115.10: 'O house of Aaron, trust in the LORD! He is their help and their shield' — the priestly guild addressed separately. The 'house of Aaron' (beit Aaron) — the Aaronic priests who serve in the temple. Their trust in YHWH rather than in the temple's ritual apparatus or the political power of their position is the calling. The refrain 'He is their help and their shield' is identical in all three addresses (vv9-11), creating a liturgical unity across the threefold community structure.
115.11: 'You who fear the LORD, trust in the LORD! He is their help and their shield' — the third address: 'those who fear the LORD' (yir'ei YHWH). This group may include the Gentile God-fearers who attached themselves to Israel's worship — the proselytes and sympathizers present at the pilgrimage festivals. Their inclusion alongside Israel and the priests creates a universal community of YHWH-fearers within the Hallel's context. The threefold invitation to trust mirrors the threefold blessing of vv12-13.
115.12: 'The LORD has remembered us; he will bless us; he will bless the house of Israel; he will bless the house of Aaron' — 'the LORD has remembered us' (YHWH zecharanu yevarech) — the covenant memory that activates covenant blessing. The threefold blessing corresponds to the threefold address (Israel, Aaron, those who fear YHWH). 'Has remembered us' is the past tense of covenant assurance: YHWH's memory of his covenant people is the ground for the future blessing.
115.13: '...he will bless those who fear the LORD, both the small and the great' — 'both the small and the great' (haqqetannim im hagedolim) — the blessing is not tiered by social status. The smallest (qatan) and the greatest (gadol) receive the same blessing from the same God. The social leveling of the covenant community: no covenant distinction between ranks of worshiper.
115.14: 'May the LORD give you increase, you and your children!' — the blessing of multiplication: yoseph YHWH aleichem (may the LORD increase you). The covenant multiplication blessing (Deut 28:4: 'blessed shall be the fruit of your womb') expressed as a Hallel prayer. The inclusion of 'your children' (veal beneichem) extends the blessing intergenerationally.
115.15: 'May you be blessed by the LORD, who made heaven and earth!' — 'who made heaven and earth' (oseh shamayim vaaretz) — the creator-title as the ground of the blessing. The one who made heaven and earth (all created space) is the one blessing the covenant community. The creator's blessing is guaranteed by his own creative power: he has resources to bless from his own creation.
115.16: 'The heavens are the LORD's heavens, but the earth he has given to the children of man' — 'the heavens are the LORD's' (hashamayim shamayim laYHWH) — YHWH's domain. 'The earth he has given to the children of man' (vehaaretz natan livnei adam) — the earth as humanity's domain by divine grant. This is the creation-mandate theology of Gen 1:28: YHWH creates, then delegates earth-governance to humans (adam). The distinction between divine and human spheres does not mean divine absence from earth but explains why earth-governance is humanity's responsibility.
115.17: 'The dead do not praise the LORD, nor do any who go down into silence' — 'the dead do not praise the LORD' (lo hameitim yehalelu Yah) — one of the OT's clearest statements about death and praise. Those who have descended to Sheol (the realm of the dead, 'silence' dumah) cannot participate in the praise of the living community. This is the OT anthropology of the underworld: Sheol is a place of shadows and silence, not of covenant relationship (Pss 6:5; 30:9; 88:10-12; Isa 38:18). The statement creates urgency for the living community: praise now, before death silences the voice.
115.18: 'But we will bless the LORD from this time forth and forevermore. Praise the LORD!' — the psalm's conclusion turns the urgency of v17 into a vow: 'we will bless the LORD' (vaanachnu nevarech Yah) — the living community taking responsibility for the praise that the dead cannot offer. 'From this time forth and forevermore' (me'atah vead olam) — the same temporal formula as Ps 113:2, creating a bracket within the Egyptian Hallel. The final 'Hallelujah!' closes the anti-idol psalm with the affirmation that YHWH alone is worthy of the praise that idols can neither give nor receive.

116.1: Ps 116: Individual Thanksgiving After Deliverance from Death — Setting for the Cup of Salvation. Ps 116 is an individual thanksgiving psalm with an unusually personal and intimate register. The psalmist addresses YHWH directly throughout and narrates his near-death experience and rescue in the first person. The opening is striking: 'I love the LORD' (ahavti ki yishma YHWH et qoli, I love because YHWH heard my voice). The ahav (love) is the relational term of covenant: the same verb used for YHWH's love for Israel (Deut 7:8) now comes from the human side. The ground of love is experiential: YHWH heard my voice — the particular, personal act of hearing.
116.2: 'Because he inclined his ear to me, therefore I will call on him as long as I live' — 'inclined his ear' (hittah ozno li) — the image of YHWH bending down to listen: the sovereign stooping to hear the petitioner. This posture of divine attention became the ground of the psalmist's lifelong prayer habit: 'therefore I will call on him as long as I live' (uveyamai eqra). The personal experience of answered prayer creates the pattern for all future prayer.
116.3: 'The snares of death encompassed me; the pangs of Sheol laid hold on me; I suffered distress and anguish' — 'snares of death' (apaphuni chevlei mavet) — the cords or nets of death surrounding the psalmist. 'Pangs of Sheol' (umetzarei she'ol metza'uni) — the anguish of Sheol's distress found him. The language of near-death experience: the psalmist was within Sheol's grasp. The 'pangs' (metzarim, straits/distress) may refer to a severe illness or life-threatening circumstance whose specifics the psalm does not disclose.
116.4: 'Then I called on the name of the LORD: O LORD, I pray, deliver my soul!' — the rescue cry: 'called on the name of the LORD' (uveshem YHWH eqra) — the divine Name as the address for prayer. 'O LORD, I pray, deliver my soul' (ana YHWH malleta naphshi) — the petition's simplicity: deliver my naphesh (soul/life). The ana (please, I pray) is an urgent petition particle, marking the seriousness of the request.
116.5: 'Gracious is the LORD, and righteous; our God is merciful' — the theological confession prompted by the rescue: 'gracious' (channun), 'righteous' (tzaddiq), 'merciful' (mrachem). These three divine attributes are not abstract doctrines but experientially verified through the psalmist's rescue. The OT theology of divine character is always grounded in historical experience — the attributes are known through the acts.
116.6: 'The LORD preserves the simple; when I was brought low, he saved me' — 'preserves the simple' (shomer peta'im YHWH) — the peti (simple one) — the naive, inexperienced person who lacks cunning. The divine protection of the simple/naive is unexpected: YHWH protects those who have the least ability to protect themselves, not those with the greatest skill. 'When I was brought low, he saved me' (daloti veli yehoshia) — the poverty-rescue pattern: at the nadir of weakness, YHWH acts.
116.7: 'Return, O my soul, to your rest; for the LORD has dealt bountifully with you' — 'return to your rest' (shuvi naphshi limenuchayichi) — the psalmist addresses his own soul, calling it back to its covenant resting place. The menuchah (rest) — the covenant rest that was disturbed by the near-death crisis — is restored. 'The LORD has dealt bountifully with you' (ki YHWH gamal alaichi) — gamal (to deal bountifully, to ripen, to wean): the word covers the full process of bringing something to completion and fullness. YHWH has brought the psalmist's experience to a full and good conclusion.
116.8: 'For you have delivered my soul from death, my eyes from tears, my feet from stumbling' — the three-part rescue: from death (mavet), from tears (dim'ah), from stumbling (dochi). The rescue is comprehensive: not just physical survival but the removal of grief and the restoration of stable walking. The three are a descending scale: from the ultimate danger (death) to the emotional response (tears) to the physical instability (stumbling). YHWH addressed all three levels.
116.9: 'I will walk before the LORD in the land of the living' — 'walk before the LORD' (ethalech lifnei YHWH) — the covenant walk in YHWH's presence (Gen 17:1: 'walk before me and be blameless'). 'In the land of the living' (beartzot hachayim) — the phrase contrasts with the realm of the dead (Sheol, the land of silence): the psalmist will live, not die, and his living will be a covenant walk in YHWH's presence.
116.10: 'I believed, even when I spoke: I am greatly afflicted' — Paul quotes this verse in 2 Cor 4:13 ('I believed, and so I spoke') as the paradigm of apostolic faith: belief that speaks even under suffering. The original context: the psalmist maintained faith (he'emanti, perfect: I have believed) even while confessing affliction (ani anitiy meod, I am greatly afflicted). Faith does not require silence about suffering; the Psalms model the co-existence of belief and lament.
116.11: 'I said in my alarm, All men are liars' — 'in my alarm' (vani amarti vechapzi, in my haste/panic) — the emotional state of crisis producing an overgeneralization: 'all men are liars' (kol haadam kozev). The panic-statement is neither endorsed nor condemned by the psalm — it is honestly reported as what was said in extremity. The contrast between the panic-confession (v11) and the covenant-walk resolution (v9) shows the range of human experience within authentic faith.
116.12: 'What shall I render to the LORD for all his benefits to me?' — the question of reciprocity: mah ashiv laYHWH (what shall I return to YHWH?). 'His benefits' (kol tagmulohi, all his dealings/benefits toward me) — the gamal root again (cf. v7). The psalmist's question is not rhetorical despair but genuine reflection: given everything YHWH has done, what appropriate response is there? The answer follows in the next verses.
116.13: 'I will lift up the cup of salvation and call on the name of the LORD' — 'the cup of salvation' (kos yeshu'ot essa) — the specific vessel of the todah (thanksgiving sacrifice): the cup of wine raised in the thanksgiving ceremony in the temple courts. The 'cup' imagery has a rich trajectory: the cup of blessing in the Passover seder (the third cup was the 'cup of blessing,' cf. 1 Cor 10:16) and ultimately the Eucharist cup of the new covenant. The cup of salvation is raised and the divine name called on — public testimony accompanying the libation.
116.14: 'I will pay my vows to the LORD in the presence of all his people' — 'pay my vows' (nedarai laYHWH ashalem) — the vow made in distress (v4: 'I pray, deliver my soul') is fulfilled publicly. 'In the presence of all his people' (negdah na lekhol amo) — the public fulfillment in the temple assembly. The ANE vow-system required public fulfillment before the community: the covenant community witnessed the transaction between worshiper and YHWH.
116.15: 'Precious in the sight of the LORD is the death of his saints' — 'precious' (yaqar, costly, valued, rare) — 'the death of his saints' (hamaveta lahasidav) is precious to YHWH. The verse is the most-quoted in Christian funeral liturgy and is interpreted in two ways: (1) YHWH does not allow his saints to die lightly or needlessly — their deaths are 'costly' to him, treated with great care; (2) YHWH values the death of his faithful as something that comes at high cost and is not easily conceded. Neither interpretation makes death desirable but both affirm divine attentiveness to the deaths of covenant people.
116.16: 'O LORD, I am your servant; I am your servant, the son of your maidservant. You have loosed my bonds' — 'I am your servant' (ani avdechah) — double identification as eved (servant). 'The son of your maidservant' (ben amatchah) — born into the divine household: the son of a maidservant was a permanent, household servant (unlike a purchased slave who might be freed). 'You have loosed my bonds' (pittachta lemoseirai) — the rescue from the near-death 'bonds' of vv3-4 is now described as liberation. The servant who was bound is now freed — still a servant, but no longer in death's bonds.
116.17: 'I will offer to you the sacrifice of thanksgiving and call on the name of the LORD' — 'sacrifice of thanksgiving' (zevach todah) — the specific sacrificial category (Lev 7:12-15): a peace-offering accompanied by leavened and unleavened bread, eaten on the day of offering, requiring the whole household and invited guests. The todah (thanksgiving/confession) sacrifice was the OT ceremony of public gratitude after deliverance. The todah connection to the Eucharist is deeply rooted: Jesus's Last Supper in the Passover context used Hallel psalms that include todah language.
116.18: 'I will pay my vows to the LORD in the presence of all his people' — the refrain from v14 repeated, creating a structural bracket. The double statement of public vow-fulfillment (vv14,18) emphasizes the communal, non-private nature of covenant gratitude. Individual rescue produces communal testimony. The vow paid in public before 'all his people' is the psalm's answer to the question of v12.
116.19: '...in the courts of the house of the LORD, in your midst, O Jerusalem. Praise the LORD!' — 'the courts of the house of the LORD' (bechatzerot beit YHWH) — the specific location: the temple courts where the todah sacrifice was offered and where the vow-fulfillment would be witnessed. 'In your midst, O Jerusalem' (betocheki Yerushalaim) — the city is addressed directly; the testimony of rescue takes place in the capital of the covenant community. The 'Hallelujah' closes the psalm, sending the testimony outward from the individual thanksgiving to universal praise.

117.1: Ps 117: The Shortest Chapter in the Bible — Universal Praise, Covenantal Ground. Ps 117 is both the shortest chapter in the Bible (2 verses) and the chapter closest to the Bible's midpoint. Its brevity belies its theological density: in two verses it encompasses universal scope (all nations, all peoples), covenant ground (chesed and emet), and eternal duration ('forever'). Paul cites this psalm in Rom 15:11 as one of four OT texts establishing that Gentiles were always intended to glorify God — the nations praising YHWH is not a NT innovation but a Psalter expectation. 'Praise the LORD, all nations! Extol him, all peoples!' (hallelu et YHWH kol goyim shabechuhu kol haumim) — the imperative extends to 'all nations' (kol goyim) and 'all peoples' (kol haumim). These are universal terms; no nation is excluded from the summons to praise YHWH.
117.2: 'For great is his steadfast love toward us, and the faithfulness of the LORD endures forever. Praise the LORD!' — the grounds for universal praise: YHWH's chesed (steadfast love) 'toward us' (alenu) — the primary recipients are Israel, but the praise is universal. The paradox of Ps 117: the nations are called to praise YHWH because of what he has done for Israel. The particularity of the covenant (YHWH's chesed toward Israel) produces the universality of the praise. 'The faithfulness of the LORD endures forever' (ve-emet YHWH leolam) — the same emet (faithfulness) that appeared in Ps 111-116 closes the Egyptian Hallel's penultimate psalm with the eternal divine attribute. The 'Hallelujah' at the close opens into Ps 118.

118.1: Psalm 118 is the culminating psalm of the Egyptian Hallel (Ps 113-118), the collection sung at Passover, Pentecost, Tabernacles, and Dedication (Hanukkah). At Passover, Ps 113-114 were sung before the meal and Ps 115-118 after — making Ps 118 the 'hymn' that Matthew and Mark note was sung at the conclusion of the Last Supper (Matt 26:30; Mark 14:26). 'Give thanks to the LORD, for he is good; his steadfast love endures forever' — the identical opening as Ps 107:1 and as the refrain in Ps 136. The formula (hodu la-Yhwh ki tov, ki le'olam chasdo) is the standard liturgical call to thanksgiving used when the ark was brought to Jerusalem (1 Chr 16:34) and at the dedication of Solomon's temple (2 Chr 7:3).
118.2: 'Let Israel declare: "His steadfast love endures forever"' — the beginning of an antiphonal structure involving three groups: Israel (the whole people), the house of Aaron (the priests, v. 3), and all who fear the LORD (a broader category including proselytes and non-Israelite worshipers, v. 4). This liturgical call-and-response pattern mirrors the structure of Ps 115:9-11 and 135:19-20, suggesting a formal temple setting where different groups of worshipers respond in turn. The repetition of the ki le'olam chasdo refrain (his steadfast love endures forever) functions as the congregation's liturgical affirmation.
118.3: 'Let the house of Aaron declare: "His steadfast love endures forever"' — the priestly house of Aaron as a distinct liturgical body, separate from the laity ('Israel' in v. 2). The division mirrors the Levitical organization of worship in Chronicles (1 Chr 23-26), where Levites and Aaronic priests served distinct functions. In the Second Temple period, the Levitical choirs would sing the Hallel while the priests conducted the sacrificial rites. The priest's own declaration of YHWH's hesed grounds their mediatorial function in covenant relationship rather than ritual technique.
118.4: 'Let all who fear the LORD declare: "His steadfast love endures forever"' — 'all who fear the LORD' (yir'ei Yhwh) is broader than ethnic Israel and the Aaronic priests; it is the category that includes Gentile God-fearers who attached themselves to Israel's worship. The same phrase appears in Ps 115:11 and 135:20. In Acts, the 'God-fearers' (phoboumenoi ton theon) are Gentiles who participated in synagogue worship without full conversion — Cornelius (Acts 10:2), the Antioch audience (Acts 13:16, 26). The psalm's opening invites this broadest possible congregation to affirm YHWH's eternal hesed.
118.5: 'From the depths of my distress I called to the LORD; the LORD answered me and gave me room to breathe' — the spatial theology of distress and rescue: min-hammetsar (from the narrow place) is contrasted with the LORD's response in merchav (a wide/spacious place). Distress = narrowness, constriction; salvation = wide open space. The root tsar/metsar is also the root of mitsrayim (Egypt), the paradigmatic 'narrow place' from which YHWH rescued Israel. Personal deliverance is narrated using Exodus spatial vocabulary: YHWH pulls the psalmist from the narrow place of oppression into the wide place of liberation.
118.6: 'The LORD is on my side; I will not be afraid. What can any person do to me?' — the rhetorical question that expects the answer 'nothing.' The structure echoes Ps 27:1 ('The LORD is my light and my salvation — whom shall I fear?'). This verse is cited in Heb 13:6: 'So we say with confidence: "The Lord is my helper; I will not be afraid. What can mere mortals do to me?"' — applied to Christian confidence in the face of economic threat (the context is freeing believers from love of money and fear of losing it). The theological logic is identical: the LORD's presence makes human hostility ultimately impotent.
118.7: 'The LORD stands on my side as my helper; I will see my enemies brought to shame' — 'I will see my enemies brought to shame' (ani er'eh beson'ay, I will look upon those who hate me) — the vindication theology of the psalms. In the ancient honor-shame world, seeing one's enemies put to shame was the mirror image of one's own honor being restored. The enemies who attacked the psalmist and sought to dishonor him will themselves be dishonored, while the psalmist lives to witness it (v. 17: 'I will not die — I will live'). The reversal of shame and honor is YHWH's work, not the psalmist's own vengeance.
118.8: 'It is better to take shelter in the LORD than to put your confidence in any person' — the first of two comparative trust sayings (tov...min, it is better than). The comparison is not that human help is evil but that YHWH's shelter is categorically superior. The verb chasah (take shelter/take refuge) is the covenant-loyalty term for fleeing to YHWH as protector — the same verb as Ps 2:12 ('Blessed are all who take refuge in him'), 5:11, 7:1. The trust comparison also appears in Ps 146:3-5: 'Do not put your trust in princes ... blessed is the one whose help is the God of Jacob.'
118.9: 'It is better to take shelter in the LORD than to put your confidence in rulers' — the second trust comparison specifically targets political leadership: nadivim (nobles, rulers, princes). The distrust of noble patronage was a significant counter-cultural claim in the ancient Near Eastern patron-client world, where powerful patrons were the primary source of protection, justice, and advancement. The psalms consistently relocate the ultimate source of security from the human patron network to YHWH. Ps 146:3: 'Do not put your trust in princes, in human beings, who cannot save.' YHWH as the superior patron who does not die (Ps 146:4) and whose purposes endure forever (Ps 146:6).
118.10: 'All the nations closed in around me, but in the name of the LORD I routed them' — the first of three parallel verses (vv. 10-12) each structured as: (threat) + 'but in the name of the LORD I cut them off.' The triple structure is liturgical intensification: increasing the sense of overwhelming threat (all nations → surrounded → swarmed like bees) against which the single response is constant and sufficient: the name of YHWH. 'All the nations' (kol-goyim) is the universal scope of the threat, whether literal (a coalition of enemies) or hyperbolic (for emphasis). The 'name of the LORD' (beshem Yhwh) as the instrument of victory: the name represents the divine person and authority.
118.11: 'They surrounded me — closed in from every side — but in the name of the LORD I cut them off' — the encirclement imagery (sabuni gam sabuni, they surrounded me, yes they surrounded me) is doubled for emphasis — the completeness of the encirclement is total. The image is military: the enemy has closed off all escape routes. Against this complete human hopelessness, the single response remains unchanged: 'in the name of the LORD I cut them off' (ki beshem Yhwh ki amilan, for in the name of the LORD I circumcised/cut them down). The verb mil (to circumcise, to cut down) may carry a double meaning: to cut them off as in military defeat, with the covenant sign of circumcision implied.
118.12: 'They swarmed around me like bees, but blazed out as quickly as a brush fire; in the name of the LORD I cut them off' — the bee simile (kidvorah daaru) appears in Deut 1:44 (the Amorites 'chased you like a swarm of bees'), making this a Deuteronomic military image. Bees represent the sudden, multiple, painful attack of a swarming enemy. The antithesis is the brush-fire metaphor: the enemy's threat is intense but brief — it burns like thorn-fire (es qotsim, fire of thorns), which blazes suddenly but consumes quickly (Eccl 7:6: 'like the crackling of thorns under the pot, so is the laughter of fools'). Their ferocity is real but transient; YHWH's name remains.
118.13: 'I was shoved to the point of falling, but the LORD was there to help me' — the passive 'I was shoved' (dachoh dechitani, pushed-push you pushed me) acknowledges the overwhelming force of the attack — the psalmist was nearly knocked down. But YHWH was the decisive help (azarani, he helped me). The passive verb leaves the attacker implicit (the nations of vv. 10-12 are the ones shoving). The near-fall and divine rescue is the experiential pattern that grounds the trust sayings of vv. 8-9: having been nearly defeated and rescued, the psalmist knows empirically that YHWH's help exceeds human alliance.
118.14: 'The LORD is my strength and my song; he has become my salvation' — this is a verbatim citation of Exod 15:2, the Song of the Sea sung by Moses and Israel after crossing the Red Sea: 'The LORD is my strength and my song; he has become my salvation.' By using this specific language, the psalmist explicitly identifies his personal military deliverance (vv. 10-13) with the paradigmatic Exodus. Personal rescue = Exodus replay. The Song of the Sea is the founding narrative of YHWH's identity as warrior-redeemer; the psalmist claims that identity for his own experience. Isa 12:2 also cites this verse, applying it to the eschatological new exodus.
118.15: 'Shouts of joy and victory echo in the tents of the righteous: "The right hand of the LORD does mighty things!"' — the 'tents of the righteous' (aholei tsadiqim) is a military idiom: the camp of YHWH's warriors (cf. 1 Sam 4:10; 13:2; 17:53 where 'tent' = military bivouac). The shouts are those of victorious soldiers returning to camp after battle. The specific acclamation — 'The right hand of the LORD does mighty things' (yamin Yhwh osah chayil) — echoes Exod 15:6: 'Your right hand, LORD, was majestic in power; your right hand, LORD, shattered the enemy.' The right hand (yamin) is the instrument of divine power in battle; vv. 15-16 repeat the acclamation three times.
118.16: 'The right hand of the LORD is raised high; the right hand of the LORD does mighty things!' — the raised right hand (yamin Yhwh romemah) is the gesture of victory: a warrior raising his weapon arm after defeating the enemy. In ANE iconography, the triumphant deity or king raises his right hand holding a weapon over a defeated foe. This is YHWH's posture of triumph: his right hand raised, having done mighty things (asah chayil, done valor/strength). The three-fold acclamation in vv. 15-16 creates a liturgical crescendo — building the celebratory energy of the victory processional before the turn toward the temple gates (v. 19).
118.17: 'I will not die — I will live, and I will tell of what the LORD has done' — the radical declaration of survival that grounds the psalm's entire testimony. The chiastic structure: not-die / live / tell — the purpose of continued life is proclamation. In Israelite theology, the dead do not praise YHWH (Ps 6:5: 'No one remembers you when he is dead; who praises you from the grave?'; Ps 88:10-12; Isa 38:18-19). Continued life is the precondition of praise and witness. Hezekiah's prayer in Isa 38:18-20 parallels this theology: 'The grave cannot praise you ... the living, the living — they praise you, as I am doing today.' The purpose of survival is testimony.
118.18: 'The LORD has disciplined me hard, but he has not let death have me' — the yissar/musar (discipline) vocabulary is the covenantal discipline of a father to a son (Deut 8:5: 'Know then in your heart that as a man disciplines his son, so the LORD your God disciplines you'; Prov 3:11-12: 'Do not despise the LORD's discipline ... because the LORD disciplines those he loves, as a father the son he delights in'). Hebrews 12:5-11 cites Prov 3:11-12 to reframe suffering as divine paternal discipline. The suffering narrated in the psalm is not divine abandonment but covenant discipline — intense (yasser yisserani, he disciplined me severely) yet bounded: 'he has not let death have me.'
118.19: 'Open the righteous gates for me so I can go in and give thanks to the LORD' — the entrance liturgy begins. The 'righteous gates' (sha'arei tsedeq) are the temple gates — likely the Eastern Gate through which worshipers entered. The request 'open to me the gates' marks the transition from the outer court to the inner sanctuary. The closest parallel is Ps 24:7-10: 'Lift up your heads, O gates! ... Who is this King of Glory? The LORD, strong and mighty...' — the other great entrance liturgy of the Psalter. The purpose stated: 'so I can go in and give thanks to the LORD' — the todah (thanksgiving) offering was the specific sacrifice associated with this entrance after deliverance from mortal danger.
118.20: 'This is the LORD's own gate; only the righteous may enter through it' — the gatekeeper's response to v. 19's request: the gate is identified as YHWH's exclusive property (zeh-hashsha'ar la-Yhwh, this is the gate belonging to YHWH) and the condition for entry is stated: only the righteous (tsadiqim) may enter. This reflects the 'entrance Torah' tradition: lists of moral conditions for entering YHWH's temple (Ps 15: 'LORD, who may dwell in your sacred tent? ... The one whose walk is blameless'; Ps 24:3-4: 'Who may ascend the mountain of the LORD? ... The one who has clean hands and a pure heart'). The gate-condition theology presupposes that temple access is morally conditioned, not automatic.
118.21: 'I praise you because you answered me and have become my salvation' — the thanksgiving declaration upon entering the gate. The todah formula: 'you answered me' (anitani) connects to v. 5 where the psalmist 'called' (qarati) and YHWH 'answered' (anani). The psalm's narrative arc: distress → call → answer → entrance → thanksgiving — is the classic todah pattern. In Jonah 2, from the fish's belly, Jonah vows the todah: 'What I have vowed I will make good; salvation comes from the LORD' (Jon 2:9). The todah prayer and sacrifice were the formal celebration of survival after mortal danger.
118.22: 'The stone that the builders threw aside has become the cornerstone' — the most cited verse of Psalm 118 in the NT, and one of the most significant OT quotations in early Christian theology. Even (stone) rejected by bonim (builders) has become rosh pinnah (head of the corner, cornerstone or capstone). In its original context, the rejected stone likely refers to Israel despised by the nations (vv. 10-12) or the Davidic king dismissed by human powers, now made central by YHWH's reversal. The wordplay between even (stone) and ben (son) in Hebrew may be intentional. NT citations: Matt 21:42 (Jesus applies it to himself after the parable of the tenants); Mark 12:10-11; Luke 20:17; Acts 4:11 (Peter: 'Jesus is the stone you builders rejected'); 1 Pet 2:7; Eph 2:20.
118.23: 'This is what the LORD has done; it is wonderful in our sight' — the exclamatory response to the cornerstone reversal (v. 22). 'Wonderful' (niflayt, from pala) is the vocabulary of divine signs and wonders — the term for YHWH's acts that exceed human expectation and capacity (Exod 3:20: 'I will stretch out my hand and strike Egypt with all the wonders that I will perform'; Ps 77:11: 'I will remember the deeds of the LORD; yes, I will remember your miracles of long ago'). The reversal of the rejected stone becoming the cornerstone is classified as a miraculous divine act, not merely a fortunate turn of events. In Matt 21:42-43, Jesus uses this verse immediately after quoting v. 22.
118.24: 'This is the day the LORD has made; let us celebrate and be glad in it' — 'the day' (hayyom hazeh) the LORD has made (asah, constructed/purposed) is the day of the reversal narrated in vv. 22-23. In its liturgical context, it is the day of salvation, the day YHWH has brought the rejected one through death's door to celebration at the temple. In Christian liturgy, this verse became central to Easter celebration and then to Sunday worship (dies Domini, the Lord's Day) — the day of resurrection as the ultimate divine reversal of death. 'Let us celebrate' (nagilah, we will exult) and 'be glad' (nismechah, we will rejoice) — two verbs of full-body joy in the divine presence.
118.25: 'LORD, save us, we pray! LORD, grant us success, we pray!' — hoshi'a-na (save now!) is the word that becomes 'Hosanna' in the NT. The Hebrew phrase ana YHWH hoshi'ah na (O LORD, save now!) + ana YHWH hatslichah na (O LORD, grant success now!) are petitions for YHWH's continued salvific action. At the Palm Sunday entry of Jesus into Jerusalem (Matt 21:9; Mark 11:9-10; John 12:13), the crowds shouted 'Hosanna!' while waving palm branches and laying garments on the road — the full Hallel processional reenacted with Jesus as its fulfillment. The word shifted from petition ('save, please!') to acclamation ('Hosanna!' as praise), which was already happening in Second Temple liturgy.
118.26: 'May God bless the one who comes in the name of the LORD! We bless you from the LORD's house' — baruch ha-ba beshem Yhwh (blessed is the one who comes in the name of the LORD) is the priestly blessing pronounced on arriving pilgrims from within the temple precincts ('we bless you from the LORD's house'). In all four Gospels, this verse is applied to Jesus at the Palm Sunday entry: Matt 21:9; Mark 11:9; Luke 19:38 ('Blessed is the king who comes in the name of the Lord!'); John 12:13. Jesus himself quotes v. 26 in his lament over Jerusalem: 'You will not see me again until you say, "Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord"' (Matt 23:39; Luke 13:35) — implying a future eschatological welcome that Jerusalem has not yet offered.
118.27: 'The LORD is God, and his light has shone on us. Bind the sacrifice with cords and bring it all the way to the horns of the altar' — 'his light has shone on us' (vaya'er lanu, he has given us light) recalls the Aaronic blessing: 'The LORD make his face shine upon you' (Num 6:25). The light of the divine presence at the temple is experienced by the worshiping community as a gift. The practical instruction follows: 'Bind the sacrifice with cords to the horns of the altar' — the todah (thanksgiving) offering. The horns (qarnot) of the altar were the projections at the four corners where sacrificial blood was applied (Exod 29:12; Lev 4:7) and where the sacrifice was tied before slaughter. The living animal was bound at the altar before the final act.
118.28: 'You are my God, and I will give you thanks; you are my God, and I will praise you' — the personal declaration completing the psalm's movement from corporate liturgy (vv. 1-4: 'let Israel declare') to individual testimony (vv. 5-18) to the temple entrance (vv. 19-27) to personal address (v. 28: 'you are my God'). The double 'you are my God' (eli atah; elimated elohay) is the covenant formula — the personal ownership of relationship. The verb odecha (I will give you thanks) is the todah root: the psalm itself is the verbal todah, the declaration of thanksgiving that was inseparable from the sacrifice. The psalm began with 'Give thanks to the LORD' and ends with 'I will give you thanks.'
118.29: 'Give thanks to the LORD, for he is good; his steadfast love endures forever' — the exact repetition of v. 1 forms the literary inclusio that closes both the psalm and the Egyptian Hallel (Ps 113-118). The closing formula places the entire psalm — the distress, the deliverance, the entrance, the cornerstone reversal, the Hosanna — within the frame of YHWH's enduring goodness and hesed. The Hallel ended with this verse is the last thing Jesus and the disciples sang before leaving for Gethsemane (Matt 26:30; Mark 14:26). The psalm that narrates death-and-survival, the cornerstone reversal, and the Hosanna acclamation was sung by Jesus knowing what was immediately ahead.

134.1: Psalm 134 is the final Song of Ascents (Pss 120-134), and it functions as the closing liturgical exchange of the pilgrimage festival. The collection of Songs of Ascents begins in the distant journey (Ps 120: "I dwell in Meshech") and ends here in the temple courts at night. "Servants of the LORD who minister in the house of the LORD by night (bĕlêlôt)" — the overnight Levitical guard rotations (1 Chr 9:27-33; 23:30). The Mishnah (Middot 1:1-2) describes the night watchmen's circuit of the temple courts. This final Song of Ascents thus closes the pilgrimage journey where it arrives: in the sanctuary itself, calling those who serve there through the night to bless YHWH even when the pilgrims have returned home.
134.2: "Lift up your hands (śĕʾû yĕdêkem) in the holy place and bless the LORD" — the gesture of uplifted hands was the standard posture for blessing and prayer in ancient Israel (Ps 28:2; 63:4; 141:2; 1 Kgs 8:22; Lam 2:19). "Holy place (qōḏeš)" — the sanctuary; the priests and Levites ministering in the temple courts lift their hands in the direction of the Most Holy Place. The call to bless YHWH reverses the typical direction of blessing: usually YHWH blesses Israel (as in the Aaronic benediction, Num 6:24-26). Here Israel — specifically the Levites on night duty — bless YHWH. The exchange of blessing is the pulse of covenant relationship.
134.3: "May the LORD bless you from Zion, he who made heaven and earth" — the divine blessing in response (v. 3 answers the human blessing of vv. 1-2). The Zion location of divine blessing connects the particular (Jerusalem temple) with the universal (maker of heaven and earth). This exact combination — "maker of heaven and earth" as the one who blesses "from Zion" — also appears in Ps 121:2 and 124:8. The cosmic creator is the local covenant God, and his local sanctuary is the point from which cosmic blessing radiates. The blessing formula closes the pilgrimage collection: those who have come to Jerusalem (Pss 120-134) receive the blessing they came for.

135.1: Psalm 135 is one of the most anthological psalms in the Psalter — it is woven together from phrases found throughout the Torah, Former Prophets, and other psalms (especially Ps 115 and 136). This intertextuality was probably intentional liturgical composition: by weaving together earlier sacred texts, the psalm creates a concentrated summary of covenant theology suitable for temple worship. The opening call "Praise the LORD; praise the name of the LORD" (hallĕlû šēm-YHWH) introduces the "name" (šēm) as the psalm's theological center — YHWH's self-disclosure and character, which the psalm will celebrate through his works in creation and history.
135.2: "You who stand in the house of the LORD, in the courts of the house of our God" — the address specifies the audience: the temple personnel serving in the courts. The "house of the LORD" and "courts of the house of our God" reflect the temple's spatial structure — outer courts accessible to all Israel, inner courts restricted to priests and Levites. The congregation standing in the outer courts and the Levites in the inner courts are all addressed. This liturgical specificity anchors the universal theology of the psalm (YHWH sovereign over all nations) in the concrete worship assembly of YHWH's particular people.
135.3: "Praise the LORD, for the LORD is good; sing praise to his name, for that is pleasant (nāʿîm)" — the double motivation for praise: YHWH's goodness (ṭôb) and the pleasantness (nāʿîm) of praising his name. Nāʿîm (pleasant, lovely, delightful) applies both to the experience of worship itself and to YHWH's character. The word also appears in Ps 133:1 ("How good and pleasant it is when God's people live together in unity") — the same root that describes covenantal community harmony now applied to covenantal praise. Worship is not merely obligatory but genuinely pleasant — an experience worth having for its own sake.
135.4: "For the LORD has chosen Jacob to be his own, Israel to be his treasured possession (sĕgullāh)" — Deut 7:6 almost verbatim: "the LORD your God has chosen you out of all the peoples on the face of the earth to be his people, his treasured possession." Sĕgullāh (treasured possession, special property) was an ANE term for the personal property of a king held separate from state treasury — a private treasure. Exod 19:5 uses it in YHWH's covenant address: "you will be my treasured possession (sĕgullāh) out of all nations." Israel is YHWH's personally held treasure — the basis of election, not a reward for merit.
135.5: "I know that the LORD is great, that our Lord is greater than all gods" — the henotheistic confession that moves toward monotheism. The formula "greater than all gods" acknowledges the existence of rival deities in the worldview of surrounding cultures while asserting YHWH's superiority over all of them. This formula appears also in Exod 18:11 (Jethro's confession), Ps 96:4-5, and Ps 97:9. The progression in the Psalter is from this henotheistic formula toward explicit monotheism (Isa 44:6-8; 45:5-6) — one God, not merely the greatest among many.
135.6: "The LORD does whatever pleases him (kōl-ʾăšer-ḥāpēṣ ʿāśāh), in the heavens and on the earth, in the seas and all their depths" — the formula of unrestricted divine sovereignty. No domain limits YHWH's action: heavens (the realm of the divine council and cosmic powers), earth (the historical realm of nations), seas (the chaos waters in ANE cosmology), and their depths (kol-tĕhômôt — the primordial abyss). This verse is almost exactly repeated in Ps 115:3 and echoed in Jonah 1:14 ("You, LORD, have done as it pleased you"). The freedom of divine action — doing "whatever pleases him" — is the foundation of the psalm's celebration of YHWH's historical acts.
135.7: "He makes clouds rise from the ends of the earth; he sends lightning with the rain and brings out the wind from his storehouses" — YHWH as controller of the weather cycle, a polemic against Baal (the storm-god) and Hadad (the rain-deity) of Canaanite religion. Jer 10:13 and 51:16 use nearly identical language ("He makes clouds rise from the ends of the earth"). The "storehouses (ʾôṣĕrôt)" of wind and rain are the divine treasury from which weather is dispensed (Job 38:22-23 — YHWH's storehouses of snow, hail, wind). By controlling the weather cycle, YHWH controls the agricultural calendar — the power Canaanite farmers attributed to Baal.
135.8: "He struck down the firstborn of Egypt, the firstborn of people and animals" — the tenth plague (Exod 12:29) cited as the premier example of YHWH's historical action. The psalm moves from creation sovereignty (vv. 6-7) to historical sovereignty (vv. 8-12), following the same pattern as Ps 136. The inclusion of "animals" alongside people specifies the totality of the plague (Exod 12:29 includes "firstborn of livestock"). In ANE theology, the firstborn of animals were also sacred — the Passover sacrifice of a firstborn lamb (Exod 12:3-5) was specifically the substitution of Israel's firstborn through the blood of a firstborn animal.
135.9: "He sent signs and wonders into your midst, Egypt, against Pharaoh and all his servants" — "signs and wonders (ʾôtôt ûmôpĕtîm)" is the technical formula for the Exodus miracles (Deut 4:34; 6:22; 7:19; 26:8). "Against Pharaoh and all his servants" — the wonders targeted the royal house specifically (as in Exod 11:5 — all the firstborn "from the firstborn of Pharaoh, who sits on the throne, to the firstborn of the female slave") because Pharaoh was the divine representative of Egyptian sovereignty, and YHWH's contest was with the Egyptian cosmic order, not merely its military power.
135.10: "He struck down many nations and killed mighty kings" — the transition from the Exodus plagues to the Transjordan and Canaanite battles. "Many nations" and "mighty kings" are summarizing language for the conquest narratives (Num 21; Josh 12 lists 31 defeated Canaanite kings). The movement in vv. 8-12 recapitulates the Pentateuch and Joshua: Egypt (v. 8-9) → Wilderness/Transjordan (vv. 10-11) → Canaan/inheritance (v. 12). The same sequence as the Exodus-to-Settlement narrative arc confirms that the land promise drove all of YHWH's historical acts.
135.11: "Sihon king of the Amorites and Og king of Bashan, and all the kingdoms of Canaan" — Sihon and Og became paradigmatic examples of divine victory (Num 21:21-35; Deut 2:26-3:11). They are cited together in at least twelve passages (Num 21; Deut 1, 2, 3, 4, 29, 31; Josh 2, 9, 12; Ps 136:19-20; Neh 9:22). Sihon ruled the fertile Transjordan plateau east of the Jordan; Og ruled Bashan (the modern Golan Heights), famous for its fortified cities and its king's enormous iron bed (Deut 3:11). Their defeat opened the land of Canaan for settlement. The pairing "Amorites and Bashan" would have been immediately recognizable to any Israelite worshiper.
135.12: "And gave their land as an inheritance (naḥălāh), an inheritance to his people Israel" — the land-as-inheritance theology (see Ps 105:11; Deut 12:9; Josh 11:23). Naḥălāh (inheritance, patrimony) emphasizes that the land is not simply conquered territory but a family estate given by divine gift through the covenant. The doubling of naḥălāh in this verse (inherited land given as inheritance) is emphatic: the whole point of the historical acts (vv. 8-11) was this — the gift of a place to belong, a homeland for the covenant people. The land as covenant gift underlies the NT theology of a new inheritance (1 Pet 1:4; Heb 9:15).
135.13: "Your name, O LORD, endures forever; your renown, O LORD, endures through all generations" — a reflection on YHWH's permanent reputation in history. The "name (šēm)" is YHWH's covenantal self-disclosure, his character as revealed in act; the "renown (zēker)" is the memorial by which he is known — the remembrance of his deeds. Exod 3:15 establishes the connection: the divine name "YHWH" is "my memorial (zikrî) for all generations." The permanence of the divine name means the permanent accessibility of covenant relationship across time — each generation encounters the same God who acted in Egypt and Canaan.
135.14: "For the LORD will vindicate (yāḏîn) his people and have compassion (yitnĕḥam) on his servants" — a covenant-legal promise. "Vindicate (dîn)" — YHWH will judge on behalf of his people, provide legal advocacy. This recalls Deut 32:36 (nearly verbatim), which is also quoted in Heb 10:30 in the context of the divine judgment that falls on those who spurn the covenant. "Have compassion" — nāḥam (comfort, relent, have compassion) is the same word as the divine "relenting" in Exod 32:14. The combination — legal vindication and compassionate relenting — describes the full character of the covenant Judge: rigorously just and deeply compassionate.
135.15: "The idols of the nations are silver and gold, made by human hands" — this verse begins an idol polemic (vv. 15-18) that is nearly verbatim from Ps 115:4-8. The reuse may be intentional: by repeating the well-known Ps 115 polemic, Ps 135 creates a liturgical echo within the temple worship context. The "silver and gold" of the idols (precious materials) contrasts with YHWH who "made heaven and earth" (v. 6) and "made clouds rise" (v. 7). The idols' value is in their material; YHWH's value is in his acts. Made by human hands (maʿăśēh yĕdê ʾādām) — the critique: any god that can be manufactured is not ultimate.
135.16: "They have mouths, but cannot speak; eyes, but cannot see" — the idol polemic continues (Ps 115:5 verbatim). The six sense faculties listed (mouth, eyes, ears, nose in v. 17, hands, feet implied) demonstrate that the idols have the form of persons without the function of persons. In ANE belief, the ritual of "opening the mouth" (pīt pî) was performed on cult statues to animate them and make them capable of eating, speaking, and receiving worship. The psalm denies that any such animation is possible — the forms without function are permanently inert. Isa 44:9-20 develops this polemic most extensively.
135.17: "They have ears, but cannot hear; nor is there any breath (rûaḥ) in their mouths" — the breath-of-life test. Rûaḥ (breath, spirit, wind) is what distinguishes the living from the dead — Gen 2:7 (YHWH breathed the breath of life into Adam) and Ezek 37:1-14 (the valley of dry bones animated by the rûaḥ). The idols have no rûaḥ — they are as lifeless as the materials from which they were made. This is the decisive test in the contrast between YHWH and idols: YHWH is the source and giver of breath/spirit; idols can neither receive nor give it. The life-test reduces all idolatry to absurdity.
135.18: "Those who make them will be like them, and so will all who trust in them" — the climax of the idol polemic (identical to Ps 115:8). The makers and worshipers of idols become like what they worship — gradually losing the capacity for true perception, speech, and breath that the idols themselves lack. This is a principle of conformity in worship: we become what we worship. Paul develops this in Rom 1:21-25 (the idolater's "thinking became futile and their foolish hearts were darkened") and 2 Cor 3:18 (those who contemplate the glory of God "are being transformed into his image"). The inverse of idol-conformity is the transformation into divine likeness through worship of the living God.
135.19: "Praise the LORD, O house of Israel; praise the LORD, O house of Aaron" — the call-and-response liturgy that closes the psalm (vv. 19-20) mirrors Ps 115:9-11 and 118:2-4 almost verbatim. The three groups — house of Israel (all the people), house of Aaron (the high priests), house of Levi (the broader Levitical order in v. 20) — represent concentric circles of covenant obligation. The house of Israel constitutes the whole covenant assembly; the house of Aaron represents the priestly mediators; the house of Levi the larger serving class. The three-part structure mirrors the spatial organization of the temple (outer court, inner court, Levitical gatekeepers).
135.20: "Praise the LORD, O house of Levi; you who fear the LORD, praise the LORD" — the fourth group, "those who fear the LORD (yirĕʾê YHWH)," may refer to proselytes, God-fearers among the Gentiles, or simply all pious Israelites who do not fall into the priestly categories. The term appears in Ps 115:11 and 118:4 in the same liturgical position. In Second Temple Judaism, "those who fear the LORD" was applied to Gentile sympathizers who attached themselves to synagogue worship without full conversion (Acts 10:2 — Cornelius is described as "a devout man who feared God"). The psalm's inclusive ending hints at a universalizing direction for covenant worship.
135.21: "Blessed be the LORD from Zion, he who dwells in Jerusalem. Praise the LORD (hallĕlû-yāh)!" — the closing doxology matches the form of Ps 134:3: YHWH blessed "from Zion." The geographic specificity — "from Zion," "in Jerusalem" — grounds the psalm's cosmic theology (YHWH sovereign over heaven, earth, and seas) in the particular sanctuary where YHWH "dwells (šōkēn)." The šōkēn vocabulary (dwelling, tabernacling) is related to the Deuteronomic theology of YHWH "causing his name to dwell (šākan)" at the chosen place (Deut 12:11). The Hallĕlû-yāh closes the psalm as an echo of its opening.

136.1: Psalm 136 is the Great Hallel — the psalm of antiphonal praise in which a leader declares each act of YHWH and the congregation responds with the refrain "for his steadfast love endures forever (kî lĕʿôlām ḥasdô)." The refrain appears 26 times, once per verse. This antiphonal structure is described in 2 Chr 7:3 (at Solomon's temple dedication) and Ezra 3:11 (at the laying of the Second Temple foundation) — the congregational response "kî lĕʿôlām ḥasdô" was the standard liturgical acclamation at major moments of covenant worship. By the Mishnaic period (m. Pesaḥim 10:6-7), Ps 136 was the Great Hallel sung at the Passover meal — making Matt 26:30 ("when they had sung a hymn") possibly a reference to this psalm after the Last Supper.
136.2: "Give thanks to the God of gods" — the superlative title in the refrain structure: YHWH is God above all other claimants to divinity. The same formula as Deut 10:17 ("the LORD your God is God of gods and Lord of lords"). The liturgical structure of the psalm means this verse was followed by the congregation's response "kî lĕʿôlām ḥasdô" — "for his steadfast love endures forever." This response grounds the theological claim (God of gods) in the relational quality that makes theology personal: ḥeseḏ, covenant loyalty. The greatest claim about God is answered by the most personal claim about his character.
136.3: "Give thanks to the Lord of lords" — the second superlative title, completing the triad with v. 1 (LORD) and v. 2 (God of gods). Deut 10:17 uses the same pair (God of gods / Lord of lords). Rev 17:14 and 19:16 apply this title to the risen Christ: "King of kings and Lord of lords." The psalm's liturgical repetition — each claim followed by the ḥeseḏ refrain — trains the congregation to associate divine greatness with covenant love rather than with power alone.
136.4: "To him who alone does great wonders" — the introduction of the creation and history sections (vv. 4-22). "Alone (lĕbaḏdô)" is the key word: no other being participates in the great wonders (niplāʾôt gĕḏōlôt). The divine works are singular — not the product of a pantheon's negotiation or a nature-cycle managed by competing deities, but the unilateral action of one God. "Great wonders" is the vocabulary of the Exodus traditions (Ps 72:18; 86:10; Job 5:9; 9:10) — establishing the theme that creation (vv. 5-9) and historical acts (vv. 10-22) are equally products of the one God's sole creative and saving power.
136.5: "Who made the heavens with wisdom (bĕtĕbûnāh)" — creation as an act of wisdom (tĕbûnāh — understanding, insight). Prov 3:19-20: "By wisdom the LORD laid the earth's foundations; by understanding he set the heavens in place; by his knowledge the watery depths were divided." Ps 104:24: "How many are your works, LORD! In wisdom you made them all." Creation is not arbitrary power but ordered intelligence — the cosmos reflects the divine wisdom that structured it. This theology becomes foundational for the Wisdom literature's claim that the same wisdom operating in creation is accessible to humans through Torah.
136.6: "Who spread out the earth above the waters (ʿal-hammayim)" — the creation cosmology: the flat earth resting on the primordial waters below (Gen 1:9-10; Ps 24:2: "he founded it on the seas and established it on the waters"). In the ANE cosmological picture, the earth was a disk floating on or resting above the cosmic underground waters (tĕhôm rabbāh — the great deep). The "spreading out (rāqaʿ)" of the earth echoes the "spreading out (rāqîaʿ)" of the heavens in Gen 1:6-8 — the same verb for the making of the visible firmament. YHWH's creative acts (vv. 5-9) mirror the first week of Gen 1.
136.7: "Who made the great lights" — the fourth day of creation (Gen 1:14-19): "God made two great lights." The psalm paraphrases the creation narrative, following its sequence. The "great lights (ʾôrîm gĕḏōlîm)" are distinguished only by their function (ruling day and night) rather than named as sun and moon — a deliberate avoidance of the Egyptian solar deity Ra and the Mesopotamian moon deity Sin, whose names the Hebrew words for sun (šemeš) and moon (yārēaḥ) might evoke in polemic context. The lights are demythologized: they are YHWH's creations, not deities.
136.8: "The sun to rule over the day" — the greater light (Gen 1:16). In Egyptian religion, the sun was Ra, the supreme deity whose daily journey across the sky was the central religious narrative. The psalm strips this mythological significance: the sun is YHWH's servant, its function is to "rule over the day" by providing light, not to be an object of worship. The antiphonal refrain that follows — "his steadfast love endures forever" — asserts that what motivates the sun's continued shining is YHWH's covenant love, not the sun deity's own nature or the demands of ritual. Creation is sustained by covenant love.
136.9: "The moon and stars to rule over the night" — the lesser light and the stars (Gen 1:16-18). In Mesopotamian religion, the moon (Sin/Nanna) and astral bodies (especially Venus/Ishtar) were major deities whose movements governed calendars, divination, and warfare decisions. Deut 4:19 explicitly warns Israel not to "look up to the sky and see the sun, the moon and the stars... and bow down to them." By placing moon and stars in the creation-by-divine-command sequence, Ps 136 strips all astral bodies of divine status — they are light-regulators for the night, serving the practical function of marking time. The same ḥeseḏ-refrain that follows the sun follows the moon: all creation alike serves under covenant love.
136.10: "Who struck down the firstborn of Egypt" — the pivot from creation (vv. 5-9) to history (vv. 10-22). The same God who made the sun and moon struck Egypt's firstborn. The continuity between cosmic creator and historical actor is the psalm's central theological claim: YHWH's ḥeseḏ operates at every scale, from cosmic structure to specific historical events. The transition is abrupt but intentional — Ps 136 is not a meditation on creation or history separately but on the single divine character (ḥeseḏ) that underlies both.
136.11: "And brought Israel out from among them" — Exod 12:51: "on that very day the LORD brought the Israelites out of Egypt." The language of Israel being "brought out (wayyôṣēʾ)" from among the Egyptians is the standard Exodus formula (Deut 5:6; 6:12; 13:5, 10; and dozens of occurrences). The Exodus is the paradigmatic act of divine ḥeseḏ in history — the event that defines YHWH as covenant-keeper, not merely as creator. Every subsequent use of "steadfast love" in the OT is colored by this defining act.
136.12: "With a mighty hand and an outstretched arm" — the standard anthropomorphic formula for the Exodus (Deut 4:34; 5:15; 7:19; 11:2; 26:8; Ps 44:3; Jer 32:21; Ezek 20:33-34). The "mighty hand (yāḏ ḥăzāqāh)" emphasizes irresistible divine power; the "outstretched arm (zĕrôaʿ nĕṭûyāh)" adds the spatial image of divine reach extending into history to rescue Israel. The formula became so standard that it functioned as a covenant-shorthand: to mention the "mighty hand and outstretched arm" was to invoke the entire Exodus tradition. The New Exodus prophets (Isa 40-55) would use the same imagery for the return from Babylon.
136.13: "Who divided the Red Sea (gāzar yam-sûp) in two" — the verb gāzar (to cut, divide, split) is unusually strong — not merely "parted" but cleaved in two, as a sword cuts. Ps 78:13 uses the same verb. The image of the sea being split into two halves emphasizes the completeness of the division — the people walked on dry ground between two walls of water (Exod 14:21-22). In Second Temple Judaism, the splitting of the sea was associated with the splitting of the Jordan (Josh 3-4) and later with Elijah's and Elisha's miracles with water — a typological series of covenant-defining sea-crossings.
136.14: "And brought Israel through the middle of it" — the crossing itself. The emphasis on "through the middle (bĕtôkô)" echoes Exod 14:22, 29 where Israel went "into the sea on dry ground, with a wall of water on their right and on their left." The geographic specificity — not around the sea, not carried over it, but through its middle — underlines the miraculous nature of the passage. In baptism theology (1 Cor 10:1-2 — "they were all baptized into Moses in the cloud and in the sea"), this passage through the waters became the pattern for covenant initiation through water.
136.15: "But swept Pharaoh and his army into the Red Sea" — Exod 14:27-28: "the water flowed back and covered the chariots and horsemen — the entire army of Pharaoh that had followed Israel into the sea. Not one of them survived." The specific mention of "Pharaoh (parʿōh) and his army" rather than merely "the Egyptians" emphasizes the defeat of the institutional power of Egyptian empire — not only soldiers but the royal military command itself was destroyed. The Song of Moses (Exod 15:4) celebrates: "Pharaoh's chariots and his army he has hurled into the sea."
136.16: "Who led his people through the wilderness" — the forty years of wilderness provision summarized in a single line. The guidance through the wilderness (hammôlîk ʿammô bammidbar) recalls the pillar of cloud and fire (Exod 13:21-22), the manna and quail (Exod 16), the water from the rock (Exod 17; Num 20), and the Sinai covenant (Exod 19-24). The brevity is liturgically appropriate — in the antiphonal structure, each act receives one line followed by the refrain. The wilderness period as a whole is a demonstration of sustained divine ḥeseḏ: forty years of daily provision in a humanly unlivable environment.
136.17: "Who struck down great kings" — the conquest narrative begins (vv. 17-22), parallel to Ps 135:10-12. "Great kings (mĕlāḵîm gĕḏōlîm)" — the same description as Deut 7:1; Josh 11:17 for the Canaanite city-states' rulers. The word "great" is relative — compared to the Egyptian empire, Canaanite city-kings were minor — but their fortified cities were formidable obstacles for a nomadic people without siege equipment. The psalm's attribution of their defeat to YHWH's direct action (rather than Israelite military tactics) follows the theological interpretation of the conquest in Deut 7:17-24: "Do not be afraid of them; remember well what the LORD your God did to Pharaoh and to all Egypt."
136.18: "And killed mighty kings" — the repetition of the conquest theme intensifies the point: the same divine ḥeseḏ that sustained Israel through the wilderness now clears the land before them. "Killed (wayyahărōg) mighty kings" — the verb is direct and unambiguous. The conquest involved real military violence, and the psalm does not euphemize it. The theological framing is that YHWH's covenant love toward Israel required the removal of competing powers from the land YHWH had promised. The ethical difficulties of this theology have been engaged throughout Jewish and Christian interpretation history — the psalm presents it without apology as a fact of covenant history.
136.19: "Sihon king of the Amorites" — the paradigmatic Transjordan defeat (Num 21:21-31; Deut 2:26-37). Sihon refused Israel passage through his territory and attacked — and was decisively defeated. His capital Heshbon (modern Hesban, Jordan) was taken. The repeated pairing of Sihon and Og in liturgical texts (Ps 135:11; Neh 9:22; Deut 3:6; Josh 2:10) made them the canonical examples of YHWH's power over kings who opposed Israel's passage. The narrative of their defeat in Num 21 and Deut 2-3 functions as a paradigm: when kings resist YHWH's plan for Israel, they are defeated.
136.20: "And Og king of Bashan" — Og's defeat (Num 21:33-35; Deut 3:1-11). Og was the last of the Rephaim (Deut 3:11) — the legendary pre-Israelite giant-people of Canaan and Transjordan (Gen 14:5; 15:20; 2 Sam 21:16-22). The tradition of Og's extraordinary size (Deut 3:11 describes his iron bed as 9 cubits long, ~4.5m) gave him a legendary quality that amplified the significance of his defeat. If even the Rephaite king of fortified Bashan could not resist YHWH, no human power could. The two kings together (Sihon the Amorite + Og the Rephaite) represented different ancient threats — political and primordial — both overcome by the same ḥeseḏ.
136.21: "And gave their land as an inheritance (naḥălāh)" — as in Ps 135:12, the conquest is framed as a gift (nātan, gave) not a military achievement. The land already belonged to YHWH (Lev 25:23: "the land is mine; you are but aliens and strangers resident with me") — he gave it to Israel as naḥălāh, his own patrimony distributed to his people. The Jubilee land laws (Lev 25) were grounded in this theology: since YHWH gave the land, human ownership is always provisional and the land must periodically revert to its original distribution.
136.22: "An inheritance to his servant Israel" — the closing of the historical section (vv. 10-22). "His servant Israel" — ʿabdô yiśrāʾēl is the corporate servant title, applied to the nation as a whole (Isa 41:8; 44:1-2; 45:4). It is also the title for the Servant of YHWH in Isaiah's Servant Songs (Isa 42:1; 49:3: "you are my servant, Israel, in whom I will display my splendor"). The psalm's attribution of the "servant" title to Israel as a whole creates the background against which the Isaianic question of how the corporate servant Israel can perform what it has failed to do becomes the question that the individual Servant must answer.
136.23: "He remembered us in our low estate (bĕšiplēnû)" — the shift to first person ("us") marks the liturgical community's appropriation of the history. The "low estate" (šiplēnû) — the condition of being brought low, humiliated, stripped of status. The same word as Hannah's "humiliation" (ʿānyî, related root) in 1 Sam 1:11. The verse may have been composed in the exilic period: Israel in Babylon was literally in its lowest historical position since the Exodus. "He remembered us" — divine remembering (zākar) is always followed by divine acting (as in Exod 2:24-25). The congregation confesses that past divine remembering grounds present hope.
136.24: "And freed us from our enemies" — the liberation from the "low estate" of v. 23. The brevity leaves the specific enemies unspecified — making the verse applicable across multiple historical situations (Egypt, Philistia, Assyria, Babylon). The Psalter's liturgical genius is often precisely this typological openness: by not naming the specific enemy, the verse remains perpetually applicable to whatever "enemies" any generation faces. The pattern — low estate → divine remembering → liberation — is the recurring structure of covenant rescue throughout Israel's history.
136.25: "Who gives food (nōtēn leḥem) to every creature" — the expansion from Israel's particular history (vv. 10-24) to universal divine provision. The present tense ("gives") marks this as ongoing rather than historical. YHWH's provision of food for "every creature (lĕkol-bāśār)" — literally all flesh — echoes Ps 104:27-28 (all creatures look to YHWH for food in due season) and Gen 1:29-30 (YHWH provides food for all living things at creation). The closing before the final doxology (v. 26) places universal creation-care alongside Israel's particular covenant history — both are expressions of the same ḥeseḏ.
136.26: "Give thanks to the God of heaven (ʾēl haššāmayim)" — the title "God of heaven" is rare in the Psalms but common in the post-exilic period (Ezra 1:2; 5:11-12; 7:12, 21; Neh 1:4-5; 2:4, 20; Dan 2:18-19, 37, 44; Jonah 1:9). The prevalence of this title in post-exilic texts suggests Ps 136 may have its final form in that period — the period when "God of heaven" was the Persian imperial designation for the God of the Jews (Cyrus's decree in Ezra 1:2 uses the same title). The closing "kî lĕʿôlām ḥasdô" — "for his steadfast love endures forever" — is the 26th and final repetition, having covered creation, Exodus, wilderness, and conquest. Everything is grounded in and returns to ḥeseḏ.

137.1: Psalm 137 is the most explicitly exilic text in the Psalter — it describes the experience of being in Babylon in historical present tense, almost certainly written during or immediately after the Babylonian captivity (587-539 BCE). "By the rivers of Babylon" — the two great rivers of Mesopotamia (Tigris and Euphrates) with their extensive canal systems made Babylon a water-rich landscape dramatically different from Judah's hills. Jewish exiles were settled in communities along these waterways (Ezek 1:1-3 — Ezekiel was by the Chebar river; Ezra 8:15 — exiles gathered by a canal). "We sat and wept (gam-bākînû)" — the weeping was ritualized mourning — perhaps at the river because ritual washing required water (cf. Ezek 1:1 — Ezekiel's visions came by the river).
137.2: "There on the poplars (ʿărābîm) we hung our harps" — the traditional translation "willows" is likely incorrect; the trees along the Euphrates and its canals were ʿărābîm (Euphrates poplars, Populus euphratica), which grow in rows along Mesopotamian waterways. Hanging the harps (kinnôrôt) on the trees was a symbolic act of musical suspension — the instruments silenced because Zion's worship could not continue in a foreign land. The harp was the primary instrument of the Levitical singers (1 Chr 25; 2 Chr 29:25). To hang up the harp was to stop being what they were: the singers of YHWH's praises.
137.3: "For there our captors asked us for songs, our tormentors demanded songs of joy (śimḥāh); they said, 'Sing us one of the songs of Zion!'" — the request for performance entertainment from captors was a form of humiliation. The "songs of Zion (šîrê ṣiyyôn)" were the temple hymns — liturgical music tied to specific places, times, and purposes in the Jerusalem cult. To perform them as entertainment for captors was to desacralize them. The same "songs of Zion" are celebrated in Ps 87:7 and Pss 46, 48, 76, 84 — pilgrim songs tied to the physical experience of Zion. The exiles' refusal to perform them as entertainment was an act of liturgical integrity.
137.4: "How can we sing the songs of the LORD while in a foreign land (ʾereṣ nēḵār)?" — the theological crisis of exile: the songs of YHWH were constitutively connected to the place of YHWH's presence. This was not merely cultural attachment but a profound theological question about whether YHWH's presence was portable. Deuteronomy had tied worship to "the place the LORD your God will choose" (Deut 12:5, 11, 14). Ezekiel would answer the question by having YHWH's kābôd depart from the temple before Babylon's destruction (Ezek 10-11) and then follow the exiles to Babylon — YHWH was present there too. But in this psalm, the theological problem is still raw: how does worship function without the sanctuary?
